lines.st: Isabelle Montague, loyal Victorian Knight of the Tower once said—
'History is like a granary.'
It stands in silence amidst the bustling estate, an unremarkable sight with a somewhat unpleasant scent of mold. Only ever thought of when stowing something away.
Containment. Silently containing things is its sole purpose.
As for people, they are like stalks of wheat growing out in the fields, braving the elements so they might sprout those precious, precious ears.
All you have experienced will become shining, golden grain, stored within the granary called history.
It's more apt to say that history is a showroom of human lives.

lines.x: Shining and golden? Yeah, I'd say that was a shining, golden life...
Vina, the sword is yours.
It is your destiny.
Father, is this...?
Vina! The hell're you doing?!
Stop swinging already! You've nearly beaten him dead!
*huff*... *huff*...
Vina! It's time!
Got it!
Vina, who do you wish to be when you leave this palace?
I choose—

lines.st: Perhaps this is how history runs its course. Those caught up in its flow won't accept things as they are, they will resist it with all their might.
But reality always comes crashing down, slamming you into the ground over and over.
You've got no choice but to get back up and figure a way to stand firm. You know the fight'll never end, and you've got to make ready for the next fist in your face.
Until you've circled round and round to find yourself back where you started, albeit by some different path—

lines.x: Wait, now that I think about it, Dagda might've said 'millstone'.
'History is like a millstone'?
I forgot what metaphor she used exactly, just that it's got to do with a mill or a windmill or something. The sort of thing that'll get people thinking of the sunset.
Ah, forget it. I never much fancied the way she talks all erudite like.

lines.st: When all's done, I'm here to record this... heart-pounding moment in history.
As a witness to this particular point, as one who personally experienced some of these events, I felt the need to share the tale with those who come after.
It's worth remembering, considering the many bitter lessons learned. It must not be forgotten, with too many painful sacrifices made.
Safe to say, the story's so twisty and turny that if you weren't there to see for yourself, you'd never guess where it ended up—

lines.x: Aha! I remember now! It was a ship!
'History is like a sailing ship adrift, and every age sees its share of experienced helmsmen who think to change its course...'
'But in the end, none will truly ever know where the sunset and the river will take them—'
Bah, sod it, I don't need to copy the feel of those historical novels I read. It's my book. Why should I write it like how Vina's governess talks?
Lemme have a think on how I'd tell the story my way...

lines.st: This here's a tale of how a hoodlum became a hero. It's all about courage, friendship, conviction, and power.
The heir to the King leaves the palace, and when she returns home, she's already fit to be called hero, standing alongside her merry band of mates.
This epic begins the summer of the year we left, in the factories of Londinium's Norport Borough.

lines.x: ...They were the happiest days of my life.
Muncaster.
Mm-hmm.
...Bristol.
Got any more?
Sheffi... Di... No, Glou—
Baird! Gimme a hint already! Vina? You really gonna watch me embarrass myself like this?
Hey, it's your bet. I can't say anything.
Baird's right, wouldn't be fair otherwise.
Don't give up now, Indra. Victoria's got at least sixty or so nomadic cities. If you can think of one more, you're a sixth of the way there.
My whole life I've never left Londinium. It's my home. Why the hell would I remember what some cities miles away are called?
You must be real fond of cleaning up the alleys back home, then.
Dammit, I shouldn't be acting like a smartarse!
Oh, Hannah, do you still recall the time we went to the video shop? After we were done duffing the Lane Brothers up?
I think I do? Didn't the film have a manor with white walls and a green lawn... Was it some kinda romance? It just went on and on.
Then do you remember what the leading lady loved eating the most at the nobles' banquets—
'Sidmouth butter and vanilla roast fowlbeast'!
Yeah! Sidmouth! That's ten!
*sigh*... Vina.
You really are the best when it comes to leading, Vina! I'll follow you my entire life, I will!
No need to get all sappy on me now.
Alright, Morgan, you lost. Here's your drink.
You know Vina helped her out, Baird... Ah, whatevs, drink's a drink.
Morgan! It's your turn to pick up the protection money from the factories on Old Farleigh Road.
Don't forget to help clean up the alleyways behind Old Hank's sandwich shop.
It's times like these that Glasgow feels spread a bit thin.
Hah, one day, I'm gonna make all of Londinium into Glasgow Gang turf, and all those fees will come straight to us! Alongside the best sandwiches in the city!
If you took all of Victoria under your wing, who'd you even protect them from? The Gaulish?
Maybe those snotty nobles? Who knows, some tosser's bound to show his face sooner or later.
Norport can't make do without us, and neither can Londinium. There'll come a day when Glasgow's the heroes of the whole city, just you wait.
Not wrong, I'd say.
Indeed. Here's to the Glasgow Gang, the most important gang in town.
To the Glasgow Gang!
Did you enjoy the play?
A Victorian duke's daughter falls in 'love' with a handsome Sarkaz youth.
In order to eliminate this formidable rival, her would-be suitors conjure all sorts of dreadful devilish plots.
Who would have thought the playwright who penned The Lady of Windsor could produce such a provocative work, albeit only after a hefty writer's fee.
What do you think, Lady Allerdale?
I can't say I'm too fond of the ending of Cemeteries of Londinium, Earl Morrill.
Under the heavy hand of persecution, the Duke's daughter and the Sarkaz lad elope, united in death as they are buried together in a public cemetery. A conclusion that's far too... formulaic.
I never expected a young lady such as yourself to have the tastes of a haranguing critic.
Is it not enough to bring tears to your eyes?
To whose eyes? Duke Cavendish's, or the Sarkaz Regent's?
Tears will only serve to blind us, my Lord, and are often an accomplice to popular works harboring ill intent.
A shame, truly. Here I was, thinking you would have some empathy for the leading lady, what with her bound to a fate beyond her control.
Ah, I am so dreadfully sorry! Pray forgive my inadvertent offense. You must know that my family was once on good terms with the Cumberlands...
I don't really mind.
It's common knowledge that Her Grace the Duke of Caster aided me at my lowest. I have only gratitude for her. I see no shame in my circumstances either.
Then since you're taking part in today's festivities, may I takes your words as Her Grace's own thoughts?
You may consider them a friendly reminder.
Londiniers should be wary of those mercenaries from Kazdel, heaven forbid some ridiculous story to sow the seeds of pity and sympathy in their unwitting minds.
Kazdel is far, far away, and they simply don't understand these Sarkaz in the slightest. Do not allow tears to blind their judgment, my Lord.
Your words would throttle a talented playwright.
Bringing the citizenry to tears would hardly be a difficult thing for a duke, I say.
Is this also Her Grace's opinion on the matter?

No matter what you might think of this playwright, I do believe 'The Lady of Windsor' is a rare work of art. I whole-heartedly invite you—and perhaps Duke Cavendish—to give it a thorough read-through.
'Fins have not the limbs to drag themselves out of the swamp. Man has not the eye to see beyond time.'
If Duke Cavendish can understand these words and take them to heart, while keeping a wary eye on this matter, he can avoid becoming the next Duke Windsor.
No offense meant, of course.
Oho, I must say I quite enjoy your incisive view on this topic, Lady Allerdale.
The eye to see beyond time... A rare thing indeed. In comparison, ambition beyond one's means is as cheap as chips, wouldn't you say?
I can't say I disagree.
Despite the many years past, there are still those who have not forgotten that Victoria once had a king.
And there are those who carry the former heir to the throne in mind, alongside that sword of royal authority.
Duke Caster's actions are hardly inscrutable. Whenever she dispenses advice to the other dukes, do not forget that there are eyes upon her in turn.
I will inform Her Grace, my Lord.
It's getting late. I fear I must be heading off.
Not staying for dinner, Lady Cumberland?
I have an appointment with my tailor to prepare some winter clothes. I'm sure you're aware that summer in Londinium lasts but a few fleeting days—
But winter? Winter never comes late.
Then I shan't keep you any longer, my Lady.
I shall send a ticket for opening night to you beforehand. If Her Grace would like to watch, Duke Cavendish would happily sponsor the troupe to tour the Caster demesne.
I pray that Her Grace will not be blinded by her own tears.
A most suffocating banquet, was it not?
And who might you be?
You may call me Clovisia. Based on that distinctive white hair of yours, you must be Lady Allerdale Cumberland.
Madam Ailshie of your estate has ordered some outfits for the upcoming season from our shop, but I'm afraid we haven't finalized the details of yours quite yet.
Sorry, I couldn't help but overhear your conversation with Earl Morris. It seems your reputation as a critic of the arts is well-earned.
You flatter me. I presume you too are concerned about the present situation in Londinium.
Hardly, hardly. It's just rather difficult to not listen in as the clientèle discuss the recent changes when you work at a tailor's.
*sigh* It's getting dark. I should head home. Once you've settled on a design, please have Madam Ailshie contact me at your convenience.
Ah, right, speaking of Madam Ailshie, she asked me to pass you the Londinium Daily.
Farewell.
Might as well see if there's anything else newsworthy today, I suppose.
A vicious brawl in Norport Borough?
Is that...?
Feckin' 'ell! That's bloody ridiculous is what it is!
It's the third time this month. Been accident after accident here in the factory ever since that scoundrel took over the union.
Watch close enough and you start to notice how whenever some brave soul puts out a complaint about the union, he ends up dead in some freak accident a few days later.
Let's go then already! Take me to 'im. I'm gonna see just how brass this bastard's balls are!
Calm down! The union handles union business, the street handles street business. That's the rule.
Giving every bastard I see a right thrashing's my rule!
I know! Of course I know... But we've got no other choice.
The union's keeping it all hush-hush inside the factory. Not even the coppers can investigate.
This new foreman... Johnny, right? Who's behind him?
I remember hearing he's got some big name backing him. A relative of Duke Davendich, apparently...
Do you mean Duke Cavendish?
Yeah! That's the one!
Is he trying to get anything out of you lot?
Hasn't been asking for money or any of the sort... but ever since he became foreman, lots of the important jobs are getting taken up by workers from outside.
I see. You head on back. We need to talk this over a bit.
What're we waiting around for, Morgan?! Are we gonna let these bastards do as they like?!
It's not that simple.
You heard the man. This isn't your average union tosser. He's got the backing of a duke. Why's he going out of his way to play tyrant in some little factory, though?
Who cares why?! Since when did we need a reason to take out buggers like him?
I know Londinium's rather restless these days. The dukes've been playing their games around the clock. Don't tell me you haven't noticed all these fresh faces on the street lately?
Time to come up with a plan, Vina. We're all ears.
Since we've taken their protection money, it's up to us to do the legwork.
Let's go have a chat with this bastard of a foreman. Doesn't matter if he's got a duke behind him.
That's more like it!
Alright, if you say so, Vina...
But just to be safe, we should scope out the factory a bit, get some info. You're with me, Baird.
Sure.
Hey! Why didn't you ask me?!
What's wrong? You've been looking rather glum lately.
It's nothing. I was just thinking... I might be enjoying myself a little too much.
What, not too happy here?
You have a duty, Siege.
I've told you before, Dagda.
I've found a life for myself now, so why—
Is this life of yours helping your neighbors seek the justice of the streets, then heading to the noble manors to lob a few bricks?
There's no escaping some things.
You promised me you wouldn't talk about this in the gym.
Then I'll stay by your side, waiting for the day you have your answer.

lines.st: Life is long, but the pivotal moments that change you? They're over in an instant.
I recall that one afternoon, before the chaos of the storm struck us, when we could still enjoy those peaceful hours.
Indra didn't speak to me the whole day on account of my hesitation. Baird had to step in and smooth things over between us, and by nightfall we were all happy as could be, buying hot dogs and beer.
But I couldn't shake off the feeling that the Glasgow Gang was about to enter the craziest chapter of this story yet.
It wasn't until those wild dreams of ours came to an end that I realized the most pressing issue...
The main character of this tale wasn't the Glasgow Gang. It was Vina. Just Vina, all along.

lines.x: What's the matter, Ailshie?
Did you not instruct us to keep a close eye on Her Royal Highness Alexandrina's whereabouts?
The chief of the Royal Police has just looked over the case file of that ghastly brawl in Norport.
It involved a video shop known as McClaren's Place and a street gang going by the name of Glasgow.
Here is the original copy of the file.
It's rather vague, merely a record of the involved parties, but I thought you ought to know.
Upon a fuzzy black-and-white photograph, a woman sucking on a lollipop stares indignantly at the camera.
'Person of Interest: Siege (self-identified)'
Miss Allerdale, if you are near-certain she is Her Royal Highness... Why not seek her out immediately?
And what then? I bring her back to Cumberland Manor? Or send her off to Caster?
Whether or not she is Alexandrina no longer matters. The dukes are watching her closely. She can escape them no longer.
It sounds to me like she would be swallowed by the city itself.
Hah. We'll be swallowed too, Ailshie.
I'm willing to bet we're not the only ones with an interest in her. This matter has long since grown out of my control.
Considering Duke Caster's orders, should you not find her and bring her to Her Grace?

I need some time to think, Ailshie.
I saw her once before. I saw...
Her Royal Highness.
But no matter how you put it, it was all such a long time ago. Things being what they are, is an unknown heir truly so important?
It's merely an inkling... From the very first time I saw her, so many years ago, I always believed she would change things.
Let's go. There's a few workers I need to pop in on. I would also like to know how Her Highness has been doing these past few years.

Who's there?!
Grk—
The coast is clear.
I've handled this side too.
This is it. The place Charlie told us about.
We heard some chatter. Lately, the union's been keeping a whole bunch of goods inside this old factory, like they were a smuggling ring.
Johnny seized control of the union not just to skim some funds, but to do this sort of business.
What's inside those containers?
Just open 'em up and we'll find out—
Hannah! Slow—
Are those...?
...Originium weapons.
They're not standard Victorian issue. No idea of their actual provenance though.
Duke Cavendish's man sneakily taking over a factory, all so he can move weapons from God-knows-where into Londinium? What's he playing at here?
The dukes quietly stockpiling weapons within the city can only mean one thing.
And now we've left our fingerprints all over the place...
What do we do now, Vina? Looks to me like it's something way bigger than we can handle.
We can't handle it ourselves, but we can tell others.
Got a light, Hannah?
Bloody hell, you can still see the factory burning from all the way out here!
This is no laughing matter, Indra!
Of course it is! We messed up some wicked duke's scheme!
We... did something really big here.
At least we can say we've done nothing wrong, yeah?
Let the coppers handle the rest of it.
They say Londinium's so big it defies borders, but at times I feel it's rather small. Wouldn't you agree?
Who would have thought I would meet you here, Alexandrina. Your Royal Highness.
Who are you?!
I'm Morrill, a humble guest at the Cavendish estate. Nobody in particular. And yet, I would say I'm one lucky chap.
You know about the factory... You know what Duke Cavendish is up to.
Though I can boast of sharing some of the Duke's burdens, I was not wholly in the know about His Grace's plans for Londinium.
However, at this moment, my only concern is you, Your Royal Highness Alexandrina.
I must say, seeing Your Highness standing before me really does call to mind the sight of His Majesty, all those years ago.
You and he are so very alike. The same childish sense of justice, the same... price you must pay for clinging to it.
What do you want?
Your little adventure with your friends this evening... Hardly matters, I say. A few boxes of lost merchandise is hardly anything compared to meeting you.
Come now. At the very least, I'll let your chums leave alive. How about that?
Why should I believe a word you're saying?
Excuse my impertinence, Your Highness, but the truth is you have no other choice.
Or should I say, you are free to try and escape, but I will be keeping a close eye on your whereabouts.
No.
Vina! The hell're you doing?!
Stop swinging already! You've nearly beaten him dead!
*huff*... *huff*...
It's fine, he's still breathing.
Vina... What's this all about?
You lot... Get out of here.
The factory, all this, it was all my doing. Nothing to do with anyone else.
Are you havin' a laugh?!
Vina... What was he saying? Why'd he keep calling you 'Your Royal Highness'?
...Because she's the heir to the throne of Victoria.
The daughter of the late King Alistair II, Alexandrina Vina Victoria.
I... I didn't mean to hide it. I just thought... It didn't really matter...

You daft, mate?
I'm not asking who you are, I'm asking why you'd try and shoulder all this by yourself!
Hannah...
Sounds like there's a long story you need to be telling us.
Sorry...
No time for that! Save it, we need to leave right now!
Where can we go, though?
Out the city.
Right now. This instant. Immediately.
I can't—

We're nearly out of time. Once the police notice the factory's up in flames, they'll contact the Defense Forces and shutter each and every port. When that happens, we wouldn't be able to fly out, even if we sprouted wings.
Don't worry about Norport. I'll hold the fort.
Wait, Baird, you're not coming with?! Hey, Morgan, say something, won't you?
I get where she's coming from. The Glasgow Gang's always going to be home to us, Hannah.
Someone's gotta welcome you lot home when you get back, right?
People are watching you. But I'm sorry, Vina. We can't protect you. Against real titans like them, the Gang's nothing more than puny little musbeasts.
You were never a Norporter, Vina. Or should I say, never only a Norporter. I knew from the day you joined us.
It's all my fault. I just wanted to protect you all, but... but now, I've dumped a heap of trouble on your laps.
Not wrong. If you're going to blame yourself, though, there's something I hope you keep in mind.
Remember that you owe Norport. Remember that one day, you'll need to come back here to repay everyone.
I swear. I swear I'll come back.
Yeah! It's our turf, after all! We'll snatch it right back someday!
But are you really set on coming with me? There's no need to tangle yourselves in all this.
I'm your trusted advisor, Vina. I'm worried for you.
If anyone comes looking to bother you, you're gonna need me to help bash their faces in!
Wherever you go, Vina, I will follow.
Never forget, Vina. No matter where you go, the Glasgow Gang will be with you.
I'll keep this place safe. We won it together, after all.
I promise you, Baird. I'll bring everyone back.
Hopefully sooner rather than later.
Here's to us. To the Glasgow Gang.
To the Glasgow Gang!
Dammit, dammit all... M-My Lady! It's the Duke of Cavendish's man!
My Lady, I-I'll contact Headquarters right away! Even if we must turn all of Londinium upside-down—
Suppress this event.
Very well, I'm contacting the hospital—What? Suppress it?
I will have my men handle this. Nothing to come will have anything to do with the Norport Police.
Do not worry. The chief of the Royal Police will not come calling on you about this or anyone else on scene, for that matter.
R-Really? How can you be so sure...?
While I cannot fix the future's course, sir, I can guide it towards a path of my liking.
This city will soon be welcoming a host of visitors. Do you not have more important things to handle?
You... Alright... Very well!
This too is part of your destiny, Vina.

lines.st: When we found out about Vina's past, I was amazed, sure, but I was more pleasantly surprised, as it were.
I could see the entire story in my mind's eye. A lost heir to the throne, a heroic fantasy rising from the streets.
Stories usually start with someone leaving, and end with a reunion. But reality begs to differ.
When we stepped out on the road away from home, none of us expected the way back could be so fraught with hardship.
I'm fond of the words Dagda spoke once we set foot on our home again, after many, many years away:
'History is like a sailing ship adrift, and every age sees its share of experienced helmsmen who think to change its course...'
'But in the end, none will truly ever know where the sunset and the river will take them.'

lines.x: Hey, who're you? Here to get some rounds in the ring?
I'd like to ask if this is where the Glasgow Gang is based? I'm looking for Siege. If I understand things right, she's your leader?
This gym's only got one leader now. She's called Baird.
Wait a sec... Lookin' at your clothes, you a noble?
Get out. You ain't welcome here.
Pardon me, then.
Wait.
You're looking for Vina? You know her?
I'm... an old friend of hers.
Vina's out on a trip. If you've got something to say, I can pass it on for you.
No, nothing in particular. It's just that as her friend, I'm concerned about her current situation.
Please let her know I'm wishing her all the best, when you can.
Sure. She's doing fine. I'll tell her on your behalf.
Allerdale turns and leaves the messy gym. She knows the woman there wasn't lying. Because she saw the photo hanging on the wall, and the smiling face upon it.
It's not time yet.
In her fit of absent-mindedness, that deep, resonant voice arises in her head once more.
'Allerdale Cumberland.'
'You will meet Vina again, someday.'
Section G12... All these linkage numbers have faded away since this place was abandoned... Is this it?
*phew* I'm praying I don't run into those damned devils again—
I think you're lost, lady.
(Hey, new guy, get her out of here. And while you're at it, ask her nicely how she found this place.)
(Remember, keep your tail clean this time. Don't make the boss mad.)
You heard him, lady. Come with me—
Let go of me!
Is this any way to treat a lady?
Mickey Robinson, hero of the Exemplars... Or rather, one of the heroes, is my husband. He entrusted me to fetch some medicine.
Th-The medicine to dull the pain from those hideous black stones. He can hardly even walk...
Hah, yet another unlucky bugger—
Shut it.
Listen, I haven't heard of any Mickey.
All you'll find in this blasted place are some bum's expired fun drugs and the dead devils' bones.
But—
Anyways, if you want Oripathy meds, go look for that newly-built Sanatorium. The new Speaker lady promised it'd be stocked.
Wait, we really have no other choice. There's simply not enough medicine there.
I know I can get it here. My husband, he...
Listen, I have a few valuables stashed away somewhere safe, all from Cumberland Manor. The late duke gave them to us. If I could just get some medicine—
Don't give a reason for the Defense Forces patrolling out there to come sniffing around here.
Just go already, lady.
Make her sign the list, then let her come by tomorrow to pick up the meds.
Yes, boss! But what if she doesn't have the money...?
We do it the old-fashioned way then.
That's weird, I don't think the boss ever stuck her nose into this sort of thing. Err, what should I...?
Hmph. Just give her the contract.
Thank you, tha—Wait, these clauses... My husband never mentioned them! If I signed this, then we...
He'll live well, at least before we come calling for our due.
Seems that hero husband of yours wasn't quite so honest with his better half, eh?
C'mon, hold that pen steady. If you can't, we can sign it for you.
The mercenary holds her writing hand tight. The trembling overtakes her. She can no longer maintain her veneer of calm.
She shuts her eyes, grits her teeth, and lets the mercenary guide her hand.
Go on, enjoy your life. Bloody war's over, after all.
Asking Millscar for help will be the best decision you and your husband have ever made, lady.
This morning, Londinium's interim health-secretary-cum-provisioner-general Madam Major was found dead in her office in New Auchterigg District.
In today's parliamentary session, Madam Speaker Alexandrina expressed her support of Madam Major's work in caring for the Infected, and lamented her untimely passing.
The final point in Madam Major's itinerary before she passed was a trip to the Dukedom of Normandy to discuss a purchase of medicine so as to relieve the shortages within Londinium's walls.
But according to some reliable sources, Madam Major died of an acute Oripathy attack.
Not only did she conceal her infection, she was also often seen arguing furiously with the Speaker, in recent times.

In this sensitive period where the number of reported Infected within the city is growing exponentially, it is clearly worth looking into the death of Madam Major, who had lost her hereditary peerage.
Even now, two months after the Sarkaz withdrawal, why are their sinister plots coming back to the fore?
As for the Exemplars, are the terms of the resettlement program still not finalized?
All eyes are on the newly-appointed Speaker, who has won the backing of a majority of the dukes.
We have managed to connect with the Speaker's close confidant, Miss Kate Morrigan, digging deeper into—
The broadcast in the Speaker's office suddenly falls silent.
Those louts are a real crock of shit, I tell you!
They were loafing about the Sanatorium cafeteria, waiting for me to sit down for lunch before they tried to wheedle some info out of me...
Then you beat the hell out of them.
I thought... That way, they wouldn't dare run their mouths on the news again...
Sorry. I didn't think about how it'd affect the way everyone sees you—
No, I would have kicked them out too, literally.
Huh? But...
It doesn't matter what you say to those newshounds when they're in the business of making up fluff. They've already decided what to put in their latest story a long time ago.
Since there's no way of shutting them up, might as well get straight to the point.
Vina strikes her two fists together, putting herself through the paces engraved in her instincts with ease.
Just like she always did back in the gym.
Hah! That's the Vina I know! I knew it, Hannah even said—
Whoa.
These clothes really do get in the way. Maybe I should find someone to make some alterations—
I happen to know a pretty good tailor.
He made clothes for the royal family when he was younger. Luckily, he made it through everything alive. Shame nobody dares hire him nowadays.
Infected as well?
Yes.
*cough* Ah yeah, any progress on that thing you mentioned in your message?
The situation is complicated.
We've captured the Sarkaz who have been hurting people. They're now cuffed in the Sineselde's parliamentary chamber.
I suspect this might have something to do with the gangs operating out of the abandoned underground factories.
Millscar.
As for whether or not the Duke of Normandy's heir is behind all this—

She and I will resume...
Negotiations.
Madam Major's death, the Londiniers' medicine supply, as well as Millscar's gang and those Sarkaz...
There're a lot of things to handle in this city, and none we can solve with our fists like we did two months ago.
You've settled into the job quick, Vina. I believe Parliament as it is now can manage these issues, so relax. I'm here to help you.
Mother taught me how to handle this sort of situation. I won't let her down.
Morgan?
I'm heading over to Parliament. See you there.
She's not quite used to this sort of life yet. None of us are.
There are some things Siege could do that this so-called Speaker can't.
How's the victim of the Sarkaz attack?
In the care of our colleagues at Rhodes Island's medical department. No life-threatening complications, for the time being.
Moreover, we've traced the source of those low-quality medicines. Most of them came from Millscar.
Many turned to them in desperation, but in the end, all they got was a pile of harassment from that gang of wandering goons.
As for whether this Millscar is in the know, or if it's the work of some overeager underlings, I'm not too sure—
Allerdale knows.
Millscar. The wildest villain running rampant in Londinium's shadows.
Allerdale. She's dead. The final furious flame of Cumberland the Ever on High.
Vina herself has already forgotten when the figures behind those two names slowly became one.
Was it when the war was nearly over?
She subconsciously paws at her clothes. By the time the Exemplars' 'heroic leader' walked out of the Palace of Westhaleg wearing this old suit of royal armor...
She had already torn down the flag of Kazdel's Military Commission at the break of dawn, and had seen a familiar figure and the Steam Knight fade away into the all-encompassing vapor.
That day, Allerdale did not go to her. Rather, she turned and disappeared into the shadows of the ruined city.
Let's go. To Parliament.

lines.st: The Sineselde, once a royal estate often rented by nobles as a banquet hall. It wasn't until everyone was dealing with the headache of where to house Parliament that Vina realized there weren't many choices left for the Londiniers.
The chaos of war had destroyed far too much of this city.

I've seen this room many times before on posters and in movies. I used to be astonished by how massive it was, big enough for every gang boss to bring all their boys and girls in to sit down and have a chat about their turfs.
But now, it disappoints me. It's filled to the gills, all cramped and suffocating.
Though Vina's never admitted it, I know her and I are of like mind—

lines.x: (Miss Morgan... Miss Morgan...)
Huh?
I didn't mean to interrupt your writing, it's just that... that...
(Barnaby's late again, I'm thinking of going to look for him. I'm worried he's gone and gotten himself soused again.)

Well, Vina's not here yet. If you're gonna look, go look, no need to ask me—
Wait!
Now that I think about it, you said the same last time, and the two of you never came back the entire session.
W-We were just locked out last time...
In all honesty, Miss Morgan, I've been carrying around the factory's design sheets for near my entire life.
If you asked me to do a quality inspection, I'd know all the ins and outs of whichever part you'd like, and there wasn't a foreman out there who'd dare say I was wrong.
But sitting here with those lords and ladies, trying to read these documents covered in gobbledygook so we can manage such a ridiculously huge city...
(Biggest thing I've looked after in my life was my workshop. God, my heart's pounding like mad.)
With us at your back, why be scared of some tosspot nobles?
An elegantly-dressed member of parliament's eyes meets Morgan's gaze, and she smiles and nods her head in greeting.
Fine weather we're having today. I hope you are all in good health.
Ah, yeah. Good health to you too.
We're fine, just fine, thanks.
(Well... Just treat it as helping Vina fill the seats.)
(I suppose they're not all tosspots, but keep in mind that we put our lives on the line to take back the right to sit here.)
(Are you seriously thinking of going back to the days before that? If so, I'll have to sort you out first before these bastards ruin this city again.)
Alright, I get it, I get it. Are Miss Indra and Miss Dagda not showing up today?
They're busy handling some public safety stuff.
(*sigh* I'd rather hold my hammer tight and go back to bed too.)
Oh, Madam Speaker, you're here—What's this?
Vina! Wait, who's that you're carrying?
Barnaby!
C-Clive...
Help him to his seat, Delphine.
He drank until he passed out in front of the Sineselde's doors. Now he won't let go of Vina.
I'm waiting for the Speaker!
I've got... got a whole lot of proposals to help with the meds shortage—
Then you should include them in your formal petition. We reformed the rules issued by Parliament all so we could bind ourselves.
Lady Delphine, I swear Barnaby here's not trying to be a simpleton on purpose. He and I have talked about his ideas loads of times!

He's just like me, sick to our stomachs the moment we stepped foot into this bloody place, like the soul's getting sucked right out of us. It's not just that we can't speak right, all the brass courage we had while chopping down devils is gone.
So I came up with this stupid idea. That maybe a bit of drink would... would...
Relax, Vina's not going to get mad over something like that.
Right, you needn't worry. Parliament nowadays is incredibly complex by nature. Everyone here's on the lookout, getting used to things and learning on the job... That includes the Speaker.
If we're going to defeat those who wish us ill off the battlefield, we're going to have to learn how they think.
It's not going to be an easy process for any of us. The two of you have done pretty well, all things considered.
What if we become them once we learn enough about them...?
I-I certainly won't, Miss Morgan!
I'm not saying you will, Clive.
I can't guarantee that won't be the case, Morgan. Nobody can.
I believe in Vina. So do Hannah and Dagda... I don't care what you all think.
I'm sure we're all very clear on why we have convened this emergency meeting.
Ever since Parliament was reestablished, we have worked around the clock to resolve the issue of the Sarkaz who continue to operate within Londinium's walls.
We gave our citizens a promise. A promise of safety.
It matters not if it is the Sarkaz, or roving mercenaries such as Millscar hiding within the city; Parliament will strive to settle the matter.
We will brook no evil that threatens the safety of our people.
...You're putting me on trial?
There will be no trial, Sarkaz. Parliament's new interim bill dictates how we handle you. The provisional court has passed it as well.
You will be dealt with in a manner that all agree upon.
Not everyone. Just you people.
You caused grave injury to a kind-hearted shopkeep, all for a loaf of bread he had already set aside for you.
He's still alive?
He is.
Good. So, when's the execution?
You really should have left with the rest of your kind when the war ended.
You still remember me? Of course you do. You were the one who let me go during the big retreat, after all.
I'd say you were my savior, head of the Exemplars.
You—
'Longshot'. That's what my daughter calls me. You didn't ask me my name at the time, and I'm betting the people sitting by your side don't give a damn what it is.
But I'm going to ask everyone here something, on behalf of a few others.
Are Sarkaz born and bred in Londinium, who have never left the city, still considered Victorians?

lines.st: To be honest, I've never really thought about that question.
The Sarkaz who called himself Longshot never yelled or shouted, but the rowdy, chattering chamber went dead quiet as he spoke.
I looked at the people all around me, paying special attention to the nobles' expressions.

Those who had long made up their minds on the issue didn't feel like speaking up. As for those who hadn't, the more nervous of them had their eyes darting about, waiting for someone else to speak up first.
But me? I was part of the third kind of people. I didn't have to make up my mind, because Vina'd do it for me.
So that day, all I had to do was wait for her answer.
Just like the time I was up on the city walls, watching the Sarkaz troops leave the city, waiting for her to give me an answer.
I don't think I'm ever going to forget that sight, so long as I live.
Back then, I also had a question for Vina: Why're we allowing those Sarkaz who killed Baird to leave with so much pomp and circumstance?
She never said anything that day.

lines.x: The Sarkaz troops are leaving.
The airship's shadow looms over the silent, orderly soldiers, the Lifebone tearing ripples through the dark clouds hanging above the ruins outside the city.
Vina stands atop the walls, quietly watching them march into the distance. Not too long ago, they were tearing apart her home.
The land is scarred and slick with blood.
How many years will it take to recover from the wounds Amnannam left on Londinium?
I don't know what you and the Doctor talked about, Vina, but there's still time to beat the shite out of all those bastards!
Our men and the boys from the ducal alliance are still waiting down at the foot of the walls. Just—
Hannah, we're the ones who most need to stay calm right now.
I'd like more than any of you to keep them here... But truth be told, we simply can't do it.
The dukes' main fleets haven't retreated too far away, Delphine.
Dagda's right! They can hem and haw all they like, but once we start the fight again, they'll have to help us out, even if it's just keeping up pretenses.
Then? Are you going to let these people who fought so hard to survive the war just throw their lives away?
Don't forget the ceasefire agreement. Now's not the time to act recklessly.
All you ever say is 'don't, don't, don't'! Screw this! Screw it all!
I can't take it any longer!
(Choking back tears) All those people... Our friends... Baird...
Indra stays not a moment longer. She fears she cannot stop herself from sliding down the walls to join the crowd below.
She fears she cannot stop herself from leading the charge, pulling her battle-worn yet battle-hungry fellows back into the fray.
Hannah...
She's been bottling herself up this whole time. Don't worry, Vina. I'll go keep an eye on her.
She's just venting. She's not the foolhardy type.
Vina. Why're we allowing those Sarkaz who killed Baird to leave with so much pomp and circumstance?
This is the best the Doctor and Wiš'adel could get us.
Londinium cannot withstand another war. There's... There's enough dead already.
You know this, Vina.
*sigh* Tell the Exemplars, no rash action from anyone—
Doctor?

opts: Rhodes Island received a few invitations from the dukes.
They want us to sit in on the post-war conference on handling Londinium.

lines.x: Handling... You mean before all the Sarkaz are even gone?
Are they really in such a rush?
I don't think the city as it is now is willing to play ball with their machinations.

opts: They realized that too.
That's why they chose you.

lines.x: Hmph...

opts: You could always refuse, Vina.

lines.x: This is my home, Doctor.
Made your decision, Vina?
Wasn't it made the moment we lead the Exemplars on our charge into the city?
They know it, and I do too.
It's not something to take lightly, but you have my full support.

opts: Then Rhodes Island will 'attend' the conference.
We'll stand by your side.

lines.x: Thank you, Doctor.
I suppose you're saying the biggest challenge is yet to come?
Hah, I'm supremely curious as to what they'll spew out of those pretty little mouths of theirs.

opts: We'll meet you as soon as we can.

lines.x: Coming home's not always easy, is it?
Shortly after the war
This was a resplendent place twenty-six years ago.
At the time, the dome above was polished to a mirror sheen, and the radiance of the twin moons and the enchanting darkness of the starry sky dancing together was a most beauteous sight.
Once, I kissed the back of His Majesty's hand here. 'Paying respects to King Alistair', a rare honor indeed.
Victoria no longer has a king, my Lady—
Or should I call you the Earl of March?
Just Eleanor is fine.
I did not beg His Grace the Duke of Normandy for the seat of County March to hold the title of Earl, but rather to expedite a few business matters, Your Highness.
If it were not for that farcical royal hanging, we might have been much closer, Vina.
She takes out a gilded purple business card, its face embossed with nothing more than a golden loosestrife flower.
Please, take it. Since you're more accustomed to do things in a businesslike manner, this might put you at ease.
I'm afraid we are not so well-acquainted. Do you all always decide the future of Londinium here, my Lady?
It was never up to me where we meet. Besides, considering all that's happened here, surely there is nowhere else more suited than this?
Eleanor looks at the faded statues and dusty doors at the end of the hallway, as if she could capture their past glory.
In the room beyond was the King of Victoria's throne. Upon it he once sat, dictating the rise and fall of the Empire.
But what remains behind those doors now?
I arrived early to both walk down memory lane and to meet with you. We have much to talk about.
Did you not wish to meet me too?
You know I'm holding a—
Vina, surely your friends from Rhodes Island have divulged what the other dukes are thinking to you.
The other dukes? I don't see much difference between you, them and the Duke of Normandy backing you.
Oh, don't get me mixed up with His Grace. He's a man worthy of respect, but it's not worth bothering him with trifles such as handling Londinium.
As for the rest of them, I dare not say I'm worthy of comparison.
The Sarkaz withdrawal does not mean that Londinium's troubles are over. None are willing to tally up the change in the Infected population, but the outcome is rather clear.
Victoria's pharmaceutical production lines are insufficient, and nobody wants to rework their precious factories for the sake of the Infected. You know what I mean.
What do you mean?
I have a long-standing partnership with the Columbian military. They've even referred me to several pharmaceutical companies.
There is no crack that cannot be plugged. For the right price, of course.
Is that a threat?
Only if you think it is. Don't be too hasty to respond. Figure out what bargaining chips you have first before you come find me, Vina.
Let's start our future partnership on the right foot.
Oh, and, you know you can tell just by looking around that your family once steered the course of all Victoria from this ancient palace.
Your Royal Highness Alexandrina, Londinium is far too small for you—
The city's far too large, if you ask me. It shames me to admit, but the wailing I heard all across Londinium had me quaking in my boots, and in my panic, I went the wrong way.
Ah, so many tragic sights in this city...
You really have grown, Ellie. I still remember how you used to clamber up my back and grab onto my antlers.
I am ever grateful for your generosity. His Grace the Duke of Normandy also reminded me that he is off soon to convalesce in greener pastures. You may join him, if you wish.
Hah, I'm not one to leave my manor before I've drained my wine cellar dry of the fine vintages within.
And here I was thinking old Normandy would understand the importance of this little meeting of ours.
But no matter. You are the reason we have gathered here today, Alexandrina.
*cough* Your Grace didn't need to cut to the chase quite so quickly.
The old soldier's running late... Did he refuse your invitation again?
He turned his eyes away from Londinium a long time ago. I'm sure your spies will have all the details of my meeting with him served to you with breakfast tomorrow.
Yet even now, his elegance is no less impressive than the time Gaul was swallowed whole.
I wouldn't consider this a good thing for Victoria.
Tara and Dublinn... Though he has not explicitly declared independence from Victoria, only the blind fail to see the reality of the situation.
Abercorn and Fife suffered tremendous losses, and so have 'forfeited the right' to take part in the big decisions. And the one behind Ashworth has decided to stay far from any sort of turmoil.
As for Normandy... His stance is rather clear-cut.
Gododdin smiles at the Young Duke. Eleanor simply performs the most minute of bows in return.
Aye, the vagaries of life.
Back in those days, we still held parliamentary banquets here, and King Alistair still sat in the throne room. None would take his words lightly.
Hah.
In the dark and dusty hall, Duke Gododdin lithely taps out a beat etched in his memories with one foot, just like in days past.

Smoke, music, and the sunlight shined upon him from the dome above. He was a spirited young man back then, with all the lords and ladies at the feast lined up to be his dance partner. Even His Majesty applauded him.
Then, together with the other dukes, he sent the King to the gallows.
No need to memorialize the dead, Your Grace. His orphan stands before us, does she not?
Hah, aye, so she does. What does it matter if Victoria has no king?
The Siege. That's all I can call you until you accept our proposal.
There are better ways to implement your ideas on the Infected, the refugees, even those pain-in-the-arse Sarkaz.
...Madam Speaker.
Should I consider this advice from the 'will of the empire'?
Call it an attempt at giving some. We have other means to handle this near-ruined city.
This is not a lifetime appointment. We will judge whether or not you are fit for the position.
Just like the past, no?
With your 'dinner table Parliament', a handful of dukes could dance around the King behind closed doors, deciding the nation's future on a whim.
And the results? Look outside. We have not yet washed ourselves clean of all the Sarkaz blood—
You look only at this little city, while I look at the whole of Victoria.
If Londinium has become an ulcer sapping Victoria's strength, I do not mind gouging it out.
I will allow other nations to see Victoria bleed, but I will not tolerate them mocking our scars, nauseating though they may be.
If you stand in a sewer, you cannot expect to have full view of an ancient empire. Do not forget that the family name 'Victoria' is no stranger to me, Your Royal Highness Alexandrina.
Only the strong and the heroic have the power to steer the empire on its future course.
Let's not frighten our future friend, Your Grace. Didn't we gather here to soothe our worries in a more pleasant manner?
Old Wellington is far from the core, Windermere is in a constant state of uproar, and the rest are keeping an eye on how we stand.
Since it's rare for us all to see eye-to-eye on a matter, let's keep things simple.
That, I cannot deny. But Vina, you can do far more than this.
Though this does not mean the Speaker must sit on the throne, Ellie.
Are you all finished?
She draws her sword, and the hall falls deathly silent.

lines.st: Vina insisted on recording some minutes for that secret meeting.
It was a meeting to decide Londinium's future, so her residents had the right to know what happened exactly.

I stood there in my hiding spot, trying to note down every word as impartially as I could, but when I saw Vina draw that sword from her hip, pointing it at those bastards who thought they knew it all...
I hoped from my deepest heart that she would strike them all down. That way, what came next might have gone differently.

lines.x: My teacher once told me the Sighs of Kings was used to behead rebellious nobles—
No, not the Sighs of Kings any longer, though I do not know what to call it now.
I have not the right to name it, as it was the workers who forged it. To commemorate that victory, whose painful sting has not yet left us.
At first, as it lay forgotten upon the muddy battlefield, I thought its duty had ended.
But those heroes who lost everything still remember it, because it symbolizes the Victoria held in their hearts. They do not wish to see her laden with dust once more.
I know what they intended when they gave it to me. They wanted me to judge in their stead... Judge those who brought them to desolation and ruin.
But you don't fear it, do you?
It was never the sword you feared.
Vina gently caresses the flourishes on both sides of the sword's crossguard, every detail sculpted by the artisans of Londinium.
A hammer and a sword. They glimmer in the darkness.
'So long as the sword gleams, Victoria's honor will shine eternal.'
They begged my aid in reforging this sword, broken to the point of no return... using my hammer as its core.
Once reforged, they offered it to me without hesitation. But at the time, I could make no promises. King? Speaker? As far as I'm concerned...
Neither title is worth a damn. I'm no different from them. I'm just a woman who wanted to go home.
And when I made it back, what did I find?
I didn't see the 'grand' Victoria you do.

But I did see Infected children in the boxing gym, hoping to learn pugilism so they could defend their homes. And drunkards, mouths full of rotting teeth, donating the drinks they had stashed away to the hospitals, all so soldiers on the operating table could be saved.
The glory of empire has long since left us, so far in the distance it can no longer warm our hard beds in the winter chill of the new year.
This sword is the duty they entrusted to me. I need neither Parliament nor royal authority to know what I must do.
I will do what I can to show them the power they hold in their hands. To make them understand the land is theirs to protect!
But until that day comes, I must stand at their forefront, no matter what title I bear.
Sewers too, may bloom. And so, here, in my home...
I will NOT be lectured by the likes of you!
Vina firmly plants the sword into the ground, its point passing through the stone like butter. It stands tall before the dukes.
All eyes fall upon the quivering sword. A very clear provocation from Vina.
'Realmblade' and 'miracle'. In the wake of the war, those two words have become joined at the hip.
Rather than 'the late king's orphan', they are much more concerned about Vina's other identity.
Hah, well said, my Lady! There's really no point in us keeping the sword nowadays.
Best you pull it right out, no point in nicking such a fine blade.
Your teacher really did give you a practical education. It seems we were worried over nothing, Duke Caster.
Indeed. A rather childish way of doing things, but one that will win over the people. Parliament will see her mature faster than ever.
Wait just a bloody minute—
We have no choice, Siege. Sundered Victoria needs a new voice, and you have stepped forward.
But I have seen many die by their own blades, leading me to think long and hard about taking up that particular one.
Do not let it cut you, Madam Speaker. That is my advice.

opts: She's more clear-headed than Your Graces think.
I'd say you've done pretty well so far, Vina.

lines.x: Doctor?!
Pardon me, Your Grace. We were... unable to stop the Doctor.
(Bored growl)
You are dismissed.

opts: Londinium needs a leader who can protect everyone.
You cannot rely on anyone else.

lines.x: Doctor...

opts: The final decision is yours, naturally.
We will do everything we can to help the Infected here.

lines.x: How interesting. A pharmaceutical company specialized in Oripathy that often dips its toe into areas outside its expertise.
You circumvented the ducal alliance, and tendered a ceasefire with those Sarkaz.

opts: Well, there was no reaction from Your Graces' fleets.
You assented to it, did you not?

lines.x: Merely a gift, as thanks for all you have done on the battlefield.
There are some lines that must not be crossed.
We will await your answer, Siege.
As for Rhodes Island, you lot have been keeping quiet for a while now. Why the sudden urgency?

opts: Time itself makes for irrational decisions.

lines.x: Oh? Time, is it...?
Vina, have a good think about what the people you're still protecting really need.
And remember, if you do make your decision, don't get too close with those Rhodes Islanders.

opts: I believe we have a good relationship with Duke of Normandy, my Lady.

lines.x: That's exactly why I'm warning Vina, 'Doctor' of Rhodes Island.
She's not quite like the other dukes.
So, what should we do now, Doctor?

opts: You already have your answer, don't you, Vina?
It's not good for Rhodes to get more involved in Londinium's affairs.
Amiya's already left for Kazdel.
Most of our staff... will withdraw from Londinium ASAP.

lines.x: It's been a long time since Babel took my brothers away. As for us who stayed, it's been real rough lately.
Hmph. Just do it already. I've got nothing else to say.
He looks all around him, watching the people on their benches whisper to one another uneasily, an unconscious sneer on his face.
This place fettered him, and in its magnanimity, it had also fettered those who would decide his fate.
Not too long ago, Vina vetoed a proposal to use the Palace of Westhaleg to house the Parliament, instead choosing this building oft rented out by the royal family.
She hoped this would be the starting point of a new Parliament.
I've finished looking into him, Vina.

Longshot, a lifelong mercenary, hails from a place in Kazdel called the Scar Market. During the war, he took part in defending the city in the name of the Military Commission. He never saw the front lines.

Two months ago, Babel organized a withdrawal effort. He didn't follow them. Instead, he got captured by Indra and Dagda while covering for some local Sarkaz as they evacuated. You really did save him.
I... don't remember that.
Hah.
After he committed this latest crime of his, we followed his trail and found the people he was hiding.
His daughter has been helping a few Sarkaz leave the city these past two months. But this time she's part of the group sneaking out herself.
*Sarkaz expletive*!
You attacked the shopkeep who recognized you for supplies, didn't you?
Aaaaaghhh—
Restrain him!
Don't lose your temper just yet, Longshot. Your loved ones managed to slip through our fingers. Someone's helping them.
Millscar. Sounds familiar, right?
Their 'business' is creeping into places they shouldn't be creeping into.
...Hah. Haha.
I don't know a damned thing, Feline. Just kill me already.
Worried my blood's going to get this pretty little place all dirty?
The war's just ended, and you're already trembling at the sight of some red?!
None of you sitting here have even one-tenth of the honesty of those Sarkaz you refuse to call Victorians!
Just leave the rest to me.
Alright.
Any new instructions from Her Grace on the latest going-ons in the city?
Her Grace has long since been aware of the trouble these Sarkaz and this Millscar have been causing, and she is very concerned about the safety of the citizens.
But now Parliament is fully assembled, and since the Speaker has been doing a fine job of solving the issues plaguing the citizenry, she believes things will improve.
However...
However?
When it comes to rooting out the risks against Londinium's safety, Her Grace is of the opinion that we should not tarry, especially in regards to Millscar.
And so, there are still problems in this city that we must urge the Speaker to resolve sooner rather than later.
Boss, Parliament hanged that devil, but we managed to snag the stuff he left for us.
These goods...
Send them out.
But boss... That's way too risky right now. Parliament's been trying to sniff us out 24/7.
Then hide them for a spell. They've paid their due, so we'll get them delivered. No need to worry about anything else.
Nobody knows about this batch anyway, maybe we can just—
The mercenary gestures at his neck, but a sharp pain accompanied by a chill suddenly prickles at his chin.
B-Boss, I had to say it, even if you kill me! That's the best way to handle things!
Didn't those devils kill a whole bunch of our people?!
If you really cared, you'd be on the Exemplars' list of decommissioned soldiers, not here.
But there's nothing wrong with caution, I suppose. With the new Speaker so concerned about the city's safety, we're an inevitable target alongside the Sarkaz.
Hmph. Those devils brought it upon themselves, though.
Two months ago, they decided not to withdraw alongside the rest of their kin. Now that things are heading south for them and they finally wish to flee, it's natural they have to pay extra.
Don't worry. If this goes well, more and more business opportunities will be coming our way.
You're back. How'd it go? Did you manage to get any?
Sanatorium's as full as a tin of fins. Everyone's trying to get their suppressant as soon as they can, not to mention what just happened to Madam Major...
Sorry, Diane. Not even Exemplars get special treatment.
Rhodes Island's been working around the clock to provide as many suppressants as they can, but there's just too many new Infected—
I... I understand.
Hmm... Well, it's not like we've got no way of getting any. I've heard from some war buddies that there's private channels...
Illegal ones?
Hey, as long as you live to see another day—
There's other means of buying medicine? Huh. My boss never told me we'd have competitors.
Hmph. Sure you didn't hear me wrong, mister?
Ah, sorry, my trip from Columbia must have tired me out too much. Hello, I'm Eliseo. Here, my business card.
Colum...bia? Pure Heart Medical?

That's right, I rushed over to Victoria to find... to find...
Err, one second, let me check my list...
Ever since I arrived in Londinium, nearly everything's been different to how the preliminary briefing described it.
Found it. Londinium's interim health secretary... Madam Major. Could you tell me how to get to the Office of Provisional Supply Procurement?
Rest assured, I'm here representing Pure Heart Medical to discuss medical cooperation. The situation here will get better soon.
Did I... say something wrong?
Didn't you hear the news? Madam Major has... passed away. Just this morning.
Sorry.
Sorry, I lost my cool for a second. No matter how hard we must strive, Pure Heart Medical's sincerity and dedication will not change.
But right now, could you please take me to her office?

lines.st: ♪May the vast lands they keep owre kynge.
♪His people, and alle his well-wyllynge,
♪And give them grace wythoute endyng!
♪Then may we call and savely syng,
♪Victoria, yeve thankes for owre hom!

lines.loc: Throne Room, Palace of WesthalegOctober 8th, 1098, 9:45 P.M.

opts: You've been humming that song ever since we stepped into the throne room.
Do you like it?

lines.x: 'Like' is too strong a word. It's just left an impression on me.
My foster parents would hum it by my bedside whenever I couldn't sleep.
I thought it was a lullaby. It's only long after they've passed on that I realized whom I was to them, who was their 'kynge'.
The song became a stranger from that day forth, but the tune has remained familiar. I would never forget it.

opts: Do you still remember him? The...

lines.x: The King? I'm not familiar with him. As for the man who is my closest blood relative...
I won't lie, Doctor, I tried to piece together an image of my father in my memory.
But when I saw him again with my own eyes, there was nothing familiar about him.
He was a king, and I'm just a gangster from the streets. We have nothing in common, save for the name Victoria.
There are more important things for me to think about.
You heard the dukes talking. They're ready to abandon Londinium... my home, to whatever fate.
This wasn't what I expected when Dr. Kal'tsit got you out of Chernobog and got me to plan a return to Victoria.
But I've come too far to let it go.

opts: Responsibility is a curious thing.
I can't let go of the Rhodes Islanders, either.
We're the same.
The dukes know you can't let go of the people who need you. They're counting on it.

lines.x: And we're counting on them being obsessed with maintaining their own power.
As long as their pride still needs the name 'Victoria', the city will have time to recover.
It's a deal where the outcome is predetermined, and both sides of the table know that.
It's the people who have had my back through all this that keeps me going.
We're home. The war is over. It's time to listen to their voices.

opts: Vina...

lines.x: What is it, Doctor?

opts: Perhaps the day will come when Rhodes Island doesn't approve of the choices you make.
Perhaps the day will come when you don't approve of the choices Rhodes Island makes.

lines.x: ...I know.
Many of us find it hard to accept that you assisted Wiš'adel with the evacuation of the remaining Sarkaz.
But this war can go on no longer. I... We've lost enough people.
And we've heard Sarkaz warning, loud and clear.
The ducal fleets held their positions during the retreat because they all got the news—
Massive Catastrophe clouds forming over the capitals of most of the Core countries almost simultaneously.
Talk about straightforward. They strolled back to Kazdel in broad daylight.
You and Dr. Kal'tsit know what they could do with that piece of Originium.

opts: They won't abuse it, unless their back is to the wall.

lines.x: For self-defense only? Maybe they think that way, for now.
But there is no such thing as a guarantee when one is in possession of power far greater than one can control.
I've seen many gangs rise rapidly with the backing of the nobility, only to fall apart from the inside just as quickly.

opts: That's why Amiya is going back to Kazdel with them.
Amiya will keep the Originium under our oversight.

lines.st: But you're not in a position to determine Sarkaz's destiny either. Both sides have been inflicted wounds that will not heal so easily.
The young Sarkaz regent has gotten his wish. The Sarkaz are once again united by concrete hatred, and obtained an Originium shard that belongs to them...
Hatred always ends the same way. Where will you be when that time comes?

lines.x: A golden lion walks out from behind the darkened throne and crouches by its side.
Gawain. It is like he was always here, like the reliefs around him, bearing witness to the Empire's rise and fall, all in the blink of an eye.
And now, his dark eyes have witnessed you.
Teacher?!

opts: This is the first time you've shown yourself before me.

lines.st: No need to fear. I am neither a monster nor an illusion.
I show myself now because I must.
Vina has chosen a path that she had not even considered, until now. She must grow, and you are in a position to help her.

opts: Rhodes Island is already assisting her work in Londinium.

lines.st: That is not what I mean. I have no intention to interfere in Victoria's destiny, whatever it may be.
But you have led her towards a confrontation with the destiny of Terra itself, on which hope is hard to come by.
I promised one who is long gone to walk with her until the very last, but now, even my own being is no longer a certainty—

opts: We will try not to drag others into it.

lines.st: I know Vina. When the day comes, she will stand with Rhodes Island, and you will need her. But where will you stand when that day comes?
There is no need to answer. She will witness on my behalf.
You are neither her follower, nor mentor, nor foe.
You already realize that the ties you share are one-of-a-kind.
Perhaps we can afford to wait for a chance at a fresh start. But Vina cannot.
I have taught you how to survive, Vina. But I cannot teach you how to become hope—
The one before you, however, can.

opts: I know what I must do.

lines.st: Then guide her, the one whom Vina trusts. You are the spark that lights her fire, the spring that drives her forward.
Listen to this one, Vina, when you find yourself in doubt.

lines.x: What is this instrument, Vina?
An engineering sample made by Rockrock. Says it keeps tired workers from growing drowsy. But how's that different from just taking a nap?
Does it work?
Vina yawns, rubs her eyes, and continues to peruse the documents before her.
No.
What DOES wake you up, though, is responding to some forty petitions to eliminate the traitor, Millscar.
Have the citizens fighting over this outside calmed down?
Yes. Horn brought a bunch of city guard's newcomers straight out of training camp.
Thank goodness it didn't get out of hand.
It helped that the soldiers had families and comrades on both sides. *sigh* I really don't know what the people who still support Millscar are thinking.
No surprise. It was Parliament that went back on its word first. We did have trouble with our medicine supply, and her stuff gave people hope.
Only false hope, though.
At first, I thought you sent the Defense Forces to...
Crack down? Using violence to suppress innocent countrymen is the most cowardly and least effective method—
Why are you looking at me like that?
Just feeling dumb for worrying at all.
Don't tease me. I've got a long way to go before I'm used to this role. Thank goodness I have a lot help from people I can trust.
My teacher, the Doctor, and you... and many others.
She stretches, puts down Rockrock's sample, and lets out a long exhalation.
I would be stumbling in the dark without the intel from your informants and our Rhodes Island colleagues.
But Millscar...
Selling dangerous drugs of unknown origin, offering predatory loans, threatening the lives of the citizenry... and colluding with the Sarkaz...
All the evidence lies in my drawer, all of it corroborated. She cannot be allowed to run unchecked just because some few benefited from association with her.
Allerdale, she—
It's not just her, Delphine.
No one wants to see this hard-earned peace broken again, after all we've been through.
Parliament promised safety to the people. I know what must be done.
Very well. An old friend has a lead on her that might interest you.
By the way, I've given serious consideration to the Doctor's suggestion.
Once things have settled down for a bit, I'll return to Windermere and take back certain things that have always belonged to me.
How long will you be?
I don't know. But the Doctor is right. I need what Mother left behind, if I am to help you... even if I was reluctant at the time of her death.
Thank you.
It's for myself, too. Victoria, Windermere, Lynncardine... I must see for myself that which Mother cared so much about.
It's the only way I can get closer to her.
Sir! Please allow me to still call you that... I-I didn't think you would come to me.
Did my application go through?
Walker, I've heard how Pitt and the others got you to leave the scene of the protest. I think—
So that's not what you're here for... Alright. Actually, I've been listening to the radio, sir.
I heard about the artillery handover to the newly reorganized Defense Forces under your command, and the newly-constructed training plate outside the city.
I went to see it myself. The facilities are superb. I can even imagine myself training there...
I'm sorry, Walker, but I can't approve your application to join the Defense Forces.
Sure, if I'm not up to standard, I'll redouble my—
You know your own health. Training and fighting will only make it worse.
My condition is under control. I'm taking medicine. I haven't felt any pain from the rocks for a while! I'm getting better—
Millscar's medicine, right? We've analyzed it, and it's...
Nothing more than a painkiller. I know.
But that's what we need. Parliament's medicine rations are running out, aren't they?
At least this makes me feel better, for a while, and lets me practice wielding a crossbow with one hand. I'll pass the Defense Forces tests, sir.
'There will be hope as long as we make it back home, no matter how bloody goddamned hard life is.' That's what you told me during the defense of Chetleigh.

lines.st: 'We are the Exemplars.'
'We make miracles.'
What about life after the miracle, then? Where do the Exemplars go?

Horn said the miracle of the previous Exemplars lived on in Gaul, in the textbooks of military schools. What about us? It was a question no one needed to think about before we truly returned to Londinium.
When the city broadcast announced that the city belonged to the Victorians again, the youngest girl in my group asked me,
'Where do we go from here?'
I said we go home; I'll go back to the Slobberknocker with Hannah, Vina and Dagda, and you get to go home too.
But the girl told me...
'I don't have a home anymore. I only have you.'

lines.x: Vina and the others are here. We ought to go down.
Vina? Speaker Vina? She's coming for me too? Because of the protest?
Did you come to keep an eye on me, sir?
She came to help you because she knew Vina was coming. Same for all of us.
There is no warmth in Londinium's sunset.
The survivors do not remember if it has always been this way. Those who come after will not even question it.
Once, The Shard was the pride of this old nomadic city. Now, people pay careful attention not to step into its shadow.
They all remember the day that terror itself descended upon its pinnacle, beneath the burning red sky.
Two walk side-by-side through the sparse crowd, the setting sun drawing long shadows behind them.
Those they pass bow to them, then go back to the work at hand, humming—
♪Then may we call and savely syng,
♪Victoria, yeve thankes for owre hom!
The refurbishment of The Shard seems to be proceeding ahead of schedule.
Rhodes Island helped a lot: providing medicine, taking in patients, and the Engineering Department has been helping with construction work in the city.
The initial incident left such an impression on the people that the proposal to modify The Shard met no significant opposition.
And we're cleaning up the Originium crystal growths in the city while we're at it.
But the engineers of the Self-Salvation Corps say it'll take some time for things to go back to normal. Even the mobility layer is affected.
Have we confirmed the retrieval team for The Shard's core technologies?
We have a preliminary list, but we're still doing background checks on some of the technicians and scholars. They have... certain links, if you know what I mean.
I do. The dukes have given me a heads-up. We'll turn a blind eye, as long as there's no serious issue.
I, for one, am hoping that they find something worthwhile in that scrapheap. It would be a good thing for Victoria.
And it's not such a bad thing for us if they started fighting over it.
Vina. Delphine.
Horn? I thought you were...
I came to visit an old comrade.
Don't mind me. I was just about to go and see how Bagpipe's doing with the new recruits.
Horn stretches, but shows no sign of leaving. Vina sighs.
Vina! You would never guess what Walker and I talked about. You remember the Davises, who came with us from Chetleigh? Well, the wife just gave birth—
I know why you're here, Morgan.
Walker and the protests are a matter for Parliament... it's not something I should get involved in.
I'm just here to talk about the whereabouts of Millscar.
Thanks, but there's no need to make things difficult for Madam Speaker.
I trust Parliament. I know many MPs. Fine people.
Parliament is not as simple as you think. There are those who would prefer to escalate the issue.
But resolving the Millscar issue as soon as possible is in the best interests of everyone, including you.

lines.st: The solder who charged into the city with us stood in the setting sun, suffering our 'interrogation'.
We all know that Vina can influence Parliament... if she chooses to.
That was the day when I truly realized that Vina had not belonged solely to the Glasgow Gang for a long time.
Maybe my Vina already said goodbye to us that afternoon.
That day, all factories of the city came rumbling to life, and steam gushed up to the sky.
I saw her golden hair flying wild in the wind, blocking the fleeting tears in her eyes. She turned towards us with a smile and said—
'We're home.'
We're home. But things have not gone the way we expected.
Does going back home mean goodbye?
Will we lose Vina?
'The factories welcome us!'
'There are survivors in the city!'
'Why do you stand in our way?'
'I'm sorry... dukes' orders. The Exemplars must not act on their own until the dukes have come to a conclusion.'
'Why are you pointing your bow at me?'
'Weren't we fighting side-by-side just a moment ago?'
'What do we do? Is that a... protest? In the city?'

lines.x: The city was angry.
The troops that stood in our way... are they falling back?
The factories are waking up? I thought the Sarkaz had shut everything down.
...I remember these factories. I know where they are. I always will.
That's Miles Industries on York Street! And that's Coppola Foods on Scott Street! And that's—
'Consolidated Inc.'... 'Hankin's'... 'South Wicker Cargo'...
(Choked) Vina... I thought they...
Those that were silent, those that had been abandoned, those that had been commandeered by the Sarkaz, and those that continued to run timidly.
All the city's factories came alive at the same time, but the Sarkaz hastily pulling back from the battlefield outside the city had no time to notice.
The steam that gushed out was the breath of steel and machine. The factories were roaring.
What's going on?
They're helping us. Londinium is helping us.
March on, Exemplars. It's time to bring this to an end.
This is Lieutenant Diane Weber of the Sixth Column, Tenth Infantry Regiment. Lower your weapons, soldiers, and escort the Exemplars in their advance.
You're defying the dukes?
Before I was a soldier, I was a Londinier. This is my home too.
Go, Siege. The fight is not yet over.
We will take back what is ours!
The first squad starts lowering their crossbows, and they are soon followed by others, who step aside to clear a path.
Exemplars, forward!
We're home.
Keep the factories running at full steam! Keep the Sarkaz at bay just a bit longer!
The dukes want people to think there's no Victorians left alive, justifying them sitting on their hands out there. But we'll make 'em hear we're still here!
The people outside the walls will know we haven't given up yet!
Thanks for the assist, Madam Catherine. You can trust our guys; everyone has a trick or two up their sleeves.
Nine and the others are working on the other factories. This whole city is about to rise up.
Are you coming back... Feist?
Is it on?
On it is! The other factories have also been restored, just as the Doctor planned.
It's not just the citizens that are helping us though. There are other people, and they call themselves—
Reunion...
Well, well. I haven't seen the city so lively in years.
I just got word that alliance troops have cleared a path for the Exemplars.
That wasn't our orders. Are you happy with the test results?
It seems we have underestimated the influence of a miracle.
We should hasten our preparations, then.
Hey, boss, looks like we don't have to cause a commotion anymore.
They're letting the Exemplars through.
Pull the ambush and give a hand to the factories we captured.
Tell the people in charge that the boilers keep burning until I say stop.
You got it.
Welcome home, Vina.
Speaker's Office.
Walker's fine. Parliament won't come down hard on him over this little thing. But tell him to stay safe.
There's no telling if the people watching us might find him inconvenient to them.
And... how are things going over there?
...Good.
We'll find you...
Allerdale.
I-I just go over there like this?
Yes. Just go over there and ask what the price is.
A-Alright.
The child approaches the merchant's stall and timidly peruses the wares.
Fireworks, kid?
Y-Yeah...
A special occasion? A gift? This one's real popular for celebrations!
B-But there's a lot of dust—

That's normal; it's been lying in the warehouse for a while. How about this, you buy one, and I'll give you one free—
What's that smell—
Fire! It's you, isn't it?!
Hah!
What are you standing there for? Run!
The merchant tries to put out the fire to no avail, and is soon surrounded by flames himself.
The exploding fireworks draws a gawking crowd.
The 'practical joker' has long fled the scene with the child.
'But... wasn't it supposed to be a joke...'
'Serves him right for marrying a devil woman, the bastard.'
The fire's getting bigger! I'll grab the man; you get the crowd out of here!
Yes, ma'am!
The piledriver spear rips the flames apart. The sounds of explosions and crackling fire sing joyfully in cacophony.
A Vouivre emerges from the flames, spear in one hand, the merchant in the other. She puts the merchant down in a safe place, then sits beside him, wiping the soot off her face.
Ach, sorry aboot yer stall, I think I might've...
It's fine, fine, don't worry.
Fine? This is... fine?
She points towards the raging fire, with fireworks still leaping out from time to time.
The gawking crowd continues to grow. A drunkard, seemingly oblivious to what is going on, pulls over an unsuspecting passerby and starts dancing.
Under the setting sun is pandemonium, drunken singing mixed with angry yelling mixed with the cackle of fireworks.
This street has not been so lively since the Sarkaz took over.
It is the very picture of chaos, but the merchant, who has already calmed down, begins to laugh.
I haven't seen the folks so happy in a very long time. Brings back memories...
But... it's your stall that's on fire.
You know what? I'm in a good mood today. Let's just call it a fireworks show for everyone!
Between you and me, ma'am... my wife can finally leave the city, tonight.
No injuries, ma'am. Should we go after the troublemaker?
Well, nobody got hurt—
We've got a duty to handle anyone who commits an offense.
Well, thank you, in any case. Say, can you dance? Or play an instrument?
What?
Can't let good fireworks go to waste, can I?
Maybe my wife will hear the commotion. My way of saying goodbye.
*sigh* I'm startin' to miss wartime. Even workin' on the farm. Anythin's easier than this 'keepin' the peace' business.
But, hey, if everyone's happy, who'm I tae complain?
Once she is satisfied that there is no danger, she lies down on a crate by the roadside, stretches, and lets the beams of the setting sun fall upon her face.
Gimme a haystack over this any day.
Where are you, Chenchen...?
Wh-Why did you burn his stall? You said you were just going to snitch something!

That shameless bastard's own fault for marrying a devil.
On the bright side, maybe somebody's already lynched his wife—
Whoa!
Who the feck are you?
Now's your chance to run, kid.
O-Okay!
Wh-What do you want?
Just doing a favor for a client. Maybe it'll bring in future business.
What's the situation with this batch?
Coast is clear, and the head count checks out, no problem. But word on the streets is that Parliament is looking for you.
Nothing they can actually do about it. Same as before, don't cause any trouble.
Speaking of trouble...
There's a kid in this batch that's giving us a hard time.
Why can't I see my dad one last time before we leave?
This man says there's fireworks outside. It's dad's fireworks!
Shh, quiet.
You don't want to leave either, do you, Mum—
The Sarkaz covers the child's mouth and holds her tightly, gazing up nervously at the woman looking quietly down at her.
I'm sorry, she's just not used to this... her father never left her side, even during the war.
Our deal with your husband was to get you out of the city. The rest is not my business.
If you want to stay, that's also your own decision.
Ma'am, they're just scared because they've never been through anything like this.
None of us have... Don't worry, I'll take care of the girl. She won't cause you any trouble.
But where are we going? What about Dad?
He'll come find us once he's taken care of things at home.
