*sigh* I knew this day would come, the day the people from Kazdel came.
You don't like the Military Commission? Isn't Theresis your legendary hero?
I'm every bit the Londinier that you are. I've never been anywhere else since I was born, and I don't know no Theresis.
Your accent, that's not how commoners talk. I take it you ended up here because of the folks from Kazdel?

I—
Never mind.
You're a good person, ma'am. No one else would come close to us at a time like this.
Without you, the new Parliament might've... nothing to be surprised, I suppose. The bigwigs never change, whatever happens.
I've seen enough in my lifetime.
This new Parliament is different—
Maybe. For people like us, though, it all feels the same.
The aged Sarkaz soothes the sobbing child and sighs.
Don't be afraid, child. Your father is an honest man, more honest than most people I've met. He'll find you.
Alright... but where will you go after leaving here?
I plan on visiting some old friends. They left during the withdrawal with their twins, who work for a pharmaceutical company.
Do you know them, ma'am?
No. We have no time. You should get ready to leave.
What is your name, ma'am?
We will not meet again.
Humor an old woman, won't you? I just want to thank you, on behalf of myself, and all those who are afraid of you—
And I would rather not direct this gratitude to a code name that's ready to be cast aside any time.
Allerdale.
Beautiful name. Thank you, Allerdale.
Allerdale. A name that should have been laid to rest in the Mausoleum of Kings.

In the darkness where even the passage of time became blurred, she had sought to die in the ruins of history, to be forgotten by all those whom she disappointed, yet she was afraid to die, afraid to be forgotten.
But when muddy water dripped from the hole that the Steam Knight left behind and fell upon her palm, she opened her eyes and saw light.
What lay at the end of the light was life, and submission again.
Her hesitation may have lasted three days, or three minutes... in the end, she made the same choice as she once did.

lines.st: 'You will meet Vina again, someday.'

lines.x: She recited it over and over in her mind, and crawled quietly into the light.

lines.loc: Office of Provisional Supply ProcurementDecember 12th, 1098, 5:32 P.M.

lines.x: What do you mean, the reservation with Madam Major is no longer valid and I have to get in queue again?
Should we be trying to buy time for the Infected who can't get medication?
I'm afraid the reservation system is under maintenance. There are a lot of files that need to be organized following Madam Major's passing.
However, we are all very busy with preparations for Madam Major's funeral.
How long are we talking?
It should be up and running within seven business days. We will inform you as soon as reasonably possible.
Have you been to the Center for Biological Infection Control? More and more citizens whose conditions are deteriorating rapidly are being sent there by the day.
Most Londiniers didn't know how to handle acute Oripathy attacks before the war. The ones who need help the most are the ones getting the least of it.
They can't afford to wait... not to mention the proliferation of drugs that have little actual effectiveness...
Your feedback is noted. We will pass it on to the Center for Biological Infection Control. Is there anything else I can help you with?
Tell me where the Speaker's office is.
If you would like to visit the Speaker, I can assist you in making a reservation—
No need to ask him. I know where the Speaker is.
Come with me, Eliseo.
I apologize for the inconvenience. Please let us know if you need any other assistance. And thank you for the coffee, sir.
It is a very valuable gift.
Aren't you furious, Diane? This is no way for them to behave. I came to Londinium to—
To save us?
No... that's not what I mean.
But it's what you think, isn't it?
I-I mean no offense. I'm grateful that you've put a roof over my head, but I also see there's nothing but chaos in the matter of helping the Infected here.
I get that it takes time to build up and improve systems and workflows. Please understand, though, that it was my job to raise such issues, back in Columbia.
I just want to see more regulated and effective workflow to provide help to more Infected. And if I may say so, we're a little ahead on that front.
I've never been to Columbia, Eliseo, and I don't know how people like us are treated back where you call home.
I would love to see it, if I get the chance.
But that gentleman has done nothing wrong. He has done what was tasked of him in such cases; in fact, he has done it better than any of his predecessors.
Londinium has many problems. But things are getting better, even better than before the war.
It seems you trust this new Parliament?
No, but I trust the Speaker. Come, I'll take you to her.
How do you know where she is?
I take it you Columbians are not in the habit of listening to the broadcasts.

Well, we're more used to watching...
Never mind. Where is the Speaker?
At the memorial service for Madam Major.
Here? There are so many people, how do we get to the Speaker?
Come with me. I know a shortcut.
Through the window, Eliseo sees Vina Victoria for the first time.
He has done his homework, and heard the legends of the young Speaker during the war.
He thought the Speaker would be more impressive.
When he sees the well-dressed Aslan speaking at the podium, he feels nothing but disappointment—
Vina Victoria is not nearly as splendid as he imagined.
Are you lost?
If you're here for the service, the entrance is that way.
Would you like a hand, Eliseo?
Oh, er, thank you.
Hey, how did you—
I was the one who put the files on Pure Heart Medical—and yourself—on the Speaker's table, long before you arrived in Londinium.
So you know what I'm here for?
Of course.
And the Speaker knows what I've been trying to do for the past few days?
Of course.
Unfortunately, it's not that simple.
I thought the Speaker would be different from the other nobles I've met.
Eliseo!
It's fine. I would just like to clear up the misunderstanding. This is not Columbia, Eliseo, and it is arrogance to judge us by your standards.
Sometimes, we must follow the rules first, in order to change things.
Not even she could do it?
Inside the hall, Vina is muttering something with a sad expression on her face, but they cannot hear what she is saying.
Delphine meets Diane's gaze, then quickly averts her eyes.
Was Madam Major another victim of the rules?
No, only a respectable woman. One of those 'nobles' you were talking about.
She could have stayed in Normandy, far from danger, but she came of her own accord to the chaos of Londinium in order to help the people here.
She refused to stop working, even as her condition deteriorated, trying to help as many people as she could. Just like you, Eliseo.
Our conflict with her was only because of the harm that her radicalness and stubbornness were doing to herself.
There are no dark secrets, no conspiracies behind her passing. She was not a 'victim'.
I'm sorry, I... I didn't know.
But that makes it even harder to understand. Why do you refuse to work with Pure Heart? We're just trying to solve your problems.
Are you aware that Pure Heart Medical is backed by the Columbian Department of Defense and the Maylander Foundation?
Of course. The military and the Foundation have always been our main stakeholders.
You... are a good, responsible man. Feel free to come and find me, if you insist on carrying out that responsibility.
Oh, er... you are...?
Delphine Windermere. You may call me Delphine. Looks like your company has work to do when it comes to client research.
I'm not giving up, Lady Delphine. I believe we can help with Londinium.
I can't make any promises on behalf of Vina or Parliament. But believe me when I say that we want to heal this city more than anyone else.
But, if you would like, you can come with me, to say goodbye to Madam Major...
And to all those who have died in the chaos of the past few years.
The bell of mourning tolls. Fowlbeasts glide close to the ground, a sign of impeding rain.
Right now, though, nobody pays any heed. Their heads are bowed.
No tears are shed. War has already spent the city of its last drop.
All leads point to this place being Millscar's hideout. Why are you still taking your time?
Don't you think it's too easy?
Besides, there has been rumors of unidentified Sarkaz activity here. Can't hurt to be careful.
I say it can't hurt to have an accident or two. Good exercise.
Why do you think I refused to help Morgan with her stupid paperwork?
You're an upright girl, to come along like this. I thought you'd shut yourself up in the tower as soon as you came back.
You really ought to be staying put at the ship, Hannah. How many times has Medical reminded you about your condition—
The sound of bells echoes in the sky above the city. As if everything has gone quiet for a moment.
The memorial service has started.
Well—no time to wallow in sentiment. Let's go, Dagda. I don't want to get my arse kicked while I'm trying to wipe the tears from my eyes.
Sometimes I just don't know what to do with you, Hannah.
What do we do if we run into Allerdale?
Punch her in the gut and leave the rest for Vina to worry about.
Who's there?!
I'm afraid you won't find her. It's not you she wants to see.
Who are you?!
The Rose Riverside's Canoe.

lines.st: I couldn't decide how to start the chapter on Allerdale, when I decided to write one for her. I realized I knew so little about the name that should've been so familiar to me.
The last Cumberland. The one who betrayed us. The one who gave her life to save us. The one who bragged about her cream stew.
Millscar. Caster's minion. Cumberland the Ever on High. Which one was the real Allerdale?

lines.x: Thirty-four minutes until the Exemplars break through the blockade
Coordinates confirmed, boss. The location in the anonymous warning message is just ahead. And we've identified the source—
Rhodes Island.
No surprises there. Let's go.
The mercenary does not move. There is uncertainty on his face.
Out with it.
The boys trust you. Without you, we would have rotted in the alleys, or the devils would have—
I've already died once, so I'm fine with putting my head on the line for you. But this just doesn't make sense. Why should we trust Rhodes Island? We know nothing about them.
The Exemplars are almost inside the city. It's going to be bedlam outside. We could take this opportunity to line our pockets...
Or at least... you should not risk yourself, being here.
You're not the only one who thinks this way, are you?
The mercenary is silent. He dares not meet Millscar's gaze, fearing her wrath.
But nothing happens.
Have you thought about what will become of Londinium after this war?
Er... the way it used to be?
No, nothing will go back to the way it used to be. No one can go back, no matter what comes of this war.
Y-You're abandoning us?
No, I need you. Soon, Londinium will become a city in need of mending.
The old order has been destroyed by death and violence. There will be plenty of gaps left behind, plenty to feed us.
But—
I'm not asking your opinion.
Listen, I don't plan on going back to the old life, which means we have to plan for a new one. I haven't forgotten about you, of course.
In order to keep our livelihood, we need people to know what we're capable of.
Don't say I didn't give you a choice. So, are you coming or not?
Oh, sod it, put me at the front of the line, boss.
I'm not imagining it, am I... what is this...?
The blood-red rocks in the gaps of the frame and in the furnace... did the devils hide them here? What are they planning?
Vampire stuff.
There is a faint smell of blood in the air. She recalls the onrushing squirming monsters that came straight out of a nightmare.
Boss! I found something here!
A ducal alliance soldier?
Something's not right. He hasn't been dead for long. The name tag... Fifth Infantry Regiment, Eighth Column. Rank... Major?!
The Earl of March's troops, under the Duke of Normandy. No external wounds, but the death was not natural.
Her unit should still be outside the walls. Why would a major from the unit be here—
Where did they come from? Kill them, and quickly. The orders must not be delayed.
There are other altars that still need to be activated. There is no time.
The devils have us surrounded! There's no way out—
Why do we need one?
'Lunatic'.
No one knows where the Steam Knight sword in her hand came from, much less where Millscar herself came from.
The mercenary's legs tremble. He regrets not turning around a few minutes ago.
As long as that purple blade would not impale him from behind.
Bugger all.
Huh. I know that sword.
The General's briefing mentioned you. You've got nerve. Is the tin can somewhere nearby?
Maybe we can kill two fowlbeasts with one stone.
Hmph.
Carry on with your work, I'll handle this—
*jingle*
Like wind chimes on the beams of a mausoleuma caressed by a breeze, the golden pendants and gems that hang from the veil of the one who slumbers sway and clang against each other in the air.
Excuse me, did anyone see my earring?
There you are, Little Garnet—Oh, it's broken...
I guess I'll have to borrow your toys, Duq'arael. Not like you're in any position to refuse.
The blood-red altar within the iron frame begins to glow, but quickly goes dark again and then shatters.
The broken red pendant in the air is now good as new, glowing with a faint light.
The red glow is almost mesmerizing.
*jingle*
The sound echoes by Millscar's ear.
Take care of Little Garnet for me, for a while.
She'll lead you where you belong. Now, you should go.
I'm surprised that anyone like Dame Kal'tsit would trust you, though.
She sees the red and black figure.
Why would a vampire save my life? Dame Kal'tsit? Dr. Kal'tsit of Rhodes Island? What are they planning?
She has many questions, but finds herself holding the cold conical earring in her hand.
There's no time to waste if you want to help that one. This is not the only altar in these parts.
What do I do?
She will tell you.
'She'? Millscar suddenly feels a pulse from the earring in her hand, like a heartbeat.
Stop her—
Shh.
*Sarkaz expletive*!
Nadine feels all the blood in her body rush back towards her heart for a second, and the vertigo that comes with it.
For the first time, she envies the kindred who have abandoned flesh and blood.
The path has been prepared.
Take care of her, and she will guide you to me.
Six minutes until the Exemplars break through the blockade
Let's go to the next one.
Boss, that weird Vampire thing... as we destroy the altars, it just keeps getting... prettier.
We've got more important things to worry about. Word is that the Exemplars have made it into the city, but they're being impeded by the ducal alliance's troops.
Have the factories gotten the order?
Aye. They're getting ready.
These strange hidden Sarkaz altars, and rumors that the dukes are ready to leave Londinium blockaded and abandon it to its fate...
Hah. Who's the one who actually wants to blow up the city?
Does it really matter?
It's been a while, Allerdale.
I thought you were dead.
I thought YOU were dead.
Even with Rhodes Island's help, there are too many of these for you to deal with, and too little time.
Who knows how many such altars the Sarkaz have hidden across the city? Without my knowledge, too. They could not have done it on their own.
More than one key district will be reduced to rubble if these altars are activated. I've discovered and destroyed several key ones, however.
By yourself...?
Vina needs help. The Exemplars need one final push. And the dukes need to hear the true voice of the people.
It was I who spread the rumors that the dukes are planning to abandon Londinium. Not that there wasn't an element of truth to it.
What do you want me to do?
You're already doing it. You and those who follow you.
But you, too, need a little push. I'll take care of the remaining altars.
You've changed. A lot.
I could say the same for you, 'Millscar'. Are you sure you like that name?
You've chosen to live. Then follow the path, and see where it leads.
Boss, she's—
Run like you've got all Kazdel on your heels. Go to the outer city, and tell the others to get ready to receive the Exemplars.
The Exemplars break through the blockade. Across the city, factories roar to life, welcoming them home.

lines.st: I keep thinking back to that afternoon.
Factories, Vina, steam, cheering, boxing, angry yelling, Baird...
The bits of memory gush to the surface together, mixing together, wiping away what happened that afternoon.
The one thing I will never forget is when Vina said: 'We're home.'
I wanted to cry. It was a long, long journey, from the day we left the city to the day we came back.
We're home. But how many did we lose, how many who walked with us on this journey?
And which of them could come back after they left, besides Allerdale?
I miss Baird.

lines.x: Two days after the Exemplars break through the blockade
I never knew taking inventory could be such a pain.
Taking inventory at the factories on our turf back in the day wasn't half as bad...
If this is pain, I'll gladly take more pain.
It sure is exciting, though, to see so many people supporting us—
(Yawn) We've been at this all night. The sun's already coming up.
We have to push forward. The others can only buy so much time.
Word from Wiš'adel is that they threw a dozen wrenches into the Military Commission's system of command, but the Nachzehrers are still guarding Westhaleg.
We've been inside the city for two days, but there has been little sign of movement from them.
Weren't there rumors that Theresis has abandoned them and gone into hiding? Maybe that's why the Nachzehrers aren't moving.
That's just speculation. Not even the Doctor's team has been able to confirm it.
Get some rest, Morgan. The alliance's order to attack will come soon—
I just don't like counting, that's all!
I want to help Dagda. I reckon I've got a little more experience sweet-talking people into helping us.
Delphine is persuading the nobles who are still on the fence. Hannah and Catherine are organizing the surviving workers and Self-Salvation Corps. I have no doubt they can get it done.
But Dagda... she's not really the temperament for this...
Go help her.
Phew.
Let's continue. Supplies from Heidi... improvised weapons by the Self-Salvation Corps...
Huh?
What's this at the bottom? It's not on the list.
She looks around and sees only Exemplars transporting supplies. She recognizes all their faces.
She carefully examines the box. After being satisfied that there is no threat, she opens it.
She is struck dumb by what she sees.
It is a set of clothing.
The cool rays of the morning sun shines upon an emblem—
The emblem of House Victoria. Her house.
Vina feels a bout of anger. Is this some kind of sick joke?
Wait, no—
She recognizes the outfit in her hands. It belonged to the Victorian royal family.
Why is it here? Who found it and delivered it to her?
She can only come to one conclusion, a single name.
Let's go. It's time.
What's the situation, boss?
Their equipment is inferior to the alliance's. The dukes are having the Exemplars take the brunt of the fighting. Casualties will be heavy...
But this is also an opportunity.
The dukes want a hero. The people need a hero. Why not add a finishing touch to the story?
You're betting on them to win.
Bet? No, the bets were already made when the battle outside the walls ended. This is just the last chance to double down.
No idea how that works, but whatever you say. What's next?
We kill.
There will be chaos once the final battle begins, and 'opportunists' too. I'm not about to let them do whatever they want right under my nose.
Say... boss, what was that thing you left behind?
A little stage prop, to give more substance to the legend.
'Garment of the royal house, retrieved from the ruins of Cumberland Manor. The leader of the Exemplars can make good use of it.'
'Don't you like the story? The last heir to the throne returns to drive out the devils, garbed in the King's regalia...'
'I heard that she didn't like the title...'
'She'll need it, if she wants to sway those who are still uncertain.'
'For now, at least.'
Is she alive—
Vina has had a little wish, ever since the day that the Steam Knight returned. Perhaps those around her had the same, but no one spoke of it.
She looks around nervously, but does not see her.
If anything, that is a relief. She is not ready.
A note?
'Tailored to your size. Hope it fits.'
There is no name on the note.
But for the first time, she knows that Allerdale is still alive.
There's something else...
A few photos, with the following words scribbled on the back.
'Something you might find interesting. Concerning a certain duke.'
I brought the Defense Forces files that were requested.
Madam Speaker, since the establishment of the patrols, we have received no fewer than two hundred requests for assistance involving Millscar and her followers.
There is concrete evidence of their violation of Parliament's newly-enacted laws.
What are we waiting for? They're getting in the way of honest people doing honest work! Give the order! Put their heads on pikes outside the Sineselde!
Feist and Rockrock have already reported the situation to me.
I want the rebuilding to progress smoothly as much as you do, but that's not how Parliament works.
Go ahead, Viscount Elsa.
Millscar's behavior is certainly questionable, but their medicine has at least provided consolation to those suffering from the current shortage, if nothing else—
Balderdash!
They're exacting payment in lives!
Sit DOWN, Lord Alan! You are in Parliament!
They are using underground facilities, relics from the war, to evade our pursuit, Viscount Elsa.
They've been decentralizing their operations, of course, but there is also evidence indicating that someone associated with Parliament is helping them.
I believe we need an explanation for that, don't we?
Are the Londinium Defense Forces requesting our assistance with the investigation, Lieutenant Skamandros?
We are, of course, eager to help the citizens face the Millscar threat. I could contact Her Grace the Duke of Caster to arrange for personnel to be deployed to Londinium—

It is much reassuring to hear that Her Grace the Duke of Caster has, at long last, deigned to generously offer her valuable assistance to Parliament, Viscount Elsa. I am very curious to know if other dukes will do the same.
Baron Hector, Viscount Gideon, Dr. Herman, and Academician Erwin... may I trust you to pass on the message to the other dukes should you, by chance, have the opportunity to communicate with them?
Startled, the members whose names are called, who have been keeping a low profile in the room, are left with little choice but to smile and bow to Vina.
There is no affirmation or denial, but the argument in the chamber has evaporated, as though it never existed to begin with.
A special provisional court will be convened of the Members of Parliament to deal with the Millscar issue. Millscar will, of course, be granted the opportunity to present her defense.
There is whispering amongst the seats, but no objections.
Will she show up for the trial, though?
If she does not appear, she will forfeit her right to a defense, and the court shall pass judgment in a fair and just manner.

Horn, kindly instruct patrols to take Millscar into custody and bring her to the Parliamentary Chamber.
Prior to Parliament's recess today...
She has two hours. I will wait for her here.
You should wait in the office, get some rest. You don't look so well.
I'll let you know if she comes—
I said, I'll wait here!
Right then.
Sorry. I'm just—
I know.
I don't understand. There are so many things that are much more urgent—Infected in need of treatment, shortage of supplies, rebuilding the city, restoring order...

My desk is constantly covered in important matters that require my attention.
But they would rather spend an entire day bickering over something that they don't even disagree about—
*sigh*...
I'm not doing a good enough job.
I remember that I promised you she would not get away, if you give my people and the Defense Forces free rein to search for her.
We're short-handed. Trying to solve the problem this way means ignoring other, more urgent problems in the city—
Then why do you think you're not doing a good enough job?
Don't try to carry a nomadic city on your back. That's what the tracks underneath it are for.
You don't have to become the perfect leader that they talk about, for whom solving every single problem is taken for granted.
Besides... from Millscar to the protests to the medicine shortage, the shadow of the dukes loom large over all these recent affairs.
There is no doubt that Caster is behind the Millscar matter, but something has to give. Millscar has gone too far.
Catching her is no easy task, though. Indra and Dagda say she has associated with a strange Vampire.
Delphine... if she doesn't show—
Do you want her to, Vina?
Wh-Why are you blindfolding me? I've paid my husband's bill!
Yes, ma'am, you're an honest woman.
You have paid the bill of Mickey Robinson. The boss sends her condolences. Your husband made many contributions to the city.
C-Can I go now?
Of course. Once you've paid your other debt, that is.
Two contracts. One, signed by your husband, the one you just paid. Another, signed by you on behalf of your husband.
But he's gone!
He's gone, but the medicine isn't. Here, do take it. Now, let's talk about your bill...
We accept credit, of course—
We don't.
Oh, boss. Er... Since when we don't—
Just now. Take her to 'get some rest'.
Alright. And then?
Send her to the Sineselde. The honorable MPs are waiting for me, aren't they?
Out of the way! Let the doctors work!
We've identified the victim, Vina. One Mrs. Lawrence, of Norport.
Are the injures life-threatening?
No, the vital organs were unharmed. She's just lost a lot of blood. Millscar's people did it.
But rumors are beginning to spread, and that's not a good thing.
Her husband, Mickey Robinson, recently passed away from acute Oripathy after being unable to get proper treatment.
He was one of the Exemplars who came home with us.
This is her answer.
Escort Mrs. Lawrence to the Defense Forces medical camp, Delphine.
What about you?
...Two more minutes.
I will keep the word of the provisional court... and my own.
At Delphine's behest, several MPs carefully carry the unconscious woman out of the Parliamentary Chamber. The carpet on the floor, which has only recently been replaced, is soaked in her blood.
It was the Sarkaz who had bled here two months ago.
There is order on the floor again.
One more minute. There is no one, except two black feathers that seem to be staring at Vina.
They fell from the unconscious woman. As the group carrying her walked past, they were swept up into the air, only to slowly drift down.
That is the place where she should have stood.
The clock strikes six.
It is time. Esteemed members of Parliament, as Millscar has forfeited her right to a defense—
In accordance with the law, I, Vina Victoria, Speaker of the Parliament of Londinium, pronounce—
A special warrant is hereby issued for the arrest of Millscar, dead or alive. Defense Forces patrols are to apprehend Millscar personnel by any means necessary.
Effective... immediately.
Also, issue a city-wide announcement that Parliament will accelerate the enactment of the City Safety Act.
On behalf of Parliament, I vow to the citizens of Londinium that the curfew will be fully lifted within a month, when the reorganization of the police forces is complete.
The provisional court will complete the necessary personnel movement and additions within two months, at which point it will be renamed the Londinium City Court.
The court will continue to process backloaded cases concerning public safety following the conclusion of the war.
Parliament and I will ensure that everyone is treated in a fair and just manner—
Regardless of any privileges that one may have enjoyed, for whatever reason—
All shall stand equal before the law.

opts: What kind of leader do you want to be, Vina?

lines.x: I... I don't know, Doctor.
But I should at least know what is right and what is wrong, no?

opts: Sometimes, right and wrong depend entirely on where you stand.

lines.x: Then what should I do?

opts: Accept those who despise you, for they keep you vigilant.
Stay true to what you believe in, to help you triumph over solitude.
I learned that from a friend who recently left us.

lines.x: Bagpipe reporting in. Sweep complete in T-11 and T-32. No sign of Millscar.
How's things over there?
No sign of Millscar in C-11 and C-20, ma'am.
What about L-10? No reports yet?
Reynold? Is there a situation?
I-I'm here!
Th-There's nobody here in L-10—
His gaze alternates between the purple blade before him and Millscar, imploring for mercy.
(Nods)
I-I'm coming back now.
Did you find anything useful?
N-No! Nothing at all!
The purple blade is lowered. He slowly backs off.
Alright. Meet up at the rendezvous point.
B-Be there in a minute.
Leave the communicator.
O-Okay.
He keeps his eyes on Millscar in the shadows. Once he is sure that she is not going to kill him, he drops the communicator, and flees for his life.
So quiet...
I prefer the underground. At least the sound there doesn't make me feel—
Aha.
Parliament has yet to lift the post-war curfew. They are trying to protect the people... from people like her.
The communicator that she just obtained continues to supply the latest news about the patrols.
Millscar strolls past the shops by the roadside. She hears the faint sound of radio from one of them, and stops before the dimly-lit storefront.
What do you think about Madam Speaker's announcement that the curfew will be lifted in stages as law and order are restored, Hans?
Considering the current pace at which the police and courts are being reorganized? A joke, if you ask me. Who's arresting whom, and who's judging whom?
You don't trust Parliament? Many citizens sleep sounder in their beds, knowing that Parliament has been hard at work rooting out the remaining devils over the past two months.
Today's sweep of underground insurgents has been particularly effective, eliminating another long-term threat to public safety.
In the span of three hours, we have received thousands of messages from citizens expressing their gratitude to Parliament, in particular to our honorable—dare I say, majestic, even—Madam Speaker.
I do not deny that she has been a decisive leader, up till now. How long do you think she can keep it up, though?
I bet you ten pounds that in three months, you wouldn't be able to tell her from those fat, filthy, lying nobles—
Hmph.
A lavish car stops next to her. The door opens. There is only the driver... and a set of familiar clothing.
She recognizes the emblem of House Cumberland.
You're alive. Her Grace the Duke is willing to recognize your value.
She is giving you a chance to come back.
Tell her that Cumberland has died.
And one who has already died once is not afraid to die again.
The door closes, and the car leaves, disappearing at the end of the street.
Bloody hell... looks like it's not just you who want to kill me, Vina.
You'd better hurry...
She walks silently in the shadows by the side of the street, disappearing into the night.
At least there will be more people who believe that you can give them that most precious, most valuable of things right now—safety.
I hope you like my final gift to you, Alexandrina...
After this, we're even.

lines.st: Morale was high before our attack on the Nachzehrer position at the Palace of Westhaleg. Couldn't say the same about the people in the city though.
They had lived for so long under Sarkaz rule that they could hardly even conceive of resistance. Dagda and I found it difficult to persuade able-bodied citizens to join us...
Until Vina showed up.
She was wearing an outfit I never thought I would see her in... an outfit for a king. She never said who gave it to her, but we could all guess the answer.
Allerdale.
I was dumbfounded. I looked at Dagda, and saw her fighting to contain her trembling.
I saw the frenzied crowd rush through streets and alleys, scrambling over one another to join us.
They carried Vina, further and further away from me.
'Her Royal Highness Alexandrina has returned! She'll lead her people to drive out the Sarkaz and take back our home!

lines.x: I thought you would reject my proposal.
The important thing is to win this final battle. Everything else is secondary.
The scion of Cumberland yet lives?
I hope so. She deserves a second chance, no matter what.
What about you? Do you understand the significance of donning this garment?
I do. But I always had a choice, didn't I?
Vina walks through the crowd, escorted by Delphine. Everyone bows to her in reverence.
This is hard to take, though...
She thought she saw a certain figure in the crowd, drowned out by the onrushing soldiers and citizens.
What is it?
...Nothing. Let's get the troops in order. It's time to put an end to this.
'You and your friends will win, Vina. The present I left you, the garb that always belonged to you, is merely icing on the cake of your final victory.'
'There is no place for me by your side when victory comes. This is the choice that I made.'
'But the end of this absurd war is simply the beginning of your story. Can you remain the ever-victorious legend when that time comes?'
'Victoria is not easily changed by anyone, not even you.'
'But perhaps, I would truly have believed—'
'Goodbye, Vina... Goodbye.'
Good job, Madam Speaker, I say!
Rip up every tile in Londinium and root out the devil scum hiding under them! *cough* *cough*...
I hear there's gonna be a big shipment of Oripathy meds coming in.
I got a friend at the procurement office. Maybe he can get me some meds first, and I'll see if I can get some for you, hah... cheers— *hic* —
You were strutting all day when you got into the duke's army... and then you went to the Exemplars, and now here you are after the end of the war, just like me.
Two Infected with nothing left—
Who are you calling Infected?!
I can fight! I'm not like you! I'm not afraid to die... *vomit*—
The other patrons look up at the staggering soldier.
A coin flies across his face and lands on the bar counter. A haggard figure grabs him by the shoulder.
He's drunk. Take him home. I've got the bill.
Don't mind what they said, madam.
He's just telling the truth, Lieutenant.
I'm retired too. But suit yourself.
Sure. As for your question... it doesn't matter if I was talking to the one whose name's on the warrant, or Reunion, or anybody else.
The workers want to do something for our home. I can't just stand by and watch everything get worse.
Extraordinary times call for extraordinary measures. That's how things have always been for us.
Just friendly advice. I've seen what medicine from dubious sources can do. I watched a soldier collapse into his drink a couple of days ago.
I'd performed emergency surgery on him during the war.
That time, he survived. But this time... it was the last checkup he ever got.
You're a kind soul.
The apothecary in my neighborhood says he can't remember how many came to him for poison during the war.
I don't remember everyone I've ever treated, of course.
I've seen much worse in my time as field medic, but at least I get to see those who walk out the field hospital again.
Spend enough time drinking here, and you begin to recognize the faces in the crowd that you've seen before, even if it was only once.
I know some doctors, the Rhodes Islanders. Maybe you need their help.
You forget that I'm a physician myself... what do you think of the Exemplars?
It's asking a lot, getting an old burdenbeast to remember something that long ago.
It's ancient history, even to me. All I remember is that decades ago, when kids would brag about this and that, they wouldn't be tossing around the names of dukes, it was the Exemplars.
There were lots of heroes. Lots of them lost.
No, what I mean is—
I know what you wanted to hear, but I don't have anything else for you. Sorry.
Most people in the new 'Exemplars' aren't soldiers. You can't compare them to the old ones.
We didn't start celebrating here in the city when the dukes defeated the Sarkaz, that's the end of it.
The walls were on fire, and unarmed Londiniers trapped inside with the Sarkaz.
Nobody knew if the dukes would spend the next two months talking before they entered the city. Nobody knew what would become of us.
It was the Exemplars who came. No one can take away what you did, not in Londinium, at least.
Catherine pauses. She notices the officer's hand trembling in the dim light of the tavern.
He reaches into his pouch, trying to find something, but finally sighs and takes a big swig from his mug.
These days, the only contribution most of us make is to the business of the city's drinking establishments.
Thankfully, the kindly owner here doesn't throw out loitering drunkards.
After all, most of those who fought tooth and nail to get back home no longer have homes at all.
To hard-won victory.
Shearer raises his mug, but Catherine does not do the same.
I'm afraid you found the wrong drinking partner. I have an appointment to keep, and I intend to be sober for it.
You're drunk, medic. Leave the alcohol to your later days.
I've heard the rumors about the Exemplars. Why don't you talk to the Speaker directly?
Catherine gets up, goes around the other patrons, nods at the owner behind the counter, and disappears behind a door in the corner obscured by boxes.
...What makes you think I haven't?
He looks in the direction where Catherine went. It leads to a forgotten corner of the city.
The Defense Force patrols went in many times, but never found anything.
None of my business.
He waves the proprietor over for another drink.
Lieutenant Shearer sent two messages. I asked the Messenger and both came from this underground pub.
But Delphine said she made arrangements for all the surviving soldiers of Duke Windermere's ship.
Are you sure we don't want to check out the street that Delphine talked about first?
No. Let's go in.

lines.st: I followed behind Vina and let her open the door, because I was still used to kicking them in.
The Glasgow Gang never, ever, caused trouble in Auchterigg Borough.
Vina said we were just getting a drink with some old Exemplar friends, but I knew there was a lot on her mind.
She had made arrangements to stop some factories and ration the refined Originium reserves for the winter.
But the word 'Exemplars' was always there at the bottom of her memo—our old friends.

lines.x: Deal. You're gonna answer my questions frankly and truthfully, right?
As long as you beat me at arm wrestling.
Brr—
Piece of cake.
Hmph, I figured my injury wouldn't be a problem...
I know what you want to ask.
'Will she disband the Exemplars?'
Honestly, I don't know. The stuff she's thinking about has been getting more and more complicated, and I've given up trying to figure it out.
My guess is no. She even came to see us in the clothes that she wore when we fought together. If that's not sending a message, I don't know what it is.
Didn't she say she just didn't want to draw too much attention?
That's what's in all the books! You wouldn't get it.
What you see here is no exception.
Many Exemplars are suffering from their lingering war wounds.
With medicine in short supply, alcohol represents one of the few sources of comfort available to them.
While some are here for information, most are here all day and night because they simply have nowhere else to go.
Is this why you wrote to me? To stand up for them?
No... I see you have not read my letter.
I'm sorry, there's been so much going on—
You used to be more straight and forthcoming, Madam Speaker.
If you think the Exemplars are calling for you to pay more attention to veterans' welfare, I can tell you that you're wrong.
They're waiting for your next order.
Everyone I've talked to for the past two months believes that Siege has grown since becoming Speaker, that she's getting even better as a leader.
And the question they ask the most is—where do we go next?
Are you telling me to continue to send them to bleed and die, instead of trying to make life better for them?
You never truly saw yourself as their leader. You saw them as equals, as comrades in arms. I respect you for that, but it's also where you have failed in your responsibilities.
Forgive my frankness, but for many of them, what's left of their lives will never know a greater happiness than the taste of victory they enjoyed as the Exemplars.
He grabs his mug and tries to take a swig.
But it slips from his hands and falls to the ground.
The others look towards Shearer, whose eyes are red as he takes deep breaths, trying to keep himself under control.
He takes out a coin, puts it on the table, and raises his hand at the proprietor without looking at him. On his part, the proprietor simply goes about cleaning up the mess without batting an eyelid.
One more of the same, please.
Where were we, Madam Speaker?

lines.st: I never got along well with Lieutenant Shearer. For a long time, Vina's fist knocking him dizzy had cemented my impression of him.
A field medic. Stubborn, but largely calm and dependable.
His surgeon's hands brought many back from the brink of death.

lines.x: You... why—
Lingering effect of an injury.
It was one hell of a battle, wasn't it?

lines.st: It wasn't his hand that was injured. I remember it well.
He sat straight and tall. I thought he had recovered with Delphine's help.
As he said, though, it was one hell of a battle we fought to return to Londinium.

lines.x: Any news about the situation in the city?
Unclear.
As of yesterday, the Military Commission still had a heavy presence in important areas.
With the Royal Court elites deployed around the walls in a standoff with the dukes, however, the resistance within the city stands a better chance.
The siege's gone on ten days already! When are the dukes gonna charge?
If the priority is to minimize casualties amongst the allied forces, then besieging the city is the best way, particularly with the Duke of Wellington's position still uncertain...
But they're playing with the lives of innocent citizens! If this drags on much longer...
Are they really okay with taking over an empty city?
If the truth about the situation here gets out—
Nothing gets out. They will not allow anything to happen out of their control.
People often underestimate the dukes, but the truth is, they have never changed.
Just like how they treated the Steam Knights.
It's only one example... I can't say I know all of them well, but I knew my own mother.
Ethics is the first thing to go out the window when dealing with matters of shared interest.
The focus had already shifted to how to retrieve The Shard tech at the joint staff meeting last night.
So they've already accepted this outcome.
Dagda...
I need a squad, Siege. I'm going to try that Confessarius passage that Shining shared with us again—
I'll be back, Vina. I promise.
Go.
You're just gonna let her go like that?
I'm keeping an eye on you, Dagda. Now's not the time to play the hero!
Well, you'd better not fall behind then, Hannah.
Delphine, any updates from Feist and Rockrock?
They've reached the backup access port from when the wall was first built, but they can't find an opportunity to get close with all the Sarkaz patrols.
The Doctor's team is trying to give us an opening. One feint attack is all that it takes to create an opening, but...
The alliance doesn't plan to attack at all.
Correct.
Delphine, can you guarantee that you'll find a way inside, if there's a gap in the Sarkaz lines?
Yes, but I need help. We can try to persuade the dukes again—
Vina shakes her head.
Give me two hours.
I'll pull a squad with decent Arts skills and some battle drones. We'll feign an attack on the access port at N12 to draw their attention—
You take the rest of them.
No! You'll be going to your death!
Chetleigh, Brentwood, the Gloriana, Silverrock Bluffs... we were going to our deaths, each and every time.
But somebody has to do it. I'm with you, Vina.
There is no answer from Vina. Morgan notices a strange look on her face.
...Vina?
Urgent message from the Doctor—
Loughshinny just went into the Wellington encampment.
...Who's Loughshinny?

lines.st: There were many things on the battlefield that I didn't understand. Calm and storm, victory and defeat, everything happened so fast.
After the alliance had besieged the city for ten days, the Gastrell, which had long departed the ducal alliance camp, suddenly moved into the heart of the allied position.
For the first time in a very long while, those who were listening to the alliance comms channel heard the voice of the old duke—
'The Gastrell shall restore the Empire's glory.'
Guns went blazing...
Broadsides fired straight at our city walls.

lines.x: Tell the people behind us, no delay through the trenches!
The bulk of the Sarkaz forces are being tied up at the front, but that doesn't mean we have all day.
Sir, there's a lot of equipment in the camp that we need time to—
There is no time!
We can deal with the equipment issue once we get into the city.
Some of the wounded can't walk on their own. We'll need to carry them by stretcher.
You follow the path that Siege has cleared for us. I'll organize the transfer of the wounded. Now go!
I'll help, sir. I'm more familiar with the situation of individual patients.
Plus, I promised them I wouldn't leave them behind.
Grab your own share of suppressant and protective gear from the patients' supplies. Better than nothing, at least.
The closer we get to the walls, the higher the atmospheric Originium particle density. The wounded will be in more danger than us.
Yes, sir.
They say you're not a Londinier, sir. Why would you risk your neck like this?
Look! Another battleship is giving covering fire for the Gastrell!
The Duke of Caster...
Weren't they saying that the Duke of Wellington betrayed us and fell out with the Duke of Caster?
Shit!
Contact his liege, we have sighted a small force of Victorians. Request permission to engage.
Get them. Leave survivors. Find out what their unit's objective is.
What happened to the clouds?
...Fire.
The sky lights up with the fire on the city walls.
The first wave of purple flame came from the bow of the Gastrell, gushing towards the walls of Londinium. And again. And again.
Like the cold waves of death that splashed against the sheer cliffs of Iberia.
It fell upon the Nachzehrers before Shearer, burning away the rot.
Londinium's walls are alight, ignited by Draco fire.
The Sarkaz defenders became kindling for the purple inferno.
Th—They're dead?
Careful. The fire has cleared a path, but it's a threat to us too.
Hah.
In the distance, he sees the figure on the withered throne fade in and out of the purple flames, gunfire from the ships roaring beside it.
On the walls, death ignites death.
Beneath the walls, the living walk amongst the ashes of the dead.
This is a war that will shock all the Core nations.
It is not the number of casualties that will interest them the most... but how many people on both sides are actually fighting for the city of Londinium itself.
It's a long way home for you.
...Lieutenant...
Lieutenant... Shearer... wake... up...
He sees a pair of hands reach out towards him.
...Lady Delphine?
Don't move. I'll carry you. We need to get back to the forward position that we captured.
We have to move before more Sarkaz arrive and fill this place with witchcraft bombs.
I—thank you—saving my life—
No need. It was a brave soldier and nurse, Sarah, who saved your life.
She used her Arts to protect you and the other wounded soldiers from the witchcraft bomb, despite her limited experience.
It was her... wait, did she—
Don't worry, she just passed out. We've taken her to the forward position at the access port.
But you've all been exposed to high-Originium environments for too long...
I knew the risks.
You succeeded in reaching the access port?
Yes.
I saw fire on the wall... it's out.
Is the battle for the walls over?
No, it has only just begun.
The Nachzehrer is locked in a standoff with the ducal alliance, relying on the city's defensive artillery and the elite troops.
Is there anything we can do?
Break through the access port, create a gap in the wall, and reveal a weakness in the Sarkaz lines.
Just like the alliance helped us... even if they didn't mean to.
Understood—*cough*—
Lieutenant Shearer, you are dismissed from this operation. That is an order.
Does that come from Siege—
It comes from me.
Stand by at the forward position. You need to rest.

lines.loc: Exemplars Forward Position, City Wall Access PortSeptember 25th, 1098, 8:21 A.M.

lines.x: ♪Then may we call and savely syng,
May the vast lands they keep owre kynge. ♪
......His people, and alle his well-wyllynge. ♪
And give them grace wythoute endyng. ♪
Why aren't you singing?
Cheer up, man! I know, we're all hurt, but we're almost home!
♪Then may we call and savely syng,
♪Victoria, yeve thankes for owre hom!
V—Victoria, yeve thankes for owre hom! ♪
Here, you can have the rest of this ration. Couldn't finish it.
It's our first breakfast since we came back home. Got to fill up!
Once the rest of our men make it into the city, we'll show those devils who really runs Londinium!

I don't like that song...
What about you, Vina?
Don't ask her. She hums it in her sleep.
Hannah!
Shut your gob and eat up, Indra.
Or I'll take it, if you don't want it, and I'll lead the assault since you got hurt attacking the Confessarius passage.
Not a chance. It's just a scratch.
A little scratch won't dull my fists. You're not gettin' ahead of me this time, Dagda.
Hmph.
I'll do another round of patrol and scout ahead once I'm done, make sure there's no mistake.
The Military Commission set up defenses at this backup access port, which should only have been known to the workers who built the wall. This means they know the city much better than we expected.
I'm back from patrol, Lady Delphine.
Spare a ration? I haven't had breakfast yet.
Lieutenant?! I thought you were recovering from your injuries.
I'm a medic. I know how to restore a soldier to combat effectiveness as quickly as possible.
I understand my own body, Lady Delphine. I am ready to return to duty.

Giving your protective equipment to the wounded, getting exposed to high-Originium environments, and being in close proximity to a witchcraft bomb explosion... you know exactly what all this means, Lieutenant.
I do. A blood test is not possible now, but I understand the pathological progress of infection.
What about it? Is that not normal in this place?
He looks around. Exemplars are crowded around the narrow passage.
The dust and Originium particles that cover their faces have not diminished the fire in their eyes at all.
They know what they are doing. They know the price they will pay.
But they are singing.

lines.st: Singing with joyous grace.

lines.x: Delphine, do you give the Lieutenant permission to return to duty?
Delphine can see the painkiller injector that Shearer is trying to hide behind his back.
Without sufficient access to Oripathy suppressants, painkillers are the next best thing to deal with the suffering brought on by Oripathy.
Will you pull through?
Of course, Lady Delphine. Never forget, I still have to see you go home and take back what is yours.
...You didn't need to do this.
Neither did you.
You want to witness what this ragtag team can achieve, what will come of this city. So do I.
Very well, Lieutenant. Back to your post.
What will Londinium be, once we drive out the devils, Vina?
Better than before, at least.
You know what I'd like to see? More flower shops, making the flowers cheaper, making it easier for me to find a girl to marry. Maybe a wealthy young woman. Or a widow who lost her husband.
I want more Originium fuel for a warmer winter.
...I want more police stations, and I wanna become a cop. I want to give, not take.
What about you?
I... I hope Mum's still waiting for me back home.
Hah, don't worry! She'll be there to open the door for you.
What about you, Dagda? You gonna stick with us to get the gym back in business, or will you lock yourself away in your tower?
You and I, we've got a score to settle, Hannah.
Let's go back to the gym together, Vina!
Yes, we will... we haven't found Baird yet...

lines.st: One more step until we're home. That one step took us three days.
We were welcomed by steam gushing from the factories, reaching into the clouds.
But many didn't live to see it. They walked into the tunnel, singing, and never came out again.

lines.x: Have one on me, Vina!
The devils ran, but they're not dead yet! They still have that tech that filled the city with Originium dust. They'll be back, mark my words!
And you can bet those dukes won't give a shit about us when something happens again.
Like you said, we need to defend ourselves.
That's why I don't believe the rumors that say the Exemplars are getting disbanded. Right, Vina?
Do you remember the wishes we made at the access port?
For the factory boilers to burn bright as they used to, for the guns on Londinium's walls to defend her as they used to.
For the doors of the police stations to be wide open to all, for the police to defy the nobles' will and treat the Infected with fairness and justice.
For the law of Parliament to be the only rule... that's what I hope to see, and what I'm trying to bring about.
Are you happy with Londinium now? Has she gotten better?
I can tell you that I'm not happy.
You're dodging the question, Madam Speaker.
If you choose to do nothing and keep the status quo of the Exemplars, that's entirely fine with everyone.
All they want is an answer. Will the Exemplars continue to exist—
This is my answer, Lieutenant.
What Parliament fears is your trust in me, personally, that runs deeper than your trust in Parliament itself.
Perhaps they fear that the Exemplars will become the private army of Vina Victoria.
What will become of the glory and honor of the Exemplars when that day comes?
Will the day come when our wishes come true?
Thank you. I will inform Parliament of what I learned today. It's not my place to give an answer over the heads of Parliament.
Let's go, Morgan.
Morgan?
You go first, Vina. I'll have a couple of rounds with the guys here.
...Sure.

lines.st: I didn't understand what Vina said that day.
But I agreed with the Lieutenant. Our Exemplar comrades needed our help. That's why I needed to stay, stay on Vina's behalf, at least talk to them.
Even though she was the Speaker, and there was so much she could do...
For the first time since the war ended, I drank until I passed out. It burned my belly.
It was Hannah who found me, and I asked her the question that I could never ask Vina—
When can we go home, back to our gym?

lines.x: The herbs are growing well. Diane would be glad.
When the farm was rebuilt, it was she who persuaded the engineers to leave behind the damaged lighting equipment and refit it for small greenhouses that didn't draw so much power.
Sorry... remind me, where were we?
We were talking about your condition.
You mentioned that you noticed a significant increase in the strength of your Originium Arts.
Yes. Sorry, I'm finding it hard to remember things lately. Keep getting distracted. The doctor says Originium has made its way into my brain...
Don't tell me, I've told you this before too?
We're just chatting. You can say anything you want. Anything else that doesn't feel right?
Difficulty walking straight? It's happening to a lot of people, though.
Ataxia. It's a common symptom of many Infected in Londinium, but I'm not sure what the cause is.
When I'm by myself, I've been trying to... it's strange. My Originium Arts seem to be changing.
Changing? I don't have any other reports of—
Never mind. Please continue, Sarah.
Are you casting with your bare hands, by the way? The doctors may have told you this already, but that's detrimental to keeping your condition under control.
Heh. You're a worrier alright, just like Diane said.
I just can't help it. It's how I search for the war memories in my clouded mind.
The battle for the walls.
You said you were a nurse, and you were trying to stop a witchcraft bomb explosion with your Arts, but it didn't work out too well.
Yes. I remember I was behind Lieutenant Shearer, and the blast threw me off my feet.
Next thing I remember, I was in the field hospital, lying next to the other acute Infected.
I don't know how many witchcraft bombs fell upon the Exemplars.
So many fates, turned on their heads in an instant... but I didn't see any of it, and I don't remember any of it... how could things be like this?
Sarah?
The patient is trembling, and begins to cast Originium Arts without realizing it.
Eliseo does not know if it is a symptom of post-traumatic stress, but he quickly attempts to direct her attention elsewhere.
Listen to me, Sarah. Take a deep breath.
Can you smell the flowers? You're in the sanatorium.
The war is over. The Exemplars are home. You're back in Londinium.
You're...
He pauses and looks up at the damaged lighting above his head.
A strange and unfamiliar city, one that, to his eyes, is not far off from a pile of rubble.
'You're in... a place where everything will get better.'
The alteration of Sarah's Originium Arts is a symptom that has never been observed in other Infected. I should ask my colleagues to keep an eye out.
I wonder if Diane could get me in touch with an international Messenger. Waiting for Maylander's regular Messenger would take too long. Londinium needs supplies, urgently...
...Huh?
Eliseo! You're here too?
—Why didn't you stop her? Weren't you looking for her?
Who?
The Speaker.
The Speaker?! I saw her coming out of an underground pub...
That's where I came from. I happened to run into her when she went to have a drink with old friends.
Sounds like she really considers herself your friend.
Yeah... if only she considered herself more than a friend.
Eliseo... I've always felt that Londinium could become a better place, that Victoria would be proud of her.
Can the Speaker really make that happen, though?
There you are, Captain! Here, take a look at this blueprint. Occ has been saying it's pointless to design a new electrical transmission distribution valve.
But more and more big guys are gonna roll out from our factory to the other plates of Londinium!
I say we ought to make preparations ahead of time.
I don't want to hear people complain when they find that these things don't have enough power and torque—
'Those short-sighted idiots!'
Stop calling me captain. This is a factory; we're not in the Self-Salvation Corps anymore.
Besides, I'm sure Occ has thought about it too. It's just about saving costs where you can.
I know, I know... but it's the young ones coming after us who would have to deal with any problems.
I know how you feel, Martin.
But we have to take things one at a time. Closure shared a bunch of drone technology with us that I would love to put into our new four-legged crawler.
But problems like power supply and automation don't just solve themselves. It's all good to have them in the blueprints, but let's take one step at a time, starting with the present.
Let's do it Occ's way for now and get a basic version out to meet our present needs.
File your blueprint with the Londinium Industry Development Association. Don't lose it.
Its mission is to restore Victoria's technological superiority, with the support of Parliament. New ideas should be respected.
Whatever you say. You're a consultant for them, after all.
There have been so many new ideas lately that it's getting hard to keep up.
People are enthusiastic. They can feel that things are changing because of their ideas.
Yes. Factory production lines, a railway system that links all the districts, and walls and artillery that protect all of Londinium.
We can build them the way we want, with our own two hands.
I could agree with everything else, but please, not the way you want, Feist, or we're all gonna end up in one big amusement park, where the only form of transportation is ziplines.
Come on, that's just the one that drew the most laughs amongst the proposals that got shot down. Not all of my ideas are like that.
By the way, are you sure your factory wants to keep Occ in the core workshop? I heard he didn't get along with you at the old place.
Each factory has its own technical secrets, and there's been friction over competition for orders...
Well, that's true. Not all of our workers were in the Self-Salvation Corps.
But if Catherine could convince the other factories to join the association and share technologies, then there's nothing for us to hide—
Somebody! Help!
Matthew just got pummeled!
Have we met before, sir?
Not exactly, Madam Speaker.
The name's Matthew, a humble businessman who owns a little factory. I simply felt the need to speak up about the present situation.
You can write to Parliament, or seek assistance from the Industry Development Association. I have... other things that require my attention.
I have written to Parliament on behalf of a dozen factories that were shut down. My letter's gone unread for the better part of a month.
It was the decision of Parliament to shut down a number of factories. You know better than I the state of Londinium's logistics.
The burden of one factory's debt can crush hundreds of families in Sudean or Highbury.
We're just asking for the scales to be tipped a little towards us—even if only in a private manner.
Workers from factories that have been shut down could not even afford to buy medicine on the black market. An experienced line worker is worth ten pieces of machinery.
I believe in my workers. There is much that they can still do for the city.
Meanwhile, invaluable Oripathy suppressants are being wasted on dying soldiers on their beds in the Sanatorium.
What are you on about?!
Explaining the realities of economic—
You bloody—
Vina's fist found the man's nose before she knew it.
The man by the name of Matthew grabs his bleeding nose with one hand and searches haplessly for a handkerchief with the other.
Blood continues to seep from the gaps in his fingers, dyeing his clothes red.
More and more onlookers gather, all of them staring at Vina.
There is no hostility or anger in their eyes, but each and every gaze reminds her of her helplessness, of her ill-timing.
I—
I understand why you're angry, Madam Speaker. I know... but someone has to say it.
I assure you it gives me no pleasure.
I was part of the Self-Salvation Corps that stayed in the city. You came with the Cautus girl, and then you left.
When the Exemplars came, and I learned from Catherine that you're their leader, I agreed to turn on the boilers to welcome you without a second thought.
But I didn't recognize you when I saw you standing in the middle of the street, dressed like a king, promising a better life for everyone on behalf of Parliament.
You knew that some promises could never be fulfilled. You chose to sacrifice some people to save others, didn't you?
She wants to shut him up.
She grabs the man by the collar and lifts him off the ground, but she can see his eyes, and the eyes of everyone standing around.
'Siege solves problems with her fists.'
She wishes she could make these problems go away with her fists, the way she used to.
Something falls out of her pocket and makes a clanging sound on the ground. It has been a long time since she had candy. She forgot the box was in her pocket.
We believed in you when we agreed to shut down and relinquish our Originium fuel. But now, we can only hope that we're not the ones being marked for sacrifice.
Not all problems can be solved by your fists... Siege.
Vina!
Let Matthew go. Please.
...Matthew's views don't represent those of all the workers.
Neither do mine, of course.

All I know is that a few friends have reached out to Rhodes Island through me, hoping to improve the state of equipment at the Sanatorium. We can't make new equipment appear out of thin air, but maybe we can make the existing kit more efficient.
Most workers still support the current medical system, I think.
No, you don't have to explain, Feist. I was the one who let the blood get to my head.
You have done as much as you could possibly do.

Improved medical equipment effectiveness, reopening the fast transport tunnels, the central heating system under experimentation... a lot of people have benefited from the changes in a short period of time.
It's all thanks to how you've given us the green light and fended off trouble for us in Parliament.
That's my job. How is Catherine?
Granny?
Well... I doubt she would answer, even if I asked. She won't even let people see her cough.
Otherwise, she's the same. Still busy, even though she's not working at the factory anymore. I rarely get to see her.
I had a conversation with her like this last month. She sat on this very chair, and turned down my invitation for her to become a Member of Parliament.
The secret workshop that you kept hidden from the Sarkaz, and the weapon inside—
Londinium doesn't need it anymore.
Yes, I have reached a consensus with Catherine on this matter.
As long as you and Catherine are able to unite most of the workers, it's not a bad thing for the weapon to stay under your watch.
Parliament needs oversight. So do I.
The system of Parliament is yet immature. We're still searching, and there are people trying to get something done through it.
But sooner or later, they must understand that the Parliament which revolved around the king is long gone.
Was it when the king was—er—
No... nothing changed with Parliament when he was hanged, other than power being transferred from the king to the dukes.
It was only recently that the surviving fellows of the Royal Academy of Sciences were able to resume work on the chronicle of Londinium—
A million words that took half a lifetime to compile and edit, encompassing every aspect of Londinium.
I never thought I would get to know my own home again from this angle.
It was only after reading it that I realized the city's resistance began a long time ago.

'When the Sarkaz gained control of the city, it was the Londiniers who still lived within who had, of their own accord, come together to maintain the city's core functions, irrespective of upbringing and status.'
The Academy's fellows see it as the precursor to the new Parliament.
Seems a little... far-fetched? But it does make sense, in a way.
But I see another side, Feist. During the war, we were able to set aside our differences, our own interests, in our struggle to survive...
Now, though, I'm not sure.
There are times that I can't even guarantee my own reason. I need someone to pull me back when I—
You want it to be us?
That was my promise with Catherine. It was you lot who gave me my sword, after all.
The war destroyed many things. Londinium needs your help in order to not fall behind the changing times.
Some of your ideas may create headaches in Parliament and the Royal Academy of Sciences, but that's not such a bad thing.
Hehe.
A city defense system that belongs only to us, automation that the dukes have long ignored, and a new deterrent for a post-Steam Knight era...
Your help is needed in all of this. And it must be for Londinium, not for any individual.
I don't deserve your trust, Feist, and neither does Parliament in its current state... send Matthew my apologies again, if you see him.
I will continue to lobby Parliament for policies that will improve the condition of workers.
The medicine that we purchased from the dukes is on its way. Things will get better, once supply routes are secured.
Hah, who dares threaten the dukes' cargo ships?
...I'll continue to communicate with the ducal representatives in the city.
We're here for you if you need any help.
Feist stands up, but then hesitates. He does not leave the room.
Granny always scolded me for my fancies.
But she said nothing the day I brought back the proposals for the future from the Industry Development Association.
She retired from the line soon after, claiming health issues.
We like to dream about the future, Vina. We'll pull through.
The office falls quiet. Vina stares at the mountain of papers on her desk, then turns on the radio.
*Bzzt*—
Late afternoon today, citizens witnessed the Speaker of Parliament, Alexandrina Victoria, put a scoundrel in his place.
It was reported that a gentleman had attempted to bribe the Speaker for his own selfish interests, which the Speaker rejected in the most direct manner possible.
Many citizens speak highly of Speaker Victoria's charisma and believe that she—
I guess we've finally learned to trust each other a little bit.
Whether you're confident that this supply route is safe enough for one person to manage... or that most of you have left Victoria, just as you said.
Some sign of trust when you have your little trinket in plain sight.
This is a city full of homeless, desperate people. Who knows if somebody won't get ideas?
Never said you're not part of the threat, of course.
Not that many people in Londinium remember Reunion's past.
Go ahead, check. Everything on the list is here, and I'll deliver the next batch of medicine on time.
You said most of you have left. What about Nine?
Left. Along with many people for whom there is no place in the city.
Our factories outside the city won't be affected, of course. And the routes you found for us will continue to be useful.
You're not going with them? Londinium isn't a great place for Infected anymore.
'Isn't a great place.' Spoken with true Londinier irony.
When I was little, I was fascinated by the Londinium in the storybooks. The palaces and the gardens where the king and the young princess lived, the adventures to be found in the maze-like factories.
Now I find that Londinium is no different from anywhere else in Victoria. It's no place to make a home.
I stay because somebody has to, and I like doing good, honest work. Besides, the treatment of the Infected in Londinium today is... not terrible.
Really?
I followed them because they saved my life. I'm not like those who are ready to die, who've already figured out how to die for something worthwhile.
Besides, in the end, it usually isn't worthwhile. Nobody deserves to die.
You saved a lot of lives. Thank you, on behalf of the workers.
Stop by Highbury the next time you're passing out pamphlets in the city. I'll see right through that disguise.
The goods check out. I'm off, then.
One more thing, Catherine...
Go ahead.
...If the rocks hurt at night, I know a few techniques to make you feel better.
I think you know... our medicine doesn't work as well as the stuff from the big pharmas.
Yes. It may fool the other workers, but it can't fool me. Same for most of the suppressants you can get in Londinium right now.
At my age, though, I don't have too many lucid days ahead of me. And I guess you could say I've seen worse.
One more day with my senses is one more thing I can do. That's plenty.
Our men have tracked down Reunion's hideouts outside the city, two major factories, and their supply routes.
Should we shut them down?
No need. Based on available information, they are not a threat to the citizens of Londinium.
Vina says she has personal reasons to be wary of Reunion.
But even she admits that they were a big help to us when they incited the citizens to oppose the dukes.
As for the gray market... just make sure we keep track of everything that's going on. Percival is not stupid. She knows what she's not supposed to touch.
The Reunion threat is much diminished now that their main members have left Londinium. Continue to monitor the situation.
I will even go so far as to say that establishing contact at a private level is an option, if there is consensus about improving the Infected situation.
Understood. We will continue to monitor Reunion activity without taking direct action.
I suppose I'm wasting you lot on this kind of work.
Of course not, Lady Delphine!
From the day we left the Duke of Windermere's ship, to setting intelligence work aside to focus on the assault of Londinium, and now...
...Do you miss the waters of Lynncardine?
It's hard to believe that you're leaving, Lady Heidi, after all the effort it took to return to this great city.
But perhaps it's time you returned to your father.
Back to Toron? Well... maybe I'll go further than that. My father is not the only guide I have in life.
Your advice has been most enlightening, of course, such as your story of dinner with the envoy of the Earl of March.
It's my pleasure to offer first-hand information to an aspiring novelist who is not involved in the political world.
Or perhaps, I will write my familiar homeland of Toron into the story of this terrible war in Londinium.
Yes, it can be painful to recall those shameful times, but someone must remember the heroic deeds we performed for the city.
Heroic... you're right. Maybe we're all heroes.
How about a love story: a girl from the Victorian borderlands falls in love with a young man who came on holiday, but the young man dies on the battlefields of Londinium.
In the end, though, the news of the young man's death turns out to be false, and he returns to her side a hero.
A brilliant idea, Lady Heidi. A love lost and found again, just like a city trampled by outsiders, but saved by our hands.
Hear, the orchestra plays. There is no wine or dessert, and the candles do not burn as bright, but the music remains beautiful.
By all means, join the dance. Leave me on this balcony with the inspiration you've supplied.
What about your friends here—
Uh...
An emissary from a foreign land, yet unfamiliar with Victorian customs. Surely you will forgive him?
Of course, of course.
Lady Heidi, is that right?
—Ah, I can show you around the place.
N—No, we shouldn't bother you any more than we already have. Thank you for the assist.
This place turns away no one. Next time, you should come in through the front door rather than the balcony, though.
Thank you for the offer, but... I'm not that interested in noble parties, to be honest.
Victorian noble politics was the first thing I studied before I came. I'm familiar with your customs, your attitudes toward the Infected, even your dance steps...
Setting aside whether a third-rate novelist would call herself noble... have you asked this soldier why she brought you here?
Sh—She didn't make me come here. I insisted on coming.
I apologize, Lady Heidi. He said he wanted to stay in Londinium and learn more about our Infected problem, so I was taking him around... in less-than-conventional ways, sometimes.
You can laugh if you want. You see how he is; not at all adept at lying or concealing his thoughts.
Rather fits the Pure Heart name, doesn't it?
Eliseo, representative of Pure Heart Medical?
I didn't expect our reputation to precede us...
It's easy to recognize the people who are interested in the Infected problem.
Less easy to see through the concerted deception of the nobility.
—Like how most of the people stepping onto the dance floor are Infected.
That's to be expected, in theory at least. The Originium particles that the Sarkaz spread don't discriminate according to social status. But...
Nobody will say it out loud. Nobody asks if they're standing next to an Infected. It's about appearances... and a dream.

Eliseo gazes at the hall. There are holes on pillars where golden ornaments once glittered, a testament to the looting during the war, but the passionate playing of the musicians is almost enough to cover up the desolation.
The self-proclaimed novelist opens the cap of her fountain pen and scribbles in a notebook.
You could call it meaningless self-consolation, at a time when basic livelihood is in question.
No, not at all.
Just different ways of living.
What is that?
The shadow of a synopsis... and a little extra information. I would like you to take it.
I have a feeling we will meet again, Eliseo.
Thank you.
If you don't mind me asking, though—you seem to be holding onto some pain that this banquet can't soothe.
I'm just missing a friend of mine.
There's one less person who will speak with me frankly, now that she's gone.
The Summit of Nations?
The Lateran is paying close attention to the events in Victoria. After all, this is a disaster caused by the Sarkaz.
However, the decisiveness that you and Parliament have shown on the Sarkaz question gave me hope for the city's revival.
Of course, His Holiness sincerely wishes to build the bridge of peace for the nations of Terra, in order to prevent another tragedy from happening.
You're not only here on behalf of His Holiness the Lateran Pope, are you?
I am but a humble Messenger, here to deliver a message to you and Parliament.
Then please send my apologies to His Holiness. I appreciate the kindness that Laterano is demonstrating, but Parliament is not in a position to address matters beyond Londinium.
Kindly inform the duke currently playing host to you that I am not going to Laterano in any capacity. As for his own plans, those have never been any business of Parliament nor myself, have they?
Of course. That's what Messengers are for.
Oren Argiolas. He was on Duke Gododdin's battleship during the war, but never participated directly on behalf of Laterano.
Just as I thought. Does Gododdin have a sweet tooth too?
If so, we don't have much for him here in Londinium.
So much for Parliament's day off.
One interesting tidbit. The Lateran Messenger went onto the ship with a group of refugees. He made an oath by placing his hand on his gun, in order to convince the Duke to accept the refugees.
And?
The scorching hot barrel gave him a blister on his hand.
Hah, that's out of nowhere. Is it important?
Day off.
Alright, I've been off. Now, which message should I read first? Investigation report on the Arts Circle remnants?
Oh?
Vina sees Lieutenant Shearer's name. Another letter.
She sighs and drops it in the 'Pending' bin.
Delphine, is this—
Hey, Vina! We caught those robbers you were on about and taught 'em a lesson!
Targeting frail Oripathy patients. Scum.
You know what's even worse? We got a bunch of cowardly wankers who wanted to join the ranks.
It's a good job you got the news quick and didn't give them a chance to grow.
I wish I could go out and move about.
Don't worry, I gave 'em an extra punch for you.
And I saved your share of the thank-you gift.
Wait, we're not collecting protection money—
When was the last time you had candy since we got back to Victoria?
Indra...
The lad who got robbed said he made 'em himself. A risk with every mouthful! It's not like you ever liked normal candy sticks.
He said he couldn't get his hands on regular sweeteners, so he's tried potatoes, carrots, Originium slug fluids...
I tried all of 'em. Didn't throw up or nuffin.
Sounds like a real pick-me-up. Can I try one?
Sure! There's plenty.
Are you okay, Vina?
Fine. I've had worse.
What about your Speaker duties?
Yeah, what are you worried about right now? New laws? Announcements? Reports?
Any work you need us to do? Especially the ones that involve punching which you can't do yourself.
I even took on a new lackey today! He's built like a straw, but I can teach him a few moves to defend himself.
It's time for Glasgow to rebuild. When can we go back—
Indra scratches her head. She did not need her friends' reproaching glances to realize she should not have asked.
Vina glances across her desk, where the mountain of papers has not grown any thinner.
Delphine walks to her side and takes the letters that she was holding.
I think it's time to give a public answer to the issues that concern our citizens. Most don't come to listen to Parliamentary debate, but they'll listen to the radio at the end of a long day.
