The situation in the city is stabilizing. I've requested a third engineering team for Norport. Soon, our home will be back to the way it was.
BETTER than the way it was.
You can go back to Lynncardine, Delphine, once we've taken care of this business.
Diane?
Lieutenant?!
I'm retired... Sarah.
Oh, okay. Sir.
I thought you had declined the Sanatorium's invitation.
Just coming to visit, and to thank you again... I hope you don't mind.
I don't remember much, to be honest. The doctors say the rocks have gone into my head—your hands are trembling.
No need to hide it from me, sir. I haven't forgotten what you taught me.
Looks like addiction symptoms. From the war?
Excessive painkiller use. After that thing at the walls.
You must stay. I'll go for a doctor. Maybe they can...
At least find you some—
More painkillers? Yeah, maybe that would soothe the pain.
That's why you're here, isn't it? Supplies are short, but maybe they can spare some.
Lady Delphine has been very good to the Sanatorium, and to us.
Just a moment. I'll be right back.
He wants to stay. Maybe he could really get some painkillers, even if there is little enough to spare.
Phew—
Oh, where did he go?
Hey, watch where you're going!
Whoa, keep your distance. Something's not right with him.
The Speaker's office is... this way... argh...
He just toppled over! Another of those poor Infected sods?
Stay away from him! We're all goners if he shatters...
I'm... not... argh... my body... I can't...
I'll go get someone! Keep your distance, everyone!
Shearer collapses on the street. The rays of the setting sun are not particularly bright, but they are enough to hurt his eyes.
Or, perhaps, it is his body's way of reminding him that only another deep-seated desire can mask the pain.
He tries to adjust his breathing, like he instructed the soldiers fighting pain during the war...
But he fails. He has already lost the fight.
Shearer sees fire. It is so hot he cannot open his eyes. The sky burns. The walls burn. He burns—
He sees the day when he lay on the ground beneath the walls.
The walls of Londinium burned with Draco fire, burning brighter and brighter, until the flames consumed the sky.
He cries out in fear, and tries to cover his eyes—
...Lieutenant...
Lieutenant... Shearer... wake... up...
He sees a hand reach out towards him.
...Lady Delphine?
I have been waiting for you, Lieutenant.
I saw the letter you wrote to Vina... but you avoided me, just like you refused my offer of treatment.
She never read my letter... did she, Lady Delphine?
I can guess what you wrote.
I'm sorry, but I can't help you here. We both know the state of medicine availability in Londinium. You can't stay here.
Where can I go?
Lynncardine.
No! I...
I wasn't asking, Lieutenant. This is an order. And don't give me that excuse about being retired.
I know why you hesitate. I know what you fear.
You're afraid to face the senior officers who betrayed and abandoned you at Irnclad Galavae...
You're afraid that you won't come good on your loyalty to me and my mother, and you're afraid to return home like this. Aren't you?
I... I don't know...
You're not as strong as you think, Lieutenant. And I'm not as weak, or noble, as you think.
Our home is Lynncardine. Not here.
The war is over, Lieutenant. This soldier should go home.
Don't be afraid. I'm going home with you.
Let's go.
(Sob) But how... how do I make it home, when I'm like this...
I'll take care of it. Did you really think I would abandon you just because you didn't come looking for me?
Thank you... Lady Delphine...
Will you return to Londinium?
Yes. I promised Vina. But she may have to wait.
I'll make arrangements for you to get treatment. We'll depart once I've taken care of what I have in front of me.
Yes, my Lady.
Lynncardine... I'm coming home, Mother.

lines.loc: Knights of the Tower Watchtower BackyardDecember 22nd, 1098, 5:32 P.M.

lines.x: I thought you'd have gotten everyone to help fix up the guts of this tower by now.
And I thought you'd be getting ready for the Speaker's speech, Vina. I'd rather not meddle with the designers while they work anyway.
They know how to build a museum better than I do, and I'm looking forward to what little surprises the 'Knights of the Tower Museum' they're making has in store for me.
But carving a monument commemorating the Knights? That's something I've got to do myself.
Here, have a look. How is it?
Never took you for a sculptor.
Master Mitchell taught me how when I was really young. Though he'd always get so drunk he'd forget to teach me his special techniques.
Come on over and I'll introduce you. This is Master Finn, the last Captain of the Watch, and the elder knight I was closest to.
This is Old Ferguson, the one who taught me to drink. This is Master Bailey, who did her best to teach me how to do makeup.
And here's Master Mitchell—not my carving, though. He made it himself after a night at the pub.
This is Carl, the crybaby. This is Martin. Every week he'd sneak off before getting sent back by his family. This is Karen...
And then there's... There's...
Vina gently hugs Dagda, comforting her.
Thanks, Vina, but I'm fine.
It's just that every time I come back here, those fading memories of mine become a lot clearer.
You carved your own name too. 'Isabelle Montague'...
The Knights of the Tower never left. We're still here. Master Finn would be glad to hear it.
You know, Parliament could retain the institution of the Knights, even if you—
Our duty is already over, no?
Victoria has no need for a monarch, so naturally she has no need for the Knights. Let the past be remembered in a museum. That's the best ending we could ask for.
Since there's no more need to light up the tower for the palace's sake, my own duty's done as well.
These days, Londinium is no longer home to Isabelle, the Knight of the Tower. There's just Dagda now. Dagda of the Glasgow Gang.
But Vina, if you still have need of the Knights, I won't hesitate. Just say the word.
Oh, don't tease me, Dagda. You heard those rumors too?
We've been looking into who's been spreading them. But it seems there's a lot of people who believe, even wholeheartedly support the idea that—
'Vina Victoria will be crowned King.'
Eliseo! Eliseo! Please wait!
Oh, Jenna. More questions you'd like to ask me?
Miss Molly wants to invite you to stay and watch us rehearse our new play—
Also, Eliseo, could you help me prove something?
What is it?
Jamie and I had a wager. That even if my gran and I moved to Columbia, we wouldn't be much better off than we are now.
His dad said that in Columbia, kids like us wouldn't get to go to school, and there wouldn't be any teachers who'd want to teach us how to do plays...
Jamie said everything you told us in class was a lie.
Jenna, you should be up on stage for the rehearsal. Don't be bothering Eliseo now.
Ah! M—Miss Molly!
I was just inviting him to stay and watch...
Allow me. Jamie's waiting for you on stage. He's about to cry any moment now.
Without you, he can't say any of his lines. Go on.
Oh, he's so annoying... Alright, I'll go...
You've got to stay and watch, Eliseo!
*sigh* Do forgive the children, Eliseo. They tend to just speak their mind.
I must admit, I'm not too familiar with Columbia, and I'm not entirely sure how Infected children would be treated—
They'd be treated very well!
...So long as their guardians can afford health insurance.
I see.
The children are very fond of you, and I'm grateful that you took the time to explain how they should take care of themselves after infection. It's important for them to know.
Thank you, truly.
I have to give the same lectures to kids back home pretty often, so it's no trouble at all. Honestly, I'd like to thank you for letting me teach them.
I thought since Parliament's on bad terms with Pure Heart Medical, the schools wouldn't let me near the children.
Parliament's never forbidden us from reaching out to you though? On the contrary, they tend to approve of this sort of thing as long as we're quiet about it, up to a certain degree.
Not just you, either. We've also invited Rhodes Island's doctors over to give the children checkups and lessons.
Parliament knows that children will need assistance.
But there's only so much that checkups and lessons can do for them. What they need is medicine and treatment.
I know. But all we can do now is stay by their side and make sure they are not overcome by fear.
At least at this school, they can still live like how they used to... Like regular people.
I really admire you, Molly.
Back home, if a young Infected is as energetic as these kids here, it usually means their family is pretty well-off.
But here... I hear some kids had nowhere to go, so you gave them shelter at the school. You've got them putting on plays, ones that bring hope to the people here.
That's amazing.
You flatter me, Eliseo.
I am merely following in the footsteps of those teachers who can no longer return to school... Especially the lady I respect the most.
Do you mean Lady Golding? I heard Heidi mention her before.
Yes. The play we are rehearsing today was the last she ever wrote in this classroom.
It's a lovely little tale. Two young children go on many adventures to find a fowlbeast that can chase away any illness, which saves their sickly friend.
Lady Heidi once asked me if Lady Golding ever called it a 'meaningless consolation' when she was writing it...
But why?
Is it alright if I stay and watch them rehearse, Molly?
For the sake of the children, and so I can show how much I respect you, and your lady.
I have a feeling that maybe here, in Londinium, there's still a lot I can do to help.
...For Lady Golding.
...For Lady Golding.
Are all the MPs present?
They are.
How much longer until the city-wide broadcast?
Two minutes.

lines.st: It was an evening close to New Year's.

After several long years living in fear, most folk could finally feel at ease and enjoy dinner with their families, listening to the Speaker of Parliament's broadcast together. They even started calling it the 'Dinnertime Declaration' in jest.
Pity that dinner didn't taste so good.

lines.x: Testing, testing, *cough*—
Shall I begin?
Go ahead.
This is Vina Victoria. As Speaker of Londinium's Parliament, I now address all the city's residents who took part in resistance during the war, and who yet live.
Two months ago, we walked out from the midst of tragedy.
You lot shut up! Can't you tell the Speaker's started her speech already?
On behalf of Parliament, allow me to convey our respect to those heroes who contributed to the war effort.
Every citizen, every soldier of the ducal alliance, every fighter of the Exemplars.
The city will remember each and every one of your deeds, and honor all those who are no longer with us.
The pub falls silent as everyone lifts their cups.
To you, my brothers... and to me.
Now, Londinium has begun her long road to recovery. Order will be reestablished, and those lives once so familiar to us will return.

But that's not all. In regards to the Exemplars, those glorious fighters of extraordinary circumstance, Parliament has decided to reserve this unit designation forevermore, so that we may commemorate both the past and the present of these troops.
I can hear the children singing, and the retired soldiers singing alongside them. Seems like they're in a good mood.
The song they're singing is from the end of the play.
The sickly child finally recovers, and together, they find happiness.
I... expected those homeless soldiers to care a lot more about the Speaker's speech.
Of course they care, but they are of firm belief that the Speaker will not abandon them.
After all the suffering endured, happiness will surely come, Eliseo. Do you believe so?
The Exemplars came together once more for one simple dream: to go home. No matter their status, no matter their standing.
Now, this dream of theirs has become reality. We are home now. We have reached the end of the road.
Hah, 'reached the end of the road'.
Seems this Speaker lady really is set on disbanding them. What an idiot, pulling apart your army of mates—
Then what do you propose she should do with such a motley crew?
Keep chasing those Sarkaz for the kill? Or set their sights on the dukes?
Uh...
We've got better things to worry about than the Exemplars.
Such as replenishing our strength as quickly as we can. Focus on that instead.
Many former Exemplars have joined the Defense Force's ranks, continuing to protect our homeland.
As for those who no longer wish to bear arms, they have the right to enjoy peace alongside their families.

Parliament promises each and every one of our Exemplar comrades a place to settle down. It may take a long time for this to come to fruition, but the door to the Speaker's office is forever open to my friends—
I'm afraid it'll take a few years to see this through. You've got a long road ahead of you, Vina.
Coming along for dinner, Catherine? There's a fair few old retired blokes who've reported back to the factory.
They'll be real happy to see you!
Sure you're not just angling for my stash of smokes? I worked real hard to collect them.
Hehe.
Fine, fine, I'll come along. Oh, right. While you're at it, tell everyone to bring a few friends along.
Once Parliament helps us restart more factories, the hands we have now won't be enough.
And I, Vina Victoria, do promise to every one of this city's residents, that in this coming future without a king—
The broadcast abruptly comes to an end. As it does so, Dagda hears an uproar suddenly echo through the skies above the city.
Why the cut-off? What's... What's going on at Parliament?
Madam Speaker, Madam Delphine, while I was fixing up the broadcast system, I noticed this old component.
It's ancient scrap, should've been replaced when His Majesty Alistair was on the throne.
Who would've thought an unremarkable thing like this could still hold up until now! My mentor never told me anything about this!
So it's all just an accident?
Why was there suddenly something on your maintenance checklist that wasn't there before?
Iؙ—I just followed what the system synced up to me—
Ladies and gentlemen, may I sit in on this round of parliamentary discussion?
Earl of March.
Eleanor. My name's not hard to remember, Vina.
You seem rather surprised to see me here. What, did no one tell you?
It seems your informants have been lax of late, Lady Delphine.
Londinium has no need for... 'informants' to host a guest, my Lady. The city welcomes every Victorian who loves this land.
Lovely to hear.
Then I suppose you won't mind if I invite a few dukes to revisit an old haunt for a little reunion.

Judging by the time, they should be arriving any moment now. Considering their sensitive position, the dukes are hesitant to drop in on any specific person's home, so I was forced to borrow one of your places.
I hope Parliament will understand. You're also welcome to join in, Vina. And you too of course, Delphine.
I know you are about to leave for Windermere. There are a few old friends who hope you could take their condolences for your mother along with you.
Eleanor doffs her hat, greeting Delphine with a smile.
She then immediately walks to Vina's side and lowers her voice.
I loved your speech, a very sensible decision. Yet...
Far, far too impatient. There are some things you are free to say to us, but not to the common public.
This is for their own good, and yours too.
So you were the one who cut our signal.
We'll be assembling at the Royal Academy of Sciences, Vina. It won't take too long, so don't be late.
By the by, sometimes, there's no need for you to think you haven't done enough. The changes this city has seen are far beyond what I imagined.

lines.loc: Royal Victorian Academy of Sciences Reception RoomDecember 22nd, 1098, 7:32 P.M.

lines.x: I assumed Your Grace would have less of an appetite.
I've always been a practical lad, Eleanor.
We all know the situation in Londinium. This light wine and bread is already enough to show us the Speaker's good faith.
The Duke of Wellington is absent, yet again.
*sigh* It seems the alliance between yourself and His Grace is not as reliable as we imagined.
We all know we're here for more than just pleasantries, Your Grace. Old Wellington would never have come in the first place.
How do you believe we should treat with Duke of Wellington, Vina?
If it has naught to do with Londinium, it has naught to do with me.
Oh, really?
Would he really care what you all think? He's always been one to seize the initiative.
All I know is that since the battle for the walls ended two months ago, the engines of the Gastrell and the other frigates have not rested for even a moment.
Wellington has been waiting for you to bite, and I'm afraid to say that he's very disappointed in you all.
Quite right.
But what you see is merely one side of the matter, Delphine Windermere.
In Old Wellington's eyes, it is still far far too early to decide what sort of relationship will exist between Tara and Victoria.
In these two months, we have had a... tacit understanding. So long as nobody moves to break the silence, then silence we shall enjoy.
But really, it's Her Grace over here whose emissaries are always sneaking into Wellington's demesne. She's much better qualified than I to speak on the matter, no?
Once I return, I will be investigating who has been going against my orders.
After you return to Windermere, young Delphine, I would happily welcome some tired guests at the Lynncardine winery, whenever you like—
Could we dispense with the banalities already?

Londinium may welcome all Victorians, but we have no time to waste chatting with each and every one of them. There are still many issues affecting our citizens that need urgent handling, all more important than the people sitting here.
I am, of course, well aware that your sudden visit to Londinium is also thanks to an... ahem, tacit understanding.
I know that Wellington's only an excuse. Parliament will not interfere in this matter. So if what you're going to discuss next doesn't involve Parliament, then I don't need to waste my time here—
That's where you're wrong, Vina. The Duke of Wellington's choice really does have something to do with you. Or to be more precise, it's to do with the late king. Your late father.
Twenty-six years ago, Old Wellington never cast a vote in the matter of how to handle your father. He simply watched in silence as King Alistair was led from the throne to the gallows...
Thereafter, he was disappointed. Disappointed in Victoria. Disappointed in the dukes. Disappointed in your father.
From that moment on, the tug of Tara's undercurrent has never ceased.
Duke Windermere never mentioned any of this to me—
Your mother didn't care. No one regretted executing the King. Sorry, Vina, you know I don't mean anything untoward.
Just because Old Wellington himself will never come to Londinium doesn't mean that he has no interest in the city, or in you.
For the time being, we still do not know his true intentions. But just because the throne has sat idle for many years does not mean Victoria needs no king.
Vina does not understand where Eleanor is going with this. She already made clear her refusal long ago.
The kingship, the crown. They were never what she needed—
Bloody hell, Vina, patrol's just come by your office to tell you—
The crown's been stolen from the throne room!
Pardon?
It seems our meeting must be postponed, Speaker Victoria. There are side matters that require your attention.
If you need help, Vina, you know where to find me.
Chances are we'll be troubling you a little longer, Madam Speaker.
You sodding—

lines.st: I don't like the throne room in the Palace of Westhaleg. It's so empty and bleak.
That lonely throne just sitting there—I can't imagine what it'd be like if Vina actually sat on it.
One woman, on that cold, lifeless chair, staring at the barren steps...
Knowing Vina, she wouldn't last a minute there—I'd bet on it.

lines.loc: Throne Room, Palace of WesthalegDecember 23, 1098, 3:26 A.M.

lines.x: Did you find the crown? Where is it?
We didn't find it, per se. Someone delivered it to the Speaker's office. We can't determine the precise time it was lost, but we're fairly certain it happened after your speech started.
Vina already placed the crown on that empty throne... so what's the point of stealing it? Was it an order from the dukes?
I don't think so.
The dukes are never of one mind—they each have their own agenda. However, for the crown to go missing at the same time they're visiting Londinium? That's far too much of a coincidence.
But, according to what you've told us, isn't that crown just an old, rusted hunk of iron now?
....Umm, not exactly, Morgan.
To many people, the crown is extraordinarily important, no matter its current state.
It's been left untouched for over twenty years, but the people have not forgotten about it. In fact, they are more eager than ever for its return.
They're waiting for a wise new monarch to put it on his/her head, someone who can dispel the shadows hanging over Victoria.
No, most people are just desperate for a miracle—something that can satisfy their craving for royal authority.
I cannot deny that. Plus, you have listened to their voices for ages, Vina.
Your identity is truly unique: a descendant of the royal family and a bona fide war hero who was nonetheless raised on the streets. It's no wonder so many people naturally feel drawn to you.
Now that Parliament is up and running and the dukes have willingly relinquished their authority, the people are all the more convinced that you've secured universal support.
The next step is plain as day.
But the dukes have no intentions of doing anything like that!
The public only cares about the outcome, Morgan.
Yes… I've understood that since the day we returned home. Unfortunately, sometimes, I'm still very good at letting people down.
Thank you for sharing the energy bars, Eliseo. I'll gladly undergo daily sampling, if I get tasty treats like this every time. Haha.
Are these really things you can just buy off the shelf in Columbia? Why do you Columbians stock up on stuff like this—is it for Catastrophe or something?
These bars are my own personal thing.
'Food is fuel.' It's about energy and nutrition. Energy bars are convenient, so why not?
Then again, Diane definitely disagrees with me on this. She's more of a foodie.
I understand. We're thankful just to fill our bellies in Londinium these days.
Her Royal Highness Alexandrina has worked very hard to ensure that we have enough food to eat.
Quit yapping his ear off, will you?
Thank you, Eliseo, for these...uh...'energy bars,' but we'd best be on our way now.
Stanley, you're not afraid that Eliseo will...?
Of course not!
I... surely I can't be arguing with you about such stupid things in front of someone else, right? It's not proper.
Huh?
*sigh* He doesn't like when I refer to Alexandrina as 'Her Royal Highness', That's all.
He and I don't see eye to eye politically. I think having a king again wouldn't be half bad, but he insists life was worse for us when the monarchy was still in power.
But think about it, whether it was back then, or right now, aren't we managing to get by all the same?
So you're saying the presence of a monarch doesn't really impact your life all that much?
It doesn't matter what she thinks. Even if someone does ascend the throne again, it's not like we'll be invited to the coronation feast.
*sigh* Stanley, when are you going to come up with an argument that actually convinces me?
Sorry about this, Eliseo. I need to talk some sense into this stubborn mule.
Oh, by the way, about the increased patrols in the city—I've heard a rumor. Supposedly, someone stole the crown, and the Defense Forces are investigating anyone who might be connected with the theft.
Let's go, Stanley. Goodbye, Eliseo.
Come to think of it, Diane has never mentioned the king or the crown, not even once. Maybe I should ask her about it when I get back.
Eliseo, you should take a break. You've been working nearly nonstop, helping collect patient data.
The city's Infected all experienced acute infection around the same time, with many showing rare pathological progressions. I need to document this valuable data thoroughly.
Whether it's for future research into Oripathy or for developing targeted treatments for Londinium's citizens, this data is critically important.
By the way, Sarah, why didn't Diane visit you today?
Diane?

I thought she was helping you. Many of my old comrades came by the sanatorium today. They wanted to ask if anything 'unusual' had come up recently. We were hoping to bring Diane along for a little reunion with them.

lines.st: Throughout the whole war, Vina never told us where she found that crown.
But it didn't matter. None of us cared, and no one would ever see that hunk of iron again.
It was supposed to remain sealed in the throne room in the Palace of Westhaleg, left to corrode and decay into nothing.
'The Age of Kings is over and done.'
That's what a drunken Vina said to me, with her arm slung around my neck.
And yet, barely two months later, here it is again, staring us in the face—The ghost of this ancient empire refuses to let go. It's always ready to claim its next victim.

lines.x: Does anyone else know about this?
We've taken steps to contain the information and will do everything we can to minimize its impact.
So... what do we do with this thing?
Morgan points at the battered crown sitting on the desk.
This is my first time seeing it up close. Can't believe so many people are fretting over this old thing...
Vina... Vina?
She notices that Vina isn't looking at the crown at all. Instead, she's standing silently by the desk, her gaze fixed on the dejected figure sitting in the chair across from her—
'The one who stole the crown'.
You really have no questions for me, Madam Speaker?
I reckon many of you have fanciful notions about me.
Besides, my colleagues will soon have a more complete and objective report delivered to my desk, regarding this matter. So—
Don't lump me in with those people! All they care about is the throne itself. I care about the one on the throne.

I know exactly what I'm doing. I'm well aware of every new law Parliament has enacted, Speaker Vina. Unauthorized entry into the throne chamber is indeed a serious crime, and I'm not planning to deny anything.
Vina shakes her head.
But that doesn't mean you're fundamentally different from those people. The provisional court will try your case according to the law.
Do you have any further objections?
No, Madam Speaker.
Delphine, I'll leave the rest to you. I'll personally return the crown to the throne room and make sure security is tightened up.
*sigh* And then I need to prepare the report for the council meeting—
Heh... a report.
You didn't have to do all this, Siege. You know all too well how inefficient Parliament is—it's just a group of individuals all looking out for their own self-interest.
You must already be exhausted, just from dealing with all those MPs, no?
Londinium has no need for a Speaker operating at the whims of Parliament. What Londinium needs is a real, absolute leader who can guide the city through its transformation.
Vina, there's no point in arguing with her about this meaningless stuff.
I know, Delphine. Madam, are you suggesting that Londinium needs a dictator?
No. I'm talking about a king. Victoria needs a king.
I can guess what you want to do, Madam Speaker. You're unlike any ruler I've ever known.
You don't even care about your own lineage. But if you wish to achieve your vision, 'Speaker', it won't be enough. This crown should not, and will not, become an obstacle to you.
She stands up, giving Vina a crisp salute.
That is all I have to say on this matter.
I give my thanks to The Exemplars, and The Siege, for all you've done for our homeland.
I'm fond of the changes she's undergone and firmly believe she'll become an even better leader in future.
It's just a shame that her transformation is still so slow... I won't live to see that day myself.
Let's go, Lady Delphine. Where should we head next?

lines.st: 'Do you really know what I wish to do?'
'You want to change Victoria...'
'No. You want to defeat her. You want to defeat the Victoria of the past.'

lines.x: Vina stands at the foot of the staircase, holding the crown in her hands as she gazes at the throne.
Beside the throne, a golden lion lays sprawled, his eyelids drooping halfway.
Gawain, you should've told me sooner.
Step by step, she ascends the staircase until she reaches the throne. Instead of sitting on it, she sits on the steps before it.
The lion doesn't seem to open his eyes. His gentle breath brushes through its mane, sending up faint specks of dust.
He is displeased.
I'm sorry, Gawain. I had another sleepless night. There are still so many thorny issues left unresolved...
She feels the warmth at her back. The golden lion has moved closer, and his massive body is now supporting her.
Just like it had when she was a child. Vina catches a whiff of sunlight.
Gawain, how nice it would be to fall asleep leaning against you like this.
Walking all the way back here from outside the city feels like... a dream...
But it's been so long since I've had a good night's sleep, let alone a deep, satisfying dream.
The lion lifts his head slightly, brushing against Vina's golden hair, tickling her just enough to make her smile faintly.
The chill in the air sends a shiver through her, jolting her back to clarity.
...Has it always been this cold here? It's not time to rest yet, is it?
What we're doing now still falls far short. There's not enough medicine, not enough food, and endless arguments—
Sometimes I wish I could just rip off this uniform and knock those idiots down one by one. Maybe things would be much simpler that way, huh?
'Defeat the Victoria of the past'...
If only it were as simple as a match in the ring.
The golden lion closes his eyes, drifting off to sleep, though his tail is still lazily swatting against Vina's arm.
Teacher, I'm sorry for complaining to you again.
There are some things I don't know how to explain to Morgan and the others. They wouldn't understand, and I don't want to make them worry.
As for Delphine... I don't want to show too much doubt or vulnerability in front of her. She's already taken on so much for me and pinned so many expectations on me.
There are already so many people counting on me, Gawain.
I don't want to let them down.
She shuts her eyes and grips the crown.
'King', 'Speaker', these titles mean nothing to her.
She just wants—

Vina, I knew I'd find you here!
Wait… were you just asleep, or?
No, I was just thinking about something. Gawain, he—
She instinctively turns her head to the side, but there's no one there. Yet that warmth remains, unmistakable.
He's gone.
(...Thank you for being here with me, Gawain.)
Morgan, what are you doing here?
Delphine convinced the provisional court to hold the crown thief in the Defense Forces' detention quarters, for now.
The full report on her is out now. I didn't want to waste time, so I came straight to you. Here, this is the report.
Her name is Diane Weber.
Morgan pauses, watching Vina closely for a reaction. But Vina simply looks at her with curiosity, waiting for her to continue.
She didn't just decide to steal the crown out of the blue.
She has been working with a pharmaceutical rep from Columbia for a while now, helping spread awareness about Oripathy infection protection methods.
That's how she learned when the guards patrolling outside the Palace of Westhaleg would change shifts. Once she got inside, she waited until your speech and slipped into the throne room.
And then, she stayed there for quite a while—at least half an hour.
What did she do in there?
I don't know, and she won't say. Maybe she was hesitating, or maybe she just got lost in thought. In any case, she managed to make off with the crown without alerting anyone.
Wait, something doesn't add up here. She stayed in the throne chamber for so long. She didn't run into the patrols after the shift change?
No, she didn't. Even she was surprised by how easily she escaped. She thought for sure the patrols would catch her.
So someone must have helped her. Was it one of the guests?
Unclear, but if she had help, she didn't know about it. She came up with this whole plan herself.
But... why? Just to bring the crown to me and goad me into—
Her name is Diane Weber.
I know—
You don't remember her. I kept thinking she looked familiar, and when I saw the report, I realized—it's her.
Remember that day when the alliance surrounded us on our doorstep? The first soldier to defy orders and escort us forward?
Vina recalled the sky that day, filled with steam, the lingering smell of smoke and gunpowder.
And the soldier whose face had become a blur in her memory.

lines.st: 'This is Lieutenant Diane Weber of the Sixth Column, Tenth Infantry Regiment. Lower your weapons, soldiers, and escort the Exemplars in their advance.'
'Before I was a soldier, I was a Londinier. This is my home too.'
'Go, Siege. The fight is not yet over.'
'We will take back what is ours!'

lines.x: Diane Weber, the soldier who defied the Duke of Caster's orders... She contracted an acute infection in the final battle against the Nachzehrer King.
That rings a bell? She was the one who took the Sighs of Kings from you and charged towards the Nachzehrers.
She doesn't have much time left.
We are short of Oripathy suppressants, and she never got enough medicine to slow her condition. We took a look at her, but—
The crown lets out a faint metallic creak as Vina's hand tightens around it. It looks so fragile, yet its resilience is undeniable.
She really loved our home, and she loved Victoria as well.
That's why she volunteered to join the ducal forces, only to defy their orders and end up forced into retirement.
She trusted you, even though I don't agree with some of her ideas. Vina, maybe we could...

lines.st: I never finished asking for mercy on Diane's behalf. Vina knew what I wanted to say, and I knew how she'd respond...
No, I knew what Speaker Vina Victoria would say.
Even now, I can't imagine what must've been going through Diane Weber's head when she made that crazy decision, but I admire her.
...Even though she stole the crown, she did it out of love for Victoria.
I don't understand. Since coming back to the city, so many things have changed—changed in ways I can't fathom and don't want to understand.
And on that day, I finally made up my mind—
From the end of that final battle outside the Palace of Westhaleg until now... I've lingered here far too long.
Maybe it's time to go home, back to the boxing gym.

lines.loc: Auchterigg Front Lines, T1October 2nd, 1098, 6:23 A.M. - The Day the War Ended

lines.x: Death... awaits... at the end...
My liege... watches... death, watches... you...
Captain, the target has been neutralized! But our remaining ammunition is estimated to last only two more minutes!
We need our next set of orders!
All units, prepare for close-quarter combat!
Hold the line for the next two minutes—I'll cover your retreat!
Siege, our artillery support is running out!
The bombardment will end in three minutes! What's the situation on your side?
We can't clear this Nachzehrer fog!
These things have severed the supply routes our workers cleared for us. We can't get supplies to your position quickly enough.
Prepare to retreat from T1.
Negative! Abandoning T1 will create a gap in our encirclement, and the alliance forces will break through from our side! Captain, I can't accept that—
The battlefield isn't a place for heroics! Honor your orders!
We've sent a distress signal to our friends at Rhodes Island. Now we have to trust the Doctor and the plan.
Soldiers, we've made it home. Do everything you can to survive! Only then can we take back what we've lost!
Siege, we'll hold the line for the final two minutes, then retreat to regroup and assist you in clearing out the creatures in the fog!
Bagpipe, keep them safe!
Aye, Captain!
If the fog can't be dispersed, don't wait for us. Head to I12 and rendezvous with Gododdin's 106th Assault Brigade. Keep fighting.
Understood.
I'll be seein' ye soon, Captain—
(Strange howl)
Look out, Siege!
It's the Confessarius chimeras! Everyone, prepare to retreat!
(Song-like shriek)
It's lit itself up! Hannah, watch out for its flames!
Oof... It hurts... Vina, are you alright?
Everyone else, fall back! I can't allow any more needless sacrifices...
(A piercing wail)
Wait—where did this backup come from!?
Anti-witchcraft cannon has struck the target. Enemy movement disrupted. Remaining squads, continue setting up anti-witchcraft cannons.
Mission objective: eliminate threats within the fog.
Friendly forces at T1, hold your position. Emergency supplies will arrive at your location in three and a half minutes.
Exemplar T1 Combat Team—message received! Please identify your unit!
Duke of Normandy's 8th Grenadier Battalion, under orders to support The Exemplars.
The Duke of Normandy? 8th Grenadier Battalion? Horn, do you know anything about this unit?
They're not under the direct command of the Duke of Normandy. They report to the Earl of March.
Lieutenant Skamandros truly has an exceptional grasp of our intelligence operations, as to be expected from someone born into a military family.
Lieutenant, keep Her Royal Highness Alexandrina safe.
As for Your Highness, please accept this small gift.
Once all these matters are resolved, I'll visit you in person, Vina.
Siege, do you know the Earl of March?
No, we've never met. But her name was mentioned in some of the information sent to me.
That doesn't matter now. With reinforcements, we should be able to reopen the supply line. Horn, we'll meet again soon!
At last, it's time to bring all of this to an end.
Finally!
The crowd cheers.
Phew—
She gazes at The Shard in the distance and the faintly visible Palace of Westhaleg, calmer than she has ever been.
Triangle, Cello, Oboe... Tempest Platoon, everyone, watch closely.
...As we scatter the dark clouds over Londinium!
All members of the Exemplar T1 Combat Team, hear this: hold your ground. Do not give them a single inch!
'Roger that!'
Captain, Rock-18 just uploaded the scan of the last segment of our route to PRTS. Did you receive it?
Got it! Rockrock, we're already repairing the final track-shift node.
Given the current damage level, how long until the repairs are complete?
Ten minutes.
Located Granny yet? We need to retrieve that weapon as soon as possible. The Exemplars will need our support on the front line.
Still trying to pinpoint the correct location.
The signals we've found over the past few days were all decoy beacons they left behind. It might take a bit more time to re-establish contact.
At least that means Granny's team is still safe for now...
Captain, if—and I mean if—we can't get in touch with Catherine, what should we do?
We'll use this passage to transport as much of Highbury's remaining supplies as possible to—
Captain?!
*cough*—
*cough* Is everyone okay?
Where did those Sarkaz come from... and what was that artillery fire I just heard?
The smoke shifts, yet a small ember glimmers brightly in the depths of the haze...
The ember of a cigarette.
A familiar, acrid smell hits Feist's nose and makes his eyes sting.
I keep telling you, when you're busy working, you always forget to watch your back.
Welcome home, brat.
Granny! We've been looking for you all this time!
I know. When someone told me the passage was being repaired, I figured it was you.
Hm?
That's—
He spots a massive shadow in the depths of the smoke.
Haha, remember the blueprints you left behind?
It took some effort, with the Sarkaz blockade and all, but we finally managed to build your big machine.
The Crawler...
I know you're there.
Your earring has grown... rather warm.
Those workers who came to support the Self-Salvation Corps earlier—that was your doing, wasn't it?
Shh, you'll disturb my friends.
Still, thank you for taking good care of Little Garnet for me, kitten.
And thank you for cleaning up those filthy rocks Duq'arael left behind.
Here. You can have this back.
I have more important things to deal with.
Off to find that hunk of sheetmetal again?
He… wouldn't want to miss this battle.
Thank you for saving me earlier. Farewell.
Where are you off to next?
To meet with him.
You're not going to go help Dame Kal'tsit?
With Amiya and Logos there, nothing will happen.
I'll wait for them beneath the Palace of Westhaleg. It's his last known location, as far as I know.
Her Highness is gone. It's time to put the past to rest.
Your wounds...
Insignificant.
You don't need to involve yourself in the frontline work anymore. Take care of yourself.
Don't you understand how powerful I am by now, runt? You learned quite a bit from us back in the day, didn't you?
Bringing you into the city was the most I could do. Whatever is between you and the Regent—Rose Riverside will no longer be involved.
Good.
Where will you go next?
I'll keep tracking her down. She disappeared so suddenly, abandoning Her Highness at the most critical moment. That demands an explanation.
One of our sharp-eyed sisters managed to track her as she left Londinium. She's still heading west, though her destination is unclear.
And when you catch her? Will you bring her back, or kill her?
She owes all the Riverbank Canoes an explanation.
But you, runt, should worry about yourself first. The Regent is close at hand.
The Riverside Canoes swore not to get involved with anything related to Babel anymore. However, helping an old acquaintance doesn't break the rules.
This smear of blood—it's a gift and a blessing, for less pain and fewer scars. Safe travels, Ascalon.
Did you really have to wipe it on my neck?
Not my fault you always hide your face so well under that hood. You look better this way.
Hmph.
Oh, by the way, I forgot to ask—what do you think of that kitten?
She does not emerge from the shadows, and the wind carries her voice.
'My judgment is unnecessary. When the dust settles, the truth will reveal itself.'
'We're all the same.'

lines.st: Even today, many still argue about when exactly the war ended.
Some say it was when the Nachzehrer King fell at the gates of the Palace of Westhaleg. Others say it was when the Sarkaz fully withdrew from Londinium.
As someone who lived through it, I've been asked quite frequently about my opinion.
Luckily, I share the exact same view as Vina:
The war ended the moment we found the courage to take the sword from Vina's hands and wield it against the Nachzehrer King ourselves.
We fought back. We destroyed, with our own hands, the enemies who tried to ruin us. We never needed a singular hero.
That day, everyone who fought for her home and her future was a hero.

lines.x: 'Everyone, no matter your unit number, which squad you belong to, or which duke you serve—'
'There's no retreating now!'
'Support the allied forces! Why should we lag behind this lot?'
'Quick, go help The Exemplars!'
'Captain, argh—my weapon's broken—'
'Take mine! If it breaks, grab another! If there's none left, snatch a blade from the devils and keep fighting!'
'Advance! Advance!!'
'For Victoria!'
'For Victoria!'
Soldier, you should not continue this assault empty-handed.
Take this sword.
...but, this is the Sighs of Kings... I can't possibly...
It belongs to Your Royal Highness.
No. It belongs to Victoria.
On the wreckage of the Glorious, trusted allies passed the sword from hand to hand until it reached me, completing its mission.
Now, take it from my hand! Let it reach the hands of everyone who needs it. Let its mission continue anew.
This is your right as a Victorian.
But if we take the sword, what will Your Royal Highness use?
I still have my hammer.
I will not stop. I will stay with you until the end.
The early morning sun breaks through the dark clouds. A golden-haired Feline and a Victorian stand together.
The soldier grips the sword tightly, her gaze locked on the determined Aslan, despite the blinding sunlight.
She refuses to look away, for she sees the thousands of Victorians rallied behind her, fighting for the same cause.
She sees the King, walking alongside her people.
As you bid.
All survivors of the 6th Column, 10th Infantry Regiment, advance with me!
For Victoria!
♪May the vast lands they keep owre kynge.
She cuts down two Nachzehrer Royal Court elites and a caster. Her blade bears two new notches.
♪His people, and alle his well-wyllynge,
However, she is too gravely injured to continue, so she passes the sword on.
A worker grips the sword and presses forward. He perishes alongside a Royal Court elite, leaving yet another notch on the blade.
♪And give them grace wythoute endyng!
An Infected picks the sword up from the pool of blood. She does not defeat any enemies, instead protecting it with her body.
In so doing, she protects her Victoria and hope itself.
♪Then may we call and savely syng,
♪Victoria, yeve thankes for owre hom!

lines.st: Finally, the sword pierced the Nachzehrer King, after which it disappeared into the chaos of the battle.
To this day, we still don't know who the final hero to wield the sword was, or whether they're still alive. But one thing is certain—the sword fulfilled its mission.
The Victorians wielded the blade against the invaders.
But its legend didn't end there. They say it was the workers who recovered the broken Sighs of Kings from the blood and mud.
As for the rest of the story, well, every child in Londinium can sing it loud and clear.

lines.loc: Auchterigg, the Nachzehrers' final campOctober 2nd, 1098, 7:35 A.M.

lines.x: I don't see any more Nachzehrers. Is the battle finally over?
But this blasted fog hasn't let up one bit.
Something's not right. The Sarkaz aren't attacking under the cover of the thick fog like before. Is this a trap?
Based on our previous encounters with the Nachzehrer army, they are far more elusive than they seem.
Horn, what do you suppose we should do next?
Coordinate with the main allied forces, hold off on advancing rashly, and minimize unnecessary casualties until we get a clearer read on the situation.
Alright. Sound strategy.
Dagda, send the orders to all of our troops.
I'll pass it on to the alliance commanders, as well as Closure and the others.
Hey, heyyy, hey! Did Hood even get the frequency right?
Huh? We're already live?
Gah! Everybody, listen up! Babel's captured the Military Commission's Command Center.
The bastards don't know their heads from their asses right now, and nobody's got it in them to help out the crusty old Nachzehrer King.
So quit wasting time and get those feet moving!
I don't care if you believe me or not, just go and get this over with!
Oh, and by the way, you might wanna look up.
The old hag owes me big this time.
Delphine—
We traced the transmission. It seems this communication was received on several different frequencies.
Both the allies and the Sarkaz probably heard it.
That voice... it was....
Wiš'adel. Sounds like they've been busy.
Horn, please re-assess the situation.
Vina, look at the sky! It's...
All eyes turn upwards, and soon they hear the sound of steam bursting at full force, echoing above the Palace of Westhaleg.
'Victoria!'
The Victorian army boils.
It's Charles Lynch! He's gone into the fog!
I can hear the commotion inside the clouds. He's battling the Nachzehrers.
Horn?
Although morale is currently high, the alliance has yet to make any major moves. Clearly, the dukes are still hesitating—they have reason to be cautious with intelligence from an unknown source.
If Rhodes Island trusts Wiš'adel, then so do I.
Ha. We trust you as well, Siege. I'll go tell everyone to get ready.
Huh? What's going on?
Did you hear that sound... right beneath our feet!
The Exemplars, we meet at last.
Wait a minute, Vina. Am I seeing things, or did a giant... thing just burrow out from underground?
A giant thing? No. It has a name.
The Crawler. My grandson designed it. Pretty good, huh?
The gray-haired pilot blows a smoke ring, standing proudly on the ladder of the massive machine.
It has four massive steam-powered hydraulic legs, encased in protective armor that bears the unmistakable signs of a worker's handiwork.
Smoke is still billowing from its enormous, black cannon barrel, a clear sign that it was the one responsible for the massive hole in the ground.
A steady stream of Self-Salvation Corps members emerge from the gaping hole beneath The Crawler, carrying combat supplies up to the surface.
You—
Siege, thank goodness we made it here in time!
Starting now, the Self-Salvation Corps will join forces with you. With Granny and her team helping out, we'll have a much greater chance of victory!
We just got that message ourselves—it seems the timing is spot on.
I-Victoria thanks you!
Horn?
All clear. The preparations are complete, and we're ready to move out at any time.
Good.
Everyone, this is the final battle. We—
Wait a minute. There's still one thing left.
I need to adjust the cannon.... there. That should do it.
Cover your ears!
The cannon strikes the giant bell at the center of Auchterigg, an ancient relic that had stood for centuries.
The bell tolls, loud, clear, and beautiful, followed by the sound of the alliance war horns and their furious battle cries.
We've got to let certain people know we're here.
You're in command now.
Victorians! Charge forth to our final victory!

lines.st: 'Victory'.
In the boxing ring, victory is about honor and glory.
On the battlefield, victory is about lives saved...and lost.
The Nachzehrer King's death terrified everyone who saw it. I don't want to think about it now, much less include it in my book.
That Sarkaz won't get a eulogy from me.
But, I'll forever honor the last Steam Knight, Sir Charles Lynch.

lines.x: .....How long has it been since he's moved?
Ten minutes.
No one dares approach Charles Lynch.
Everyone gathers around the last Steam Knight, down on one knee in a show of respect.
He stands tall and unwavering before the Palace of Westhaleg, steam gushing from every crack in his armor.
Scorching, unrelenting.
Like the sound of a beating heart.
How can he still be standing with such grievous wounds from the battle with the Nachzehrer king...
It was never his body alone that kept him upright, or he would have never made it out of the Mausoleum of Kings.
Vina walks towards the Steam Knight, as everyone else watches on.
Vina, don't get close to him. He's off his rocker!
I have to. I have to know for sure.
But he almost killed you back in the underground, Vina!
Let her do it.
Something's not right with the Steam Knight... Sir Charles. Siege probably sensed it too.
But—
Trust her.
Vina approaches the Steam Knight. She can feel the temperature rise from the hot steam.
I've never seen you like this, not even back in the Mausoleum of Kings.
The war is over. You've protected Victoria. You've completed your mission.
(Steam hissing)
What are you still searching for? The sword? Sorry. It was lost somewhere on the battlefield.
(Steam hissing)
Or perhaps you're waiting for something else?
Vina gently touches Sir Charles's armor. Every scratch and mark etched into it tells the Steam Knight's story—
The battle with his comrades on the day he swore to inherit the title.
The battle on the frontier to quell uprisings—he survived amidst a storm of witchcraft.
The betrayal in the Mausoleum of Kings.
The charge towards the Glorious, towards Originium...
Sir Charles Lynch has never fallen.
Sir Charles Lynch shall forever watch over Victoria.
You're looking at the Palace of Westhaleg. I understand now.
You're not the only one. Many more are looking over at the palace.
Vina looks back at the gathered Victorians in the distance—wounded, silent, excited, weeping...
The people had already formed a long human wall on their own, holding back the restive and agitated crowd.
They are all murmuring different names.
'Siege', 'Vina', 'Alexandrina', 'Victoria'.
(Steam hissing)
I never believed you'd gone mad.
Sir Charles, this is our second official meeting.
I once hoped that if you found your way out of the darkness, you would find something worth protecting.
We've already made it this far. Please wait a little longer, until I return.
(Steam hissing)
Vina walks past the Steam Knight, up the steps of the Palace of Westhaleg, and alone through its door.
Time slowly passes.
At first, the crowd is silent, but gradually they stir, as unrest begins to spread.
Why is it taking so long? Could there be other enemies inside?
Should we go and help?
Quiet! Have faith in Her Royal Highness Alexandrina!
Can't you see that even the Steam Knight is waiting here in silence?
I've heard the crown is still inside. Do you think that when she comes out, we'll have a new King?!
Something's not right here. Based on our intelligence, there shouldn't be any more of the Military Commission's forces left.
There should be nothing left in that palace.
Are we just gonna stand here like idiots? We oughta just go in and see for ourselves!
I'll go.
There are better choices—
Well, we're the only ones who can drive the Crawler.
As long as we've got the Crawler, we've nothing to fear. We'll be protected, even against the Steam Knight.

lines.st: Vina never told us what she saw in the Palace of Westhaleg, and we never asked Feist about it either.
It doesn't matter.
I'm just relieved that once the Military Commission's banner fell from atop the Palace, Vina didn't actually walk out wearing the crown.
That's my Vina!
At least, that's how I felt at the time...

lines.x: There she is! She's coming out!
Where's the crown? Why isn't she wearing the crown?
Quiet, you moron! She hasn't been coronated yet. Why would she be wearing the crown already?
What's that in her hands? Is that the devils' flag?
Good people of Victoria. The Nachzehrer King is dead.
I have torn down this flag that the enemy has stabbed into the heart of our great Londinium.
We will tear down every Sarkaz banner left in our home and drive out every enemy still consumed by their bloodlust!
But please, spare the unarmed ones. Spare anyone willing to surrender.
The war is over! We must not avenge slaughter with more slaughter, nor meet hatred with more hatred!
I, Vina Victoria, wish to speak to all the heroes who have fought to defend our Victoria.
Hear me. We have liberated Londinium, liberated Victoria!
In these coming days, months, and years——
We must revive Victoria! We shall revive Victoria!
Vina raises her hammer aloft for all to see.
The people rise to their feet, and a wave of cheers spreads outward from the center, each surge louder than the last.
Londinium and her citizens celebrate her rebirth.
At the center of the crowd, the last Steam Knight lets out his final roar, steam bursting forth.
His heart stops beating, so he ignites it with the suit's burning core.
His vision blurs, so he clears it with steam hot enough to melt flesh.
Then, Sir Charles sees it:
Victoria's flag basking in the sun's golden glow.
Sir Charles Lynch can finally fall.
He falls to the light of dawn, to a new beginning.
Steam completely envelops Sir Charles's enormous body, making his demise unceremonious and almost unnoticed.
Amidst the cheering crowd, a figure walks into the cloud of steam. Allerdale bids farewell to the hero she once so deeply admired.
You could have killed me, back then.
The despicable meeting her end at the hands of her hero... That would have been the perfect ending to her story.
So why must the despicable be the one to bid farewell to the hero?
Sir Charles, you have completed your mission.
My father once taught me that every Steam Knight returning triumphantly from his mission would have a squire to remove his armor and wipe away the dust—
Allow me to remove your armor.
...It has long fused with your body.
'Core... temperature... dropping'
I will keep your core. I promise that your heart will never stop burning. It will bear witness to Victoria's future.
Sir Charles, you've seen the things you hoped to protect restored to their former brilliance.
Have you any final words, good Steam Knight?

lines.st: 'From this day forward, you will be honored as a Steam Knight.'
'Sir Charles Lynch, tell me, what do you want for your reward?'
'Your Majesty, I wish for your blade to always be sharp.'
'I wish for the wheat my wife harvests to fill the granary by our doorstep year after year.'
'I wish to hold my newborn daughter in my arms and fly over Londinium, gazing down at the factories bustling day and night throughout the city.'
'I wish for my neighbor, whose legs were crippled by illness, to stand again, so we can drink, sing, and celebrate through the night with our comrades we know so well.'
'Your Majesty, what I wish for is a life of lasting peace. If this reward requires me to fight for it myself...'
'Then, Your Majesty, I pledge to you, I will always keep fighting, for you, for all my loved ones, for myself, and for...'
'...Victoria.'

lines.x: 'Victoria.'
'The Oath of Sir Charles Lynch, the final words of the last Steam Knight.'
You never abandoned yourself, even when Victoria abandoned you...
Ha... 'Steam Knight', I don't deserve that title, nor this set of armor.
This war has already claimed too many lives.
I hope yours is the last. Goodbye, Sir Charles Lynch.
Listen. They're all bidding you farewell.
'Victoria! Victory!'
'Victoria!' 'For Victoria!!!'
'(last burst of steam)'
'Victoria', his final hoarse whisper merges with the crowd's shouting, like raindrops quietly dropping into the sea.
Allerdale sees the excited faces in the crowd, yet cannot help falling into silence.
Her eyes are drawn to the woman standing on the steps—Vina. She knows Vina can see her as well.
Vina just stands there, looking back at her, her lips moving slightly.
Did she recognize me? Allerdale sighs.
You made the right choice. You are standing exactly where you belong.
As am I.
Londinium does not need any more heroes, Vina.
She turns and walks away, disappearing into the white billows of steam.
What are you looking at, Vina?
The Steam Knight is gone. He has completed his mission.
——? That steam... I thought it was like last time—
This time is different.
Then we...
There is no need to bother him. Someone is already seeing him off.
Alright.
Celebration, and mourning.
Vina, what should we do next?
The people stand cheering in front of the Palace of Westhaleg. They all look to Vina.
They're waiting for you.
Then I will show them all, Delphine.
...is this the Speaker's answer? She sent you, her old comrades, to drive us away?
No. Guarding the Sineselde is only one of the Defense Forces' responsibilities. Justice is justice, Hoff.
Heroes shouldn't be treated this way. Diane and all The Exemplars who bled don't deserve this, do they, Commander?
I understand what you're saying, but this isn't how Parliament makes decisions.
We cannot let this become a precedent.
I do not know politics, Captain. I only know that Diane is a good person. If Parliament insists, many hearts will be lost.
And you, sir? You still will not yield?
I know Diane well. She would never act out of selfishness. Nor does she have any ties with the nobility.
Therefore, I humbly ask the Speaker to take time out of her busy schedule to reconsider Diane's verdict.
This was a decision rendered by the provisional court. Diane broke into the locked throne room in direct defiance of Parliament.
We also know Diane very well, but... I'm afraid there's nothing—
—Diane?!
Yes. She's the one who introduced us to you!
After the war, we lost our homes and nearly our lives. If it weren't for her constant encouragement, I'd probably still be passed out in a barrel of ale.
You don't think she's a bad person either, do you? It's just that Vina—
Speaker Vina, sorry. It's a force of habit. Speaker Vina must have made a mistake.
We've all gone to her office, but I feel like just having numbers on our side might not be enough... Feist, your words carry more weight—please, I'm begging you.
Sorry, but I have to assume that if Parliament put Diane on trial, they must have also allowed her to present a defense.
No matter how strange or unfair I think their decision is, having Speaker Vina force them to change it at the last minute would be even more unfair.
But Diane's case should be treated differently. We just barely survived the war, and now she's going to die over something so stupid?
Listen to the radio. Listen to the patrons in the bars. You'll hear that nobody's happy about this decision.
What do you think, Catherine? You said we could lodge our complaints with Vina anytime.
I think Diane is just a stupid girl who did a stupid thing. Nothing more.
She means well, but what she did was moronic. She defied a direct order of Parliament and left Vina with no other choice.
So, you're planning to turn down the pharma rep?
How do you expect me to convince Parliament?
Actually, Vina does have a chance to solve this. With her reputation, persuading Parliament to impose a lighter punishment is not impossible.
But many eyes are watching as she makes her final decision.
This is just one of the many tricky issues that the Speaker of Londinium's Parliament has to face. A hero may be able to save everyone in a war, but...
—Lady Delphine.
We spent quite a bit of time trying to find you.
Me?
In just one day, Diane's case caused quite an uproar across Londinium. We briefly investigated who was spreading the news and inciting the public.
So... Parliament wants me to shut up.
No, Eliseo. Compared to deliberate acts of sabotage, your actions have had significantly less impact.
Actually, we all admire you for fighting so hard for someone you have not known for very long.
Vina understands how you feel. She wanted to come see you in person, but I stopped her.
I apologize, but Madame Speaker has more urgent matters to attend to presently.
Is there... absolutely no room for negotiation regarding Diane's case?
No. Vina could change things. We all know that.
Frankly, this is a very sad case. However, it will serve as a message from Parliament to all the citizens.
I saw that you requested to review Parliament's records, so you must be well aware of Diane's charges, the defense that she herself presented, and the various PMs' opinions of her.
The citizenry has collectively established Londinium's current laws, and the provisional court made its judgment of Diane in accordance with those laws. Isn't this how you do things in Columbia?
But she's a war hero—shouldn't her merits outweigh her faults?
That's a notion steeped in reason, yet brimming with arrogance.
I often come across this phrase in history books, usually said by old Victorian kings.
But if Vina crosses this line and the others allow it to happen, then what is the point of having Parliament?
How would our Parliament be any different from the one where the king himself served as Speaker?
I don't know the answer to that question, Eliseo, but none of us want to take the risk of finding out.
Parliament will not interfere with any of your endeavors. However, as far as Diane's case is concerned, I'm sorry—this is Parliament's decision, and it cannot be changed.
Diane never told me, specifically, what kind of soldier she was during the war. Most of the time, she talked to me about her hopes for Londinium's future.
Most of the people I meet in Londinium harbor high hopes and dreams for the city's future.
This is the city's great fortune. Our duty is to ensure we never let its people down.
I see.
The herbs Diane grew in the greenhouse against all odds will mature in February. I hear they can alleviate the excruciating pain of Oripathy.
I'll wait until they mature and take some samples back to Columbia.
Okay. If you need any help before then, just let us know.
Actually, when I first arrived in Londinium, I was also curious as to what kind of person Speaker Vina was...

lines.st: When Hannah and I visit schools and sanatoriums to give 'surprises' to the Infected children, they always ask us about 'the story of Princess Alexandrina, the Princess Royal.'
We then tell them the whole tale of the street urchin who became a legendary war hero and the savior of Londinium.
But the story always ends when Vina walks out of the Palace of Westhaleg. At that point, it's not so fit for children.
How I wish the story could end with Vina tearing off those fancy clothes and returning with us to Norport, to the Glasgow Gang.
But I know that's not possible anymore.

On the day of the mass protests against Diane's verdict, Vina explained to us, in detail, the proposals she was going to submit to the dukes at her upcoming meeting. They covered a whole lot of topics like food, healthcare, education, and civil service reforms.
Vina had the right solutions to all of those issues, but she didn't say a word about Diane.
We listened quietly to all of her proposals and then left without bringing it up.

lines.x: Fuel inventory report... Comprehensive report on all city sanatoriums... Report on Oripathy suppressant distribution...
Vina returns her gaze to the documents in front of her. She struggles to absorb the reams of data.
She knows that in just a few minutes, the bell on the table will ring to remind her to head to the banquet hall.
She also knows that those guests don't truly care about the numbers she has tried to memorize, much less the actual lives, deaths, and hardships they represent.
But she cares. This time she's the one who invited the dukes for dinner.
I hope this works.
In the now-quiet office, the faint shouts of protesters outside the window could still be heard.
...I'm sorry, Diane.

lines.loc: Defense Forces Temporary Detention RoomDecember 25th, 1098, 8:32 P.M.

lines.x: Phew—Zone 2 is finally all cleaned up, now it's just Zone 4 left. How are you doing? Tough work, eh? Want to take a break?
I'm cleaning just as fast as you are, as you can plainly see.
You don't need to try and accommodate me just because I'm an Infected.
That's not it at all. I just thought you must be tired, with everything you've been through and all.
Oh, I thought... never mind. As for that incident with the crown, I'm fully clear on that now.
It was just a spur-of-the-moment thing! Besides, you're not the only one who wanted to do that.
Actually, it seems like all of a sudden, people aren't as afraid of the Infected anymore.
That's because we're in Londinium. The policies instituted by the Speaker and Parliament have, at the very least, stopped many people from openly showing fear and resentment towards the Infected.
But according to a Columbian friend of mine, outside of Londinium, things haven't changed.
Anyway, thanks for helping with the cleanup; we would've had the captain on us again without you.
I should be the one thanking you for not shackling me. Having something to do helps put my mind at ease.
If you ask me, you need not worry about Parliament's decision. Think about it. Of all the places in Londinium where they could have detained you, why did they choose the Defense Forces' lockup?
It's because we're all either old war buddies or familiar faces who can look out for you. Don't worry. Once things cool down, we'll find a reason for you to go home.
The protestors outside the Speaker's office, they're the ones who are missing the picture. We all know what kind of person Vina is.
Hmm? Are you feeling alright? Do you need something to drink?
You're saying... the Speaker is going to release me?
I'm saying just rest here for now, where it's safe, until things die down out there.
The captain didn't allow you to have a radio or read the newspaper, right? It's because he was worried that you might get to thinking too much.
If you're still feeling jittery, my good buddy—he gets thrown in the drunk tank every few days—he's stashed some premium hooch around here somewhere.
I'll go grab a few bottles for you.
Diane gives a faint, wry smile as she strains to catch the sounds coming from outside.
But aside from the faint sound of water running through pipes, she hears nothing but the distant drills of the Defense Forces.
Ever since she was brought in here, she has been more or less cut off from the outside world.
I wonder if Eliseo is doing alright?
I really should have let him know beforehand. He must be so worried about me now…
Indeed. He went to nearly everyone he knows to plead on your behalf.
Madam Speaker!
Good. They aren't treating you like some common criminal.
And you made sure of that?
No. They did it on their own—they believe you don't deserve this sentence.
Many came to me on your behalf. Your friends, my friends… our comrades-in-arms, and even some civilians who don't know you at all.
—Diane, I snatched some of my buddy's liquor for you.
He takes a big swig of whiskey.
Urk!
Before the soldier even reaches Diane and Vina, he snaps to attention, the whiskey caught awkwardly in his mouth—too afraid to swallow or spit it out.
He quickly hides the bottle behind him and salutes Vina.
Gulp!
No need to hide it, Patel.
As I recall, Horn did mention that drinking is prohibited in the barracks.
(Swallows) Uhh... ahem…
Yes, Vina! But… this is… uh…
I won't tell Horn, but in return, you'll keep my visiting Diane a secret.
And set the bottle down, will you? Weren't you bringing it to Diane, anyway?
Uh… alright. Vina, you came here to discuss the plans with Diane, right?
Yes.
See, Diane? I told you not to worry, didn't I?
I'll go finish cleaning Zone 4. I've been on duty all day, and I didn't see anyone here.
Would you like a taste of the whiskey Patel left? It doesn't smell all that bad.
Diane shakes her head slightly, a bit downcast.
Mind if I have a sip, then? Meeting with those dukes has left me parched.
Madam Speaker, did you put me here because you really wanted to…?
We thought you'd feel better among old friends. You would rather I threw you in solitary?
No, of course not.
Honestly, I'm not as resolute as you think. My past isn't much different from yours, or from that of most Londiniers.
Diane, I did hesitate over my decision.
So, does your visit mean Parliament has come to a verdict?
(Sigh) I feel it best that I inform you of Parliament's decision in person.
I bid you adieu, Lieutenant Diane Weber of the former 6th Column, 10th Infantry Regiment. You are unquestionably a war hero.
Thank you for your great service to our homeland.
Parliament...? I see. Madam Speaker, when will it happen?
Tomorrow.
Alright.
Have you any regrets?
Have you?
Perhaps my biggest regret is that, after the war, I didn't immediately strip off this outfit and hide myself away back in Norport.
Ha, you would never have done that. I know you. The Siege will never abandon our homeland, and the Speaker of Londinium will never abandon Victoria.
...Hah.
I have no regrets either, Vina—may I call you that?
You may.
We've met many times and fought side by side just as often. But today, I think, is the first day we can truly call ourselves friends.
Vina, before I go, would you share a drink with me?
When you walked alone into the Palace of Westhaleg, everyone believed that the woman who emerged would be wearing the crown.
'A hero must one day be crowned king.'
Every collection of fairytales in that old bookstore on Baker Street had a story like that. It was my favorite place to go before I enlisted.
I really did want to witness history, Vina.
But I let you down. I let a lot of people down.
Yes... You could have been a great ruler.
Just because I wanted to make Londinium a better place? Didn't the old king, the one who was hanged public, also want to make Londinium, to make all of Victoria, a better place?
Things rarely go as planned.
Sometimes, what you believe is right may look wrong to others. I've only recently begun to understand that.
Then what is there to do?
I'm still searching for that answer, Diane.
Someone I trust told me to try to accept those who hate me and to stand firm in my own sense of justice…
I'm still learning… but I never imagined this path would be so lonely.
You're drunk… Vina.
Vina shakes her head. She draws her sword and hands it to Diane.
Do you remember Kyngasycath, the Sighs of Kings?
Yes. I took it from your own hands, after all.
After the battle, I discovered that it had completely dulled. The smiths worked tirelessly, day and night, to reforge it for me.
This new sword is heavier. It carries the weight of even greater expectations than before.
Diane, this sword reminds me every day of the right path—
The future of Victoria doesn't rest with me or Parliament. It rests on all of you—those who reclaimed our home with their own strength.
Vina, I have one last question.
What really happened in the Palace of Westhaleg that day?
What kind of story would you like to hear?
A heroic fairytale that'll make it into the books of that old bookstore, or the truth, so plain that it's almost boring?
Haha.
Diane takes a long swig of her drink, her hand brushing over the cold steel of the sword.
To be honest, I prefer the old sword… When I used it to strike down our enemies, those were the moments I felt closest to Victoria.
Back when I was living a simple, uneventful life, I never imagined I'd have thoughts like these…
And besides, there's hardly any time left to savor those quiet, pre-war days, is there?
Vina, forgive me, but I can't save the last bit of this whiskey for you. I really need it—
(Choking up) Why now, of all times, am I suddenly afraid to die?
(Choking back tears) Vina, before I'm completely drunk, would you tell me one last fairytale?
Her tears fall on the sword, sliding down its blade. 'Drip', 'drip'...
Diane… you already know the stories of the past, and you were there for the best parts of this fairytale.
But you may not know how it ends.
That day, I entered the Palace of Westhaleg, tore down the Sarkaz banner, and was about to leave—when suddenly, I heard someone call my name from the throne room.
'Alexandrina Vina Victoria.'
Then I pushed open the door that had been sealed for so long.
I saw—
my father. Alistair II, speaking to me.
My Alexandrina! You're finally home.
Come sit by my side, daughter of Victoria.
Vina couldn't make out the King's face.
The golden mask blended with his golden hair, radiating authority and an air of mystery.
He was Victoria personified.
My daughter, are you afraid of me? Are you afraid of Victoria?
Vina, never reveal even the slightest hint of fear to your subjects. Your teacher should have taught you that.
You died long ago.
Twenty-six years ago, they hanged you from the gallows.
Laughable! Who could kill Victoria?!
Those greedy condorwings thought they could tear the flesh from my body to feed themselves.
They were so blinded by fear that they conspired in regicide, all so they could feast upon the lands of the Aslans for their own gain.
But no one can kill us—not the dukes, not the Red Dragon. We are the beating heart of Victoria!
So that's why… your statue in the Mausoleum of Kings was never completed…
The dukes never truly laid you to rest; you've been here all along.
What little courage they possessed drove them to confine me in this throne room, but never to claim my throne. Cowardly usurpers!
They fear my people; they fear my Steam Knights; they fear us, Vina.
My daughter, tell me, do my people still remember me? Do my knights still guard our palace?
The people of Victoria have long since adapted to life without a monarch. As for your knights, they're all gone.
They were murdered by the very people they were sworn to protect.
And what of you, Vina?
I… I can barely remember what you look like.
Then come closer. Take off my mask and look closely… you'll see only yourself, Alexandrina. The blood of the royal line flows in us both.
I'm sorry, my child, that I failed to keep my promise.
I intended to hand you a peaceful and powerful Victoria, yet betrayal has left me sitting here, rotting on my throne, for over twenty years.
But at last, you've returned. Take my scepter, wear my crown…
Proclaim to our people that their sovereign has returned.
Purge our land of all dissent and punish every coward who betrayed the crown. Let glory endure—this is Victoria's rightful way!
The king's scepter struck heavily against the throne platform, its dull echo reverberating.
…Blood was never the source of our strength.
How familiar those words are—just like my defiant boasts to my own father.
(Chuckles) You still have much to learn, my daughter. Let me teach you a long-overdue lesson: the meaning of 'royal power'!
Still, I grant you the right to oppose me.
Alexandrina, return to my side, and listen.
No. You're already dead, Your Majesty. All that remains here is your echo, lost in the glories of a bygone empire.
I do not need you to grant me anything, nor do I need you to validate the strength of my convictions.
...I just want you to rest in peace, Father.
Vina tightened her grip on her hammer, stepped onto the platform, and walked toward the Empire's last glimmer of twilight.
Vina... I don't like this story.
The King who found no peace in his eternal rest upon the throne, and the hero who laid down her crown…
The story's over, but has anything really changed?
You and King Alistair both wanted to make Victoria better… but in the end…
Diane takes a hard swig from the bottle.
Cough—cough!
The burning liquor stings, coaxing more tears from her already swollen eyes.
If the King couldn't do it, then what hope do we have?
How can we possibly change anything?
But the story's not over yet, Diane.
I had also believed it would all end when the king found peace... but then someone unexpected appeared.
Should I even ask what brings you here?
A white figure emerges from the shadows behind the throne.

lines.st: 'Faint glimpses of that fairy can be seen in so many of Victoria's legends…'
'Power, intrigue, destiny, dreams coming true—throughout the centuries-old tales, she seems to possess different powers. Yet no one can say for certain which one is truly her.'

lines.x: 'Vina, who did you see?'

lines.st: 'I saw the Unicorn.'

lines.x: Vina, you've far exceeded my expectations. You've shown me new possibilities.
Put down your hammer. I am not your enemy.
We fought side by side once, didn't we?
Who are you, Unicorn?
A tailor's daughter, a leader of the Self-Salvation Corps, a witness to Victoria...
Of course, many old men, even as they faced death, insisted on calling me 'the Nation's Sentinel' in their history books. Wishful thinking, I suppose.
Over the hundreds of years I've remained in Victoria, I've assumed, and later erased, countless identities, but at present, I am Clovisia.
In short, I am your friend, one sincerely pleased by all you've achieved. Does this answer satisfy you, Vina?
Another one of the 'long-lived'? You deceived every ally in the Self-Salvation Corps, as well as all of Rhodes Island.
Deceived? I've never deceived anyone. Everyone has their desires; I merely fulfill them.
Ordinary people tormented by Sarkaz bloodshed wanted to survive, so I led the Self-Salvation Corps to find a way out.
Ancient families, mired in power struggles, feared annihilation in the Empire's whirlpool, so I eliminated every threat to the continuation of the ducal lineage.
Frederick III hoped Victoria wouldn't fall into chaos beneath the Red Dragon's shadow—I could grant that wish, too.
So now you've come to me?
Alexandrina, daughter of Alistair, why would you think I'm only just noticing you now?
Vina, history is like a sailing ship, and you all are the ones who steer its course.
Since you wish to change Victoria, I can help you. What role would you assume to realize your ideals?
You could be a king, a speaker, or even the sole leader of The Exemplars—any of these titles are yours to choose.
'Vina, I must say, yours isn't a new story. I've read far too many like it in old shops...'
'In the end, the hero always accepts the fairy's gift. So, in the end, you chose the role of speaker?'

lines.st: 'No, Diane. I chose nothing.'
'For hundreds of years, the Unicorn has chosen the heroes, and the heroes have merely chosen their boons.'
'But why?'
