'Yes, why did I have to choose her?'

lines.x: Why are you laughing, Vina?
Do you hear that sound outside?
They're cheering for victory, and for Victoria.
Many of them do not have grand ambitions. They don't think of protecting anyone. Some of them would not even share a piece of bread with a starving man.
Yet they are still willing to risk their lives for even the smallest sliver of hope. They are my 'Victoria'.
I like those fools, and I'm willing to look out for them.
And you, Clovisia, have no right to make me choose something I've been doing all along.
You'd best leave, unless you don't mind it getting awkward.
Siege!
You're a fool too, Alexandrina—just like your father.
Send them a message for me. Tell them the tailor's daughter is dead.
I have fulfilled their wishes.
Siege! I got the Crawler in here to back you up!
We've been waiting outside for you forever. We were afraid something happened to you.
It's been taken care of. Thank you, Feist.
Huh?
Come on then. Aren't they still all waiting for us? This battle must end now.
That's the end of the story, Diane.
Diane?
The condemned soldier has finished the last drop of alcohol and is sound asleep.
Diane wanted to drink herself into a stupor. Vina has finished telling her story, but Diane cannot bring herself to face her own ending.
She wanted to drink herself into a dream, a dream in which even death could not catch up to her. She gets her wish.
*sigh*
I'm sorry, Diane. I am not worthy of being a monarch. I'm not even sure I'm qualified to serve as speaker.
Besides, this war has changed many things. Old beliefs have been shattered, and we are striving to rebuild our lives amidst the imminent threat of destruction.
I believe that soon, a great transformation will begin in Londinium and sweep across all of Victoria...
I don't know what tomorrow will bring, but I am certain of one thing: now that we've found the courage to face adversity, why should we ever return to the past?
Victoria no longer needs a monarch, Diane.
Sweet dreams.
On the eve of her march to the gallows, the drunken soldier sleeps the most peaceful sleep of her life.

She dreams of the king's coronation. She dreams of the factory in her hometown, the whistle blowing to signal the end of the workday, as the workers shake off their hats and step out through the factory gates…
Then she soars into the sky and sees Londinium gliding across the mist-covered, verdant earth.
She dreams of Victoria. She is singing her song.

lines.st: May the vast lands they keep owre kynge ♪
His people, and alle his well-wyllynge ♪
And give them grace wythoute endying ♪
Then may we call and savely syng ♪
Victoria, yeve thankes for owre hom! ♪

lines.x: Vina, I can't believe it—the graffiti on the gym walls is still there! Look at this one—Morgan painted it after dreaming of being a famous artist.
Oh, right! Look at this photo—the sign on the movie theater has been nailed back up. And look, there are two dumb-looking fowlbeasts perched on top of it, haha.
Norport is in much better shape than the last time we saw it.
I can't wait to go back and see it with my own eyes.
Uh-huh...
So, where did these photos come from?
The envelope only had the photos, no signature. But plenty of people have joined the engineering team to help rebuild our home. Some of them are old friends from Norport.
Oh, there are two videotapes in here too. They're just some old, worn-out movies, but hey, Delphine, you love watching bad movies, don't you?
Here you go.
Thank you.
Vina... are you okay? You seem a bit distracted.
Are you all going back?
Why the serious look? We're just heading home early to do some housekeeping.
Morgan, you were more excited than anyone when we said we were going home... or am I missing something?
How about you, Dagda?
I'll go lend them a hand. There's not much I can help with here, Vina.
The Knights of the Tower Museum is coming along smoothly. My wish has been fulfilled.
Alright.
Vina, actually, we could still—
The bell tolls.
It's 4:00. Vina...
I know... We can set out now.
Do you want to go see Diane?
No, I have a meeting with Parliament and the dukes.
Oh...
Vina, those protesters outside—
I'll handle them personally. I know their concerns go beyond just Diane—they've been waiting a long time for a king.
Ha, well, that's definitely something the Glasgow Gang can't meddle in.
Morgan...
Alright, alright. We'll head back and tidy up. You know, Norport is better than I expected—but not by much.
We'll be waiting for you, Vina.
Vina, you could ask them to stay.
Of course, I'd love for the Glasgow Gang to stay together, but Auchterigg isn't their home.
Besides, just take a listen—there are plenty of people outside waiting for my response. But before I address them, I intend to bring back more than just empty promises from the dukes.
Vina, Parliament has done more than just make empty promises. They've helped revive our industries, restore order, resettle those affected by the war—
Tell me, Delphine—does everyone now have an equal right to a decent life?
I need to show them that even without a king, we can prove this nation's potential to any doubters.
Let's go. I don't want to be late for the meeting with the dukes.
We should sing something.
Sing what?
Any song will do, as long as it's not that blasted Auchterigg Carol.
Like the song Hannah hums every time she steps out of the ring? 'Hannah's a star, knocking 'em down, one by one, round after round♪'?
Stuff it! I don't do that anymore!
Besides, didn't you all say the song I made up to cheer for Vina was better?
Oh, please. If I remember, you just replaced all the names in Dagda's favorite song with Vina, right?
I wonder if Vina even remembers something that silly.
Of course she does!
I know she mustn't have forgotten. It's just...
Forget it. She said it best herself: looking forward is what really matters.
And there's still a long road ahead, isn't there?

lines.st: That day, we didn't take a detour to watch Diane's end, and we didn't listen to any radio broadcasts.
I heard her Oripathy was so bad that they had to separate her to prevent any incidents… To think that her last shred of dignity came from Oripathy.
I've stopped trying to make sense of these things. As we left Central Londinium and caught sight of the factories of Norport…
I knew we were home.

lines.x: Vina, are you not attending the execution? They are relying on you to calm the many protesters there should things take an unfortunate turn.
My good Earl of March, I'm not that indispensable. Besides, the Defense Forces are already maintaining order on-site, and several MPs are there to oversee the proceedings. They don't need me.
Diane Weber truly was a hero. Shame really. She didn't really do anything wrong.
Alexandrina, you've managed this matter well, but you need to be more heavy-handed.
You're too concerned with dissenting voices, most of which don't matter.
Madam Speaker, why not let us shield you from the storm until Londinium's Parliament fully matures?
Wellington's stance remains ambiguous, the Windermere demesne is simmering with unrest, and the Duke of Ashworth continues to keep a low profile as always.
If the Speaker requires assistance, we wouldn't dare refuse. We must secure Victoria's future!
Very well, since you've offered, I won't hesitate to accept.

Your Graces, you are undoubtedly aware of the dire situation with the Infected within the city. I must insist that you promptly reopen the supply route you have kept closed for over a month under the guise of ensuring safety.
Return the medicine and supplies we've already paid for. Of course, if you're still concerned, the Defense Forces can escort the shipment and clear out those 'bandits' along the way.
Of course, those are minor issues. Now, how about my proposal for forming and training Infected fighting squads?
I understand you're considering forming Infected units in your own territory and want us to test its feasibility. That's fine, but I insist that the safety of the soldiers remain a priority.
I'll make all the data available to you, of course, and to every other duke. After all, this has critical implications for the future strength of Victoria, doesn't it?
This is no simple matter, Madam Speaker. In truth, it has given me quite a headache. I'll be waiting eagerly for some good news from you.
As for Your Grace, Duke of Caster, the Trilby Ashers should soon be delivering an invitation.
After all these years, Londinium will finally reopen diplomatic channels with foreign envoys—this time in the name of Victoria. I trust you all will be in attendance when the time comes.
But you'd best pray that Londinium presents herself in a form you can be proud of.
I'll consider this a provocation, Alexandrina.
From the moment you deliberately blocked Londinium from contacting other nations after the war, you've been provoking Parliament.
You call it 'protection'. Where I'm from, we have another word for it—'kidnapping'.
Unfortunately for you, we always have our ways of reaching those whom we want.
Oh, Vina, is it my turn to negotiate now?
No, Earl of March. We have nothing to discuss, and certainly no need to be friends.
I'm afraid that's not for you to decide.
Hmph. Whatever your intentions may be, Londinium's affairs are none of Columbia's business.
It seems you've found what you need to refuse any deals with me?
All I have is a few photos. Surely, you're aware of what's hidden beneath Londinium, right?
Oh?
Vina, it seems you've managed to find yourself some leverage.
Alexandrina, I admire your candor. But I must remind you, you're the Speaker of Parliament now, not just some... street thug with the Glasgow Gang.
Well, do you know how we used to keep corrupt cops away back in Norport?
Either everyone pretends nothing happened and keeps things as they are, or we expose all their dirty laundry and everyone goes down in flames. It worked every time.
And I'm sure your Graces understand that it has always been Victoria who has kept you safe, not the other way around. I believe your Grace has even said something of a similar nature before.
Alexandrina... I'm starting to doubt our decision of choosing you as Speaker.
You're mistaken. The decision was mine all along, not yours.
I know your schedules are packed, so let's not digress anymore.
I have a long list of things to discuss with you, Parliament seats, medical purchases, military reform, private communication lines...
Supply routes, aid for reconstruction… so let's not waste time. Of course, you're free to continue arguing with me.
Don't worry, this time my sword won't be on your necks.
She's coming! Everyone, get over here!
If we can make the Speaker hear us, we can save that poor soldier's life.
On the street, the eagerly awaiting crowd surrounds Vina, who stands alone.
She knows they are all waiting for her answer.
You've heard us! That innocent hero doesn't deserve this fate! It's not too late to change your mind!
Sorry, I have nothing to say with regard to Diane.
The Parliament has decided. As its Speaker, I stand by its decision.
Everyone, please disperse. Then again, if you absolutely insist on a resolution, you're welcome to stay.
Just now, I reclaimed many things from the visiting dukes—things that rightfully belonged to us and are crucial to our future.
I will tell you about each of these things, one by one. This is the only matter I'll be addressing.
The sounds of protest begin to subside. While most await the outcome of the meeting, a few discontented voices still rise from the crowd.
Is there really nothing that can be done for that poor soul? But why?
This isn't something that needs explaining. Do you want the Speaker to go back on her word?
If we start bending the rules now, the new laws passed by Parliament won't be worth the paper they're printed on.
Catherine, what brings you here?
Some old friends passing by saw the protest and worried things might get out of hand, so they asked me to see if I could help.
As luck would have it, quite a few workers just got off their shifts, heard about it, and came to support you.
Take a look.
As more and more workers gather around, they wave at Vina and spot familiar friends and family members among the protesters.
Conversations begin to ripple through the crowd, and the street suddenly comes alive with activity.
Looks like we're a bit late.
Lieutenant?! Aren't you supposed to be waiting at the Sanatorium for your departure?
Delphine told me about your situation. I wasn't planning to come, but once the others at the Sanatorium found out, they insisted on coming to see who was causing you trouble this time.
I couldn't stop them, so here we are.
A column of soldiers, marching in perfect formation and singing, approaches the congested crowd.
The noise instantly dies down as people instinctively make way for the soldiers, who march up to stand in front of Vina, forming a barrier between her and the protesters.
Vina, we all know you went to negotiate with the dukes today, to fight for us.
We're not about to let you be humiliated in front of the dukes today!
We might not be ready for another battle, but we'll win anyone in numbers!
Thank you, all of you!
Everyone, come quick! I think they're over there!
A group of rowdy children fearlessly push through the crowd, squeezing their way right up to Vina.
It's really you!
You know me?
Of course! We've all heard your story. The great hero of the Exemplars, our very own Speaker!
I've made so many new friends recently—they're all people who walked back to Londinium with you.
They told me you would definitely recognize them. Take a look.
H-Hello, Vina?
Annie? And there's Anabella! Oh, Jones! You're here too!
I guess they were telling the truth then!
But... Why have you come?
We ran into this lady with really tan skin. She said you might run into trouble and told us to come help you!
Uh... did we actually help?
Of course you did!
Thank you, Morgan.
More and more people gather in front of the steps, while those who had been protesting for Diane start to quiet down.
Sigh... whew—
She thinks about everything that has happened over the past few days.
She thinks about the drunken Diane, the departing Glasgow Gang, and the stolen crown.
Bloody hell...
Vina relaxes, sitting down on the steps and gazing at the fiery red sky.
The retired soldiers sit as well, huddling with the other citizens around Vina.
Ha, let's all take a break, okay?
Can I ask you something?
Will we ever have a king again?
I don't know, but not having a king might not be such a bad thing.
Why is that?
You'll understand when you grow up.
Huh?
What do you think—will this be good or bad for Victoria?
It's still too early to tell.
If we have to accept the dawn of a new era, then we all need to change the way we think to keep up.
A Parliament regulated by the people, no more individual authority, no king… that's a first in Victorian history.
Even I have to admit, she may well be biting off more than she can chew.
Catherine glances at the Speaker, who is sitting on the steps without her expected grace, and can't help but wonder.
Vina is surrounded by the children. They all smile and laugh together.
Every so often, she raises her voice to share jokes about the dukes or what she managed to wrestle from the dukes. Things that belonged to the people.
The sunset's warm light bathes her figure, before slowly fading as the time passes.
I'd say the days ahead will only get better.
She breathes out a ring of smoke, watching as the thin wisps drift away into the dying light.
Parliament has passed a resolution: The crown will be placed on the throne in the Palace of Westhaleg, guarded around the clock by the Defense Forces.
Until a suitable candidate for the throne is identified, the Crown will be guarded by Parliament.
Once a candidate is chosen, a nationwide referendum will be conducted under Parliamentary review and supervision to finalize the coronation.
Additionally, Parliament will soon announce decisions regarding the distribution of living supplies and other public welfare matters—
Ha, Parliamentary supervision.
These meetings are truly exhausting. I much prefer settling matters at a grand banquet.
At least there's wine when my lips are parched. *sigh* Sir Davis is certainly taking his sweet time fetching the wine.
Your Grace, Duke of Caster, where are you headed next?
You already know the answer, so why pretend to ask?
Getting the news from you is quicker than waiting for Wellington's spies to report back.
So how about you, Eleanor?
Your Grace, we're hardly close enough to be exchanging warm farewells.
Your words wound me, Eleanor. I suppose I'll have to present a fine bottle of wine on my next visit to old Normandy and ask him to put in a good word about me to you.
Your carriage has arrived, sir. Allow me to get the door for you.
Much obliged, Earl of March.
And remember, we are all Victorians, after all.
Of course.
Ah, it's been so long since I last heard these bells. The sound brings back memories.
Your Grace, I've brought you one of the finest wines from our cellar!
Even those Sarkaz devils couldn't sniff this one out after they took my estate.
Sir Davis, you are a man of rare wit.
Rest assured, there will always be a place for you in the Gododdin territories.
The Duke of Gododdin takes a sip of the wine and turns to make a remark, but then suddenly freezes.
He is confronted with a stunning sight—
...The sun has set...
The crimson sun sinks slowly, as though setting the entire city ablaze.
The scars the Sarkaz had torn into the city stand out even more vividly in the evening glow, as if they are still bleeding.
(Sneezes) The winter sunset truly shows no mercy.
Ah, much like the Speaker's attitude toward us, I suppose.
I could send someone to deliver a few sets of winter clothing for Your Grace.
Take care of yourself—Victoria isn't ready to do without you just yet.
Many thanks.
After all, we can't very well go before Wellington, can we?
Why the sudden melancholy, Your Graces?
I find this sunset rather breathtaking.
Day fades into night, and night into day—how often do we get to witness such a sight?
Londinium's thick fog and heavy clouds rarely part like this.
Eleanor, back in the day, the lads at school would always joke that it was the sun that had to follow behind Londinium.
It would rise as we please, and never set if we willed it.
Now, though, she has to go full speed just to barely keep up with the setting sun.
That thought keeps me awake at night.
Day or night, sunrise or sunset, only Londinium can be Victoria's capital.
Earl of March, remember my words.
Droll. Very droll indeed.
Hah, the sun really does move.
Another bell tolls.
More black-feathered fowlbeasts stir and take to the skies over the city, circling before flying through the steam towards the setting sun.
As the sun dips out of sight, the glow of the twin moons becomes ever clearer.
Night approaches.
How'd things go on your end, Dagda?
Mr. Lane's still alive, but he's got a busted leg. His sworn brother didn't make it, though. I tried asking some old friends...
None of them know when that brother passed away exactly. Might've been when trying to escape Norport, might've been some Sarkaz at the end of their tether during the allied counterattack...
Did you manage to see Mr. Lane himself?
I did. He works at the port now. He didn't bring up the old days, and he avoided talking about his crew.
But he thinks that now, he's living a good life.
Bloody hell.
I strolled around one of the factories we used to look after. It's full of little punks now. None of those youngsters have even heard of us Glasgow Gang.
That pack of brats deserves a right trashing!
Did you swing by the old restaurant we always went to?
Torn down. Parliament sent some men over to get in touch with all the old shopkeeps. Anything so run-down it couldn't be fixed up, and whose owner was missing, was slated to be torn down and rebuilt.
The pub on Sunset Street, Paul's Bar, the magazine stall that runt from Kazimierz ran... They're all gone.
Hannah...
I checked out that public cemetery they were talking about too. I couldn't find McClaren, or—
(Choking back tears) Dammit, why don't these graves have names on them?
Stuff me, can't believe you caught me crying.
Let's head home already. Don't wanna leave Morgan waiting.
I thought... I thought the gym was going to be a rougher sight.
Thank you.
Looks to me like you've been taking care of the place this whole time.
I thought if you were still alive, you'd be coming back sooner or later. Doesn't matter how you look at it, I'm still part of the Glasgow Gang.
You didn't get on Duke Windermere's ship with us back then—
I wasn't so far gone that I needed saving by the dukes.
But... I gotta admit, I was being a right fool back then. After all that, I took some of the crew out to fight some devils hanging around Norport.
I ended up hearing about the Exemplars from them.
You pulled off some crazy stunts I could never dream of. An army, hah... When the battle outside was in total chaos, some of the gang helped out on the front line with me.
But there wasn't much we could do. All I got in the end were a few cuts and bruises—
You're Infected too?
Yep. Heard the same happened to Indra after she took a bad scrape on the battlefield not too long ago.
She's real lucky she had friends like you there with her.
Morgan stares blankly ahead. She'd never considered that problem. Not even Indra herself had.
No, no, that ain't right. Why do I feel all funny hearing you say that? Though I gotta admit I'm not sure what's not right about what you said.
Hah, whatever you say. Anyway, if you ever get the chance, tell Siege I said sorry.
If you stick around you can tell her yourself.
My Glasgow Gang's long gone. Since you're coming back, it's only right that I head out.
Did Delphine not come back with you?
She's standing by Vina's side.
Hah, sounds like you lot had a squabble too. I heard about her mum from a broadcast... Doesn't matter who she might be, at least she's got some nerve.
Where you headed off to next?
Norport needs more hands to rebuild it, and the port's busy shuttling in goods from outside all day every day. Me and my crew are gonna keep an eye on it.
And then... And then I'll head into Central to bash in some merchant bastard's head—Err...
That's only a maybe. Norport's changed, Morgan.
The folks I know are gone, and the shops too. There's some new gang of brats that's popped up. Don't know their arse from their elbow yet they're still trying to fight for dominance...
Anyway, I'm giving the gym back to you lot.
Parliament talked a big game in that broadcast of theirs. I talked it over with some of the boys. We'll wait a spell, then head over to some other dukedom to see what's what.
We'll find a new turf.
A new turf, a new beginning. I'll need some time to forget a few things.
Morgan, was that Cador leaving just now?
Yep.
He came to say goodbye, and decided to hand this back to us while he was at it.
She pulls out a butterfly knife from her pocket. It's badly scratched, but the emblem of the Glasgow Gang can still be clearly seen.
Baird's butterfly knife. Hannah would recognize it anywhere. It was her who insisted on engraving that emblem once she beat Baird, after all.
Cador found it on the body of a cleaner... He couldn't find Baird herself, though.
I went there too.
All I found was some stuff on the wall I didn't get. It's almost all gone, what with all the rain. But I'm sure...
I'm sure she was there. I saw her handwriting.
'I hope so too'...

lines.st: 'I hope so too.'
What did Baird hope for? We thought about it long and hard, but we were never able to make sense of it.
Her wishes had already been washed back into Norport's soil by the rain.
We decided not to dwell on it much longer.
We never could guess her last little secret.
She's definitely having a good laugh at us right now.

lines.x: I remember that whenever we went to the video shop, Baird hated weepy scenes about grieving the most.
Yeah. She never thought death was worth being all that sad for.
Pass the knife to me. I'll think of a way to hang it up on the wall.
Then I'll go get some drinks. To celebrate us coming home, and a toast to Baird!
Should we wait for Vina?
She ain't got the time to come.
But just in case—
Then we'll hold off right before we get completely pissed, Hannah.
Booze waits for no man.

lines.st: That day, without having to say a word on the matter, we all drank our drinks real slow.
We were waiting for one more person.
It seems there was a lot of chit-chat going on that day. Not that we remember any of it after the hangover.
All I remember is that Hannah and I got in a fight, and Dagda's brow got itself another scar. Why? None of us knew.
If Vina were there, it'll all be fine. She always stayed the most sober out of all of us, and she'd remember for us.
But she never showed up that day.

lines.x: I dragged the body back, Boss.
But... Will this really be okay? I mean, snatching away the corpse of a condemned criminal from right under Parliament's nose—
Did you really think you could take that away without some silent approval from above?
What of the Columbian?
He's not in the best of moods... Boss, do we really need him to carry these goods? What if—
Millscar says nothing. The mercenary sighs.
Alright, I get it. Look, Boss, I'm not trying to make you mad. But ever since Parliament made a move on us, everyone's been on edge.
It doesn't concern you lot.
I'll handle the Columbian.
Eliseo. I presume I don't need to introduce myself.
Millscar. I've heard about you.
Good, no need to waste words then. I need you to see this batch of goods out of Londinium safely.
You will, of course, receive payment. I can send this 'corpse' along with you.
Leave the city, and go as far away from it as you can.
Her name is Diane, Millscar.
Who she is isn't important to me.
What's important is that she doesn't show her face again in Londinium.
You call this payment? Making someone who just got home leave again?
Eliseo, you are well aware she cannot stay in the city. As far as everyone is concerned, she is dead. Hanged from the gallows.
Vina made a mistake. So long as this person is alive and within the city, sooner or later someone is going to use her as a knife to stab Vina in the back.
*sigh* She really must learn to be more decisive.
You know the condition of her infection better than I do, Eliseo. She doesn't have long.
You're welcome to refuse, of course. But I will still help Vina settle this matter. In my own way.
I know your story, Millscar. Or rather, Lady Allerdale Cumberland.
There's a lot of versions of it out there, but they all agree you really did fit with the title of 'Cumberland the Ever on High', once upon a time.
Fine, I'll get Diane out of here. I can take better care of her back in Columbia.
Happy?
You're a good man, Eliseo. She's lucky to have made a friend in you.
Get ready. You could be leaving at any time.
You either die a hero, or you live long enough to see yourself become the villain.
Did you ever think you'd turn out this way?
Why ask just me?
Countless heroes lived through the war. From the most radiant of them to those that none remember. I too am curious as to how they will change in the face of reality.
Lying down amidst the meadow, the soldier senses a slight itch. The cold wind blows at the stalks of grass, and their tips tickle her face.
Is this what the land of the dead is like? She opens her eyes and sees someone most unexpected.
Huh—Wha—?!
You're awake.
You—
It's me, Eliseo. You shouldn't just go off on your own to do dangerous stuff without a word, Diane.
I-I remember standing atop the gallows...
You're dead, at least to those who witnessed the execution.
Before we left, I asked the people at Rhodes Island to give you the once-over. It's not looking too good.
A lot of your major organs have already begun to crystallize. In other words, Originium is replacing those organs.
So... I'm not dead? Am I lucky or unlucky?
I'm not sure if the Speaker knew about this beforehand...
So it was Vina who let me go? That's rather foolish of her.
To tell you the truth, I really don't get some of you people. *sigh* Sorry, Diane. I'm afraid you can't go back to Londinium.
Only now does she realize they are no longer in Londinium. She sees the tell-tale giant tracks laid down by the nomadic city not too far off in the distance.
Just ahead of them, Sarkaz children run around in the meadow, waving at her as they pass.
Most displaced Sarkaz have lost the light in their eyes to confusion. They can go anywhere, but nowhere can they call home.
I'll be taking you to Columbia, Diane. I might be able to do more for you there.
Alright then. Columbia, eh...?
I still remember saying that if I get the chance, I'd love to see the place you keep talking about.
How far are we from Columbia, Eliseo?
Well, still very far away. Let me get out my map—
Will I be able to make it there in my condition? Walking the whole way?
...I see. Could you promise me one thing, Eliseo?
I know what you're going to ask, Diane.
When the day comes, I'll bring you home. I'll bring you back to Londinium.
I'm rather curious myself as to how much the city would have changed by the time we return.

lines.st: The day we went back to the gym and got drunk, I had a dream.
In that dream, there was a tomb, all dressed up to the nines. Our names were carved onto the plaques inside it—
'Morgan', Kate Morrigan... 'Indra', Hannah Jackson
'Dagda', Isabelle Montague... 'Baird', Brigit Lucan
Outside the tomb, there was a blazing carriage, and atop it sat a golden lion. He was calling my name.
Vina Victoria.

lines.x: Mmfh... Vina...
...A blanket? Who put this blanket on me?
Urgh, my head hurts real bad. What time is it—
Morgan, didn't I tell you not to drink like a sodding wino all the time?
Hah, considering how wild Hannah and the rest can get, shouldn't you be looking after them?
Eh...? Vina? Vina!
Morgan nimbly launches herself out of the sofa, leaping over the short table to throw herself at Vina.
Vina, you came over!
I thought... I thought...
You thought what?
Why didn't you wake me, Hannah?!
Weren't you yapping and yowling yesterday about how she definitely wasn't coming?
Look at you. You turned out the most excited out of all of us.
No I'm not!
Stubborn prick.
Quiet down, Morgan. Dagda's still asleep.
(Mmph...)
The one resting her head atop Indra's lap twitches her ears, still lost within a lovely drunken dream.
Seems like Dagda drank the most out of you lot. I tidied up a lot of empty bottles.
She must've been in a real rotten mood, Vina.
Sorry.
I just hope I'm not too late.
Well of course you're not!
Wasn't what you said yesterday, Morgan.
(Shut it, Hannah!)
Welcome home, Vina!
I was really hoping to pop in earlier, but there's simply far too much to handle over at Parliament.
Then... How'd you...
I'm afraid I've given Horn another headache to deal with.
I suspect she'll only just have discovered I slipped away—
Then does that mean you need to head back?
I can't run away from my responsibilities.
But I owe you all an apology.
Vina, you don't need to—
Eh? Siege? Vina!
How long've I been asleep?!
Relax, Vina's only just arrived back.
That knife hanging on the wall—
It's hers. Cador brought it back.
Cador?!

lines.st: Vina never brought up having to head back the whole day, but we all knew—
That the Siege Vina, the boss of the Glasgow Gang, the ever-victorious king of the 'Slobberknocker', and (one of) my best friend(s) could leave us at any moment.
So I was loathe to lose a single moment where I could tell Vina about all the changes in Norport that she hadn't had the chance to see yet...
Cador, Mr. Lane, the video shop...
The reworked port, the rebuilt stores, a handful of old friends who rushed home after hearing the news...

Of course, who could forget the inexperienced little buggers who wanted to 'take over our gym'. After Vina beat them all to kingdom come with a smile on her face, they apologized by way of a few cartons of beer, and said they wanted to join us—
Seems like Norport's forgotten about us, but I don't think that's such a bad thing.
The war really was over.

lines.x: Do you still remember this place?
Why don't we get back to our little games, see who's fastest this time around?
Before Vina has even finished speaking, Indra has already begun to set off.
Hannah, you lousy cheat!
She didn't say nothing about a countdown. I'm still the champ here!
Hmph.
You've gotten slower ever since your injury, Hannah.
Say that after you've caught up.
Eh? Wha—?
What's going on? Wait for me!
Don't fall behind, Delphine! Follow me!
Hear that, Del? You're a real slowpoke!
Several figures speed through the streets, and a few workers heading home after work give an inquisitive glance at the brash crew.
They can't quite seem to remember who this gang is—
'Must be another bunch of outsiders just arrived at Norport. Maybe.'
High above the gaps between the factories, the Glasgow Gang return to a favorite spot of theirs.
Here, they can look down upon the squatting factories, and look out at the Shard's pointed peak over at Central.
It was here that they started, and it was here that they left home. After a long period of wandering about, they have finally returned to this familiar place.
But—
I left a glass for Baird.
Sure you don't want a tipple, Dagda?
No thank you. I'm not going to embarrass myself in front of Vina again.
I'll give your share to her, then.
Just fruit juice for you, Del?
Don't call me that. I'm still not too used to alcohol.
Suit yourself. I'll keep watch over you all.
Hell yeah! Finally, the part where we can start bloody drinking!
To Baird! To the Glasgow Gang!
To Baird! To the Glasgow Gang!
To Rhodes Island! To the Exemplars!
To everyone who fought in the war!
And, and, to all those who risked their lives coming home!
To all those who live, and who left... To all those still by our side...
To all those who shed blood and tears, to all those who struggle in darkness and in pain...
...To Victoria.
To Victoria!
There is no need for sorrow. The living engrave the moment in their memories, while those who left leave behind their hopes and dreams.
The fowlbeasts croon as they spiral overhead, echoing their words.
You heading off too, Delphine?
Only for a short while. I need to go back to Lynncardine. Shearer will come along to recuperate.
If you ever get real fed up of those lousy bastards, you can always come back here.
This is your home too.
Of course I will! I promised Vina, after all.
If there's ever a day you feel like it's all too much for you, Vina—
I'll come back.
But I think I can handle it.
I hope the Londiniers can live in peace and happiness, and I hope that everyone can live their best lives. It's what they deserve.
Everyone should be like us. Smiling, without a tear in sight or an empty belly to be heard.
I'm not going to let everything we've accomplished go to waste.
If someone tries to take it from us, I'll be fighting back.
Just like how we looked after this place back then.
Vina...

To the Glasgow Gang. To all of you.

lines.st: Vina left us on the morning of the day after. She didn't let us see her off. Maybe she didn't want to wake us.
In truth, we were all awake. I watched her change into those clothes I was never fond of, and walk off on her lonesome towards Central Londinium.
I hope Vina managed to get a real good night's sleep, the best she's had in a long while. After all, it's only when she's at home that she doesn't need to think about anything.
I spent the entire night writing down everything that happened the day Vina came home, no matter how big or small.
I believe there will come a day that you, Vina, will read these words I wrote down.
They might not be of too much use, but they can help you remember some of the more easily-forgotten things.
Vina, do you still remember that dream I wrote about earlier?
In that dream, there was a tomb, all dressed up to the nines. Our names were carved onto the plaques inside it—
Outside the tomb, there was a blazing carriage, and atop it sat a golden lion. He was calling my name.
Vina Victoria.
I dreamt I was you.
I dreamt of a time I was all old and withered, some afternoon decades after I lost touch with my Glasgow friends...
I walked into the tomb that 'Siege' built for the gang.
I cherish that unforgettable memory that is no longer mine. I cherish the tale that started in Norport.
Vina, from the day you put on those clothes, I knew...
In the end, you walk alone. You have no choice but to walk alone.
I'm sorry. There's no way for us to help you, or to stay by your side...
But I wanted to let you know, we will always be here.
The Glasgow Gang will always be here.
To you, Vina Victoria.
—'The Memoir of Alexandrina'
By Kate Morrigan
